raw_text: ABBOT, SAMUEL H. F.—Was queen's counsel at Tobago from 1850 to 1857; appointed attorney-general of that colony, 1857. Is a member of the privy council, and of the executive committee.

ACKROYD, F. J.—Was clerk in the registry of the supreme court at Mauritius, Sept. 1853; clerk to first puisne judge, April, 1859; chief clerk in the Registry of Supreme Court, January, 1863; appointed March 1, 1864, chief clerk to the master of the supreme court.

raw_text: ADAMS, A. Y.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; took science honours in his undergraduate course; was appointed to the commissioner of roads' department, Ceylon, 1853; acting assistant government agent and police magistrate, Matelle, 1854; stipendiary justice of the peace, 1854; joint police magistrate and commissioner of requests, Kornegalle, 1856; temple land commissioner, 1857; writer, 1858; acting district judge, and police magistrate, Badulla, and assistant government agent, Trincomalie, 1860; acting assistant government agent, Badulla, 1861; resumed duties as judge in August; acting temple land commissioner, Dec. 1861; district commissioner of requests, and police magistrate of Badulla, 1862; acting district judge, Negombo, June, 1863; appointment confirmed Sept. 1863.
ADAMSON, J.—Chief clerk colonial office, Cape of Good Hope, has been 17 years in the colonial service.

ADDERLEY, RT. HON. CHARLES BOWYER, M.P. for North Stafford; Under-Secretary of State for the colonies in Lord Derby's third administration, July, 1866. Educated at Christ Church, Oxford, where he graduated B.A., 1838. Was President of the Board of Health and Vice-President of the Board of Education from March, 1858, till June, 1859; M.P. for Staffordshire North, for which he was first returned 1841. ADELAIDE (AUSTRALIA), 1st BISHOP of.—Founded 1847.—RIGHT REV. AUGUSTUS SHORT, D.D.—Educated at Westminster and at Christ-church, Oxford, where he took 1st class honours in classics in 1824; and graduated M.A. in 1826; appointed vicar of Ravensthorpe, 1835; rural dean, 1844; Bampton Lecturer at Oxford, 1846; and 1st bishop of Adelaide, 1847. The diocese includes the whole colony of South Australia.

ADOLPHUS, EDWIN.—Clerk to colonial secretary, Honduras, 1856 to 1859; appointed provost-marshal, 1859; and acted as private secretary to the administrator of the government, 1859 and 1861; and was in October, 1864, appointed Magistrate of the Northern District Honduras.

Is also registrar of the vice-admiralty court; entered colonial service, Hong Kong, in 1845, as clerk of the supreme court; appointed deputy registrar, 1850; acting chief magistrate, 1860; acting colonial secretary, 1861, to Feb. 1864, and again from 15th March, 1865, to 11th March, 1866.

raw_text: ALLISON, A. B.—Chief clerk and draughtsman in the surveyor-general's department, Natal, 1859; was appointed to the civil service of that colony in 1852; appointed captain of the mounted police in that colony, 1862.

ALLWOOD, WILLIAM.—Second class clerk in the colonial secretary's office, Jamaica; appointed 8th September, 1866; was a clerk in the general post office, Jamaica, from 1st February, 1864, to 30th April, 1865; second clerk in the Government secretary's office, from 1st May, 1865, to 7th September, 1866.

ALLWOOD, JAMES, jun.—First class clerk in the finance office, Jamaica; appointed as second clerk in the governor's secretary's office in July, 1862; was acting second clerk in the executive committee office from February, 1865, to May, 1866; and clerk in the irrigation department from May to October, 1866; on the change of constitution was transferred to his present appointment in the finance office.

ALSBURY, GEORGE.—Was appointed clerk in the Custom House, London, January 19, 1833; was promoted as collector of customs at the port of Anguilla, St. Kitt's, West Indies, March 16, 1836; was appointed justice of the peace and treasurer of the island in 1836; on the repeal of the imperial duties in 1850 he was selected and appointed by the lords of her majesty's treasury as controller of customs and navigation laws, and superintendent of the islands St. Kitt's, Nevis, and Anguilla, which post he held till 5th January, 1855, when that office was abolished, and he obtained a pension. Was employed in the passport department July of the Foreign Office from July 21, 1855, till 21, 1858, when he was appointed first clerk in that department. Presiding Magistrate Anguilla, Dec. 1863.

raw_text: ANDREWS, ROBERT.—Second writer in secretary's office, and collector of rates at the Gambia; appointed 1841; retired on a pension.

ANDREWS, WALTER BOYD TATE.—Appointed clerk to colonial secretary's office, Western Australia, 1840; chief clerk survey office, 1847; clerk to colonial secretary's office, South Australia, 1848; deputy registrar of births, deaths, and marriages, 1850; deputy registrar-general of the province of South Australia, 1856; acting registrar-general of deeds, 1862. Registrar general, 1865.

ANNESLEY, HON. SYDNEY.—Fourth son of Lord Valentia; entered the 16th lancers in 1848, and served with them four years; accompanied the Oxfordshire Militia, during the Crimean war, to Corfu, and remained with them until 1858, when he was appointed A.D.C. to Sir John Young, the lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands, and served in that capacity until Sir John returned to England; appointed, 1860; was aide-de-camp for one year to Sir Hercules Robinson, the governor of Hong Kong.

ANSON, ARCH. E. HARBORD.—Entered the royal artillery, June, 1844; promoted to be captain July, 1852; served at the siege of Sebastopol in 1855 in the trenches with the siege train, and at the bombardments of the 6th and 17th June; appointed inspector-general of police at Mauritius, Sept. 1858.

events.text_span: Superintendent, Government Orphan Asylum, Mauritius, 1st September, 1859.

raw_text: ANSORGE, REV. P.—Superintendent, Government Orphan Asylum, Mauritius, 1st September, 1859.

raw_text: ANTHING, LOUIS.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Namaqualand division of Cape Colony; appointed April, 1859; was clerk in the office of the master of the supreme court, 1850 to 1865; clerk to the civil commissioner, Malmesbury, Dec. 1856 to 1859.

ANTIGUA, 3rd BISHOP OF. Founded 1842.—RIGHT REV. WILLIAM WALROND JACKSON, D.D.—Educated at Codrington College, Barbados, where he acquired great distinction, was chaplain to the forces and to the former bishop of Antigua; consecrated 1860. The diocese includes Antigua, Montserrat, Nevis, St. Kitt's, Anguilla, Tortola, and Dominica.

ATTHILL, J. G. PORTER.—Was attorney-general of St. Lucia, 1844 to 1849; puisne judge, 1849 to 1859; and appointed chief justice of the colony, 1859. Is a member of the Irish bar.

A.—Advocate of the royal court of St. Lucia, and proctor of the court of vice-admiralty of the island; admitted September 15th, 1842; stipendiary magistrate, July 22nd, 1850; acting registrar of the courts in May, 1857; an official member of the legislative council while the colonial secretary shall be administering the government, January, 1862.

raw_text: AURET, W. H.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Paarl, Cape of Good Hope; appointed July, 1864; is also road magistrate; was appointed a clerk in the treasury at the Cape, Jan. 1849; clerk to the commissioner at Albany, 1849; clerk to civil commissioner, and magistrate, Namaqualand, August, 1855; civil commissioner and magistrate of Victoria, West, to date of present appointment.

AUSTIN, H. W.—Receiver general of Jamaica, 1865; has been in the colonial service since 1851, when he held the appointments of acting assistant government secretary, stipendiary justice and private secretary to the governor of British Guiana; appointed governor's secretary, 1853, and colonial secretary of Jamaica, 1864; acted as such till he was appointed receiver-general by letters patent; is also a member of the privy council of that island.

AUSTIN, JOHN G.—Was acting stipendiary magistrate in British Guiana from Nov. 1849 to May, 1850; assistant government secretary, May, 1850, to June, 1851; acting government secretary, June, 1851, to Dec. 1851; assistant government secretary, Dec. 1851, to Feb. 1853; and immigration agent-general, from Feb. 1853; appointed January 1864, lieutenant-governor of Honduras.

district magistrate Pamplemousses, March, 1858

raw_text: AUTARD, DE BRAGARD, G. A.—Acting district magistrate, Mauritius, June, 1853; stipendiary and district magistrate Savanne, Mauritius, 1854; district magistrate Pamplemousses, March, 1858; and senior magistrate of that district May, 1860.

AYLIFF, JOHN.—Was appointed in 1846 by Sir P. Maitland, when Governor of the Colony of the Cape of Good Hope, to raise a body of natives for service in the field during the Kaffir War; acted as field adjutant to the division of Major Sutton; appointed commandant of native levies in the field, 1847; appointed interpreter to the lord high commissioner of British Kaffraria, 1849; acted as secretary to the High Commissioner under General Cathcart during the campaign across the Kei, and that against Moshesh, and in the same capacity under Sir G. Grey to Natal in 1855; appointed auditor of Kaffraria, January, 1856, and treasurer of Natal, 1862.

events.text_span: Revenue officer, river Demerara, appointed 1861

raw_text: BACKER, JOHN HOWARD, — Revenue officer, river Demerara, appointed 1861; was previously in colonial customs and a sub-registrar.

events.position: appointed chief melter in the assay department
events.text_span: appointed chief melter in the assay department, British Columbia, Sept. 1859.

raw_text: BACON, CHARLES A.—Educated at Eton; formerly in the army; appointed chief melter in the assay department, British Columbia, Sept. 1859.
BAGOT, C.—Comptroller of customs and rum duties at port of George Town, British Guiana; appointed 1849. Has been in the customs' department since 1836.

raw_text: BAILEY, ARTHUR.—Appointed surveyor-general of the Falkland Islands, 1848; is also registrar of the colony; was on the ordnance survey of England under colonel Colby, R.E., from 1835 to 1841; appointed assistant surveyor to the expedition sent to New Zealand under Mr. Ligar in 1841, and served in that capacity till 1848.

BAILEY, J. B. A.—Appointed a writer to the Ceylon government, 1863; acting commissioner of requests, and police magistrate, Pantura, 1864; commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Calpentyn, 1865, and acting assistant government agent at Mulletivoe; appointed government agent, Timcomalie, January, 1866; acting assistant colonial secretary, September, 1866.

BAILEY, Rev. J. B. H.—Appointed principal assistant in the Colombo Academy, Ceylon, July, 1838; head master of the Colombo Academy, 1839; acting principal of that academy, 1840; inspector of schools in Ceylon, 1845; acted in 1849 and 1850 as principal of the central school and native normal institution; in conjunction with his office of inspector of schools, Mr. Bailey was appointed officiating clergyman to the troops at Colombo, from July 1850 to Oct. 1851, and from Aug. 1857 to Dec. 1858; acting secretary to the central school commission, from 22nd Oct. 1855 to 31st Dec. 1859; and acting colonial chaplain St. Peter's Church, Colombo, June, 1860; confirmed in that appointment, 1862; is also secretary to the central school commission.

BAILEY, J.—Was appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, 1846; acting deputy fiscal for the western province, 1853; confirmed in that appointment 1855; was acting assistant government agent at Colombo from 1856 to 1858: when he resumed his duties as deputy fiscal. Retired on pension, 1865.

BAILEY, J.—Was educated at Trinity College, Oxford, where he graduated, B.A., in 1847; appointed a writer in Ceylon, 1848, and was employed in the western, northern, and north-western provinces; acting commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Putlam, and Calpentyn, north-western province, 1850; acting assistant government agent, commissioner of requests, and police magistrate, Nuwera Ellia, central province, 1852; assistant government agent at Kandy, central province, 1853; acting assistant government agent, commissioner of requests, and police magistrate at Matelle, central province, 1853; acting assistant government agent, district judge, commissioner of requests, and police magistrate, Badulla, central province, 1854; assistant government agent, Badulla, 1855; acting principal assistant colonial secretary and clerk to the executive and legislative councils, 1858, in which appointment he was confirmed, 1860. Retired on a pension, 1865.

BAIRD, ANDREW FRAZER.—Appointed to H.M. Customs, British Guiana, in February, 1836, and served in that department in Demerara and Berbice, until March, 1841; appointed postholder at Ampa, in the River Essequibo, 21st April, 1841; made a justice of the peace on the 11th May following; appointed superintendent of rivers and creeks, and commissary of taxation for the river Essequibo and its tributaries, 18th August, 1843; was acting assistant government secretary in Demerara, and circuit stipendiary magistrate, from 6th June, 1845 until October, 1847; appointed a special justice of the peace, 12th October, 1847; and resumed his duties as superintendent of and police magistrate for the Essequibo river district; appointed stipendiary magistrate for the West Coast district of Berbice, 24th June, 1857; commissioner of affidavits, 6th August, 1864; and acting sheriff of Berbice, 22nd August. 1865.

events.position: Appointed acting district magistrate in Mauritius
events.text_span: Appointed acting district magistrate in Mauritius, July, 1857
junior district magistrate, Pamplemousses, May, 1860.

raw_text: BAKER, W. E.—Appointed acting district magistrate in Mauritius, July, 1857; junior district magistrate, Pamplemousses, May, 1860.

BALL, HENRY JOHN, B.A., Oxon, and B.A., Univ. Lond.—Special pleader from 1846 to 1853; called to the bar in 1853, home circuit, and appointed attorney-general of British Honduras in 1855; appointed judge of the summary jurisdiction court, Hong Kong, 1862; acted as chief justice from May, 1863, until October, 1864, and again from May, 1865, until November, 1866. During the time of his acting as chief justice sat as a member of the legislative council; was appointed chairman of the sanitary commission in 1863; and one of the two commissioners for preparing a new edition of the laws of Hong Kong in 1864; appointed an unofficial member of the legislative council in 1866.

raw_text: BALL, H. M.—Formerly in the army; stipendiary magistrate at Lytton in the colony of British Columbia; appointed 1859.

raw_text: BALSTON, F. E.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Frasersburg division of the Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1860; was clerk to the magistrate and postmaster at East London, Nov. 1848; clerk to the civil commissioner of Colesberg, 1856 to 1860.

BANCROFT, CAPTAIN W. C., 16th Foot.—Entered the army as ensign in the 3rd West Indian regiment in June 1844; promoted to lieutenant in April 1846, and exchanged to 76th reg. in Jan. 1841; on reduction of 2nd battalion of this corps in 1850, he was transferred to 16th foot, and got his company in Feb. 1858. Was appointed fort-adjutant in the island of Grenada in April, 1847, and removed to Barbados in July of the same year to become aide-de-camp to Lieut.-Gen. S. H. Berkeley. On that general's departure for England, in July 1850, he joined the staff of Major-Gen. Bunbury, K.H.; and in Aug. of the same year was appointed assistant military secretary at Jamaica, which office he held until Oct, 1853. In Feb. 1854, he was made military secretary and aide-de-camp to Sir Henry Barkly. K.C.B., the captain-general and governor of Jamaica, and in 1856 proceeded with his excellence in the same capacity to Victoria.

events.text_span: Civil chaplain, Mahebourg, Mauritius, January, 1863.

raw_text: BANKS, REV. W. T.—Civil chaplain, Mahebourg, Mauritius, January, 1863.

BARBADOS, 2ND BISHOP OF. Founded 1824.— RIGHT REV. THOMAS PARRY, D.D.—Educated at Oriel College, Oxford, where he graduated 1st class in mathematics and 2nd class in classics, 1816; became a fellow of Balliol, and graduated M.A. 1819; appointed archdeacon of Antigua, 1825; transferred to Barbados as archdeacon, 1840; is visitor of Codrington College, Barbados. The diocese formerly included the three archdeaconries of Barbados, Antigua, and British Guiana; but in 1842 they were divided into three bishoprics, and the present diocese of this see is confined to Barbados, Trinidad, Grenada, St. Vincent, Tobago, and St. Lucia.

raw_text: BARCLAY, SIR DAVID, 10th BART.—Member of the legislative council of Mauritius (by warrant, 1846); formerly captain 99th foot, and aide-de-camp to the governor of that island.
BARCLAY, A.—Receiver-general of Jamaica; appointed provisionally, 1848; by letters patent, 1852. Is a member of the privy council of that island.

BARCLAY, COLVILLE A. D.—Volunteer treasury, Mauritius, March 26, 1846; extra clerk, Treasury, Feb. 21, 1848; transferred to savings' bank, May 15, 1848; manager of ditto, April, 1, 1851; acting officer of civil status, Feb. 1, 1860; officer in charge of Treasury, Aug. 28, 1860; resumed duties as manager of savings' bank, May 1, 1861. Acting Treasurer, March, 1863; resumed duties as manager of S. B., May, 1865.

BARKLY, K.C.B. (Civil. Creat. 1853.)—SIR HENRY BARKLY.—Appointed governor and commander-in-chief of British Guiana, Dec. 1848; capt.-general and governor-in-chief of Jamaica, Aug. 1853; capt.-general and governor-in-chief of Victoria, 1856. Received the order of the Bath after services in British Guiana; was M.P. for Leominster from April, 1845 to Feb. 1849; governor of Mauritius, Aug. 1863.

BARLEE, F. P., F.R.G.S.—Served in the ordnance department from 1844 to 1855; on retiring from that department in 1855, was appointed colonial secretary of Western Australia, and is a member of the executive and legislative councils of that colony.

raw_text: BARROW, SIR G., 2nd BART., created 1835.—Appointed clerk in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, July, 1825; promoted senior clerk, July, 1843.

BARRY, MICHAEL R.—Educated in France and at the Queen's University and Ireland, where he graduated D.L., 1860; entered Queen's College, Cork, in 1854, when he took scholarship in classics; became a member of the Hon. Society of Lincoln's Inn, 1855, and was called to the Bar, 1859. Practised at the Chancery bar in England from that date until 1864, when he was appointed Queen's Advocate at the Gold Coast.

BARTON, W.—Was formerly in the army; was gazetted an ensign in the 87th, or Prince of Wales' Royal Irish regiment, May 9th, 1811; ensign in the first Foot Guards, 4th Feb., 1813; lieut. and captain 1st or Grenadier regiment of Foot Guards 3rd July, 1815; served during the campaign of 1814 in Holland; was present at the bombardment of Antwerp and storming of Bergen op Boom; commanded the light company, and got on to the ramparts with the forlorn hope, under Captain (now General) Shaw, of the Coldstream Guards. Also served during the campaign of 1815, and was wounded at Quatre Bras, and served in the expedition sent to Portugal under General Sir Wm. Clinton in Dec. 1826. Was appointed to the commissioner of roads department, Ceylon, 1843; commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Matura, 1845; acting assistant government agent, Matura, 1847; commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Negombo, 1847; acting assistant government agent, commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Kaigalle, 1854; acting in the same offices, and also district judge, Ratnapoora, 1854; assistant government agent, Matura, 1856; and postmaster-general, 1859.

BASANTA, IGNATIUS.—Clerk in the Treasury, 1831; senior clerk, receiver-general's office, 1 Jan., 1851; acted as auditor-general, 1863. Is at present acting registrar general. Was many years in the commissariat department.

H.—Second clerk in the magistrate's office, D'Urban, from Feb. 1862 to August, 1863; appointed acting second clerk, Colonial secretary's office, Aug. 1863 to Dec. 1863; clerk in the governor's office, from Jan. 1864; acted as private secretary to Lieutenant-Governor Scott, from July to 30th Dec. 1864.

BAYLEY, CHAS. J., C.B.—Educated at Eton and Trinity College, Cambridge; was Newcastle scholar at Eton, 1835; scholar of Trinity and Member's (Bachelor) prizeman in 1839; called to the Bar in 1844 by the Inner Temple, was on the Northern and West Riding Circuits till June, 1849; appointed colonial secretary of Mauritius, 1849; and governor of the Bahamas, from Feb., 1857.

BAYNES, EDWIN DONALD.—Was private secretary to the president of Montserrat from 1842 to 1850; colonial secretary of Montserrat, 1850, which he held in conjunction with the office of treasurer, to which he was appointed in 1854; was also controller of customs and navigation laws, a member of the executive council, a commissioner of public works, and a non-elective member of the legislative assembly at Montserrat; administered the government of Tobago from April to December, 1860, during the absence of the lieut.-governor; promoted to the secretaryship of Antigua, May, 1863; administrator of the government of Grenada from April to December, 1865; and again, 1866.

BEAUMONT, JOSEPH.—Chief justice of British Guiana and judge of Vice-Admiralty Court; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, in June 1852; admitted ad eundem at Lincoln's Inn shortly afterwards. From that time to the date of his appointment as chief justice of British Guiana (April 1863), he practised at the bar, and mainly at the chancery bar, in England.

BEDINGFELD, FELIX.—Was private secretary to the governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands, 1834; private secretary to the governor-in-chief of the Windward Islands, 1836; stipendiary magistrate of Bahamas, 1842; Crown commissioner Turks Islands, 1843; master of the supreme court of Trinidad, 1849; appointed treasurer of Mauritius, March, 1854; promoted to be colonial secretary of that island in Aug., 1860. Is official member of the executive and legislative councils, commissioner of the special court of admiralty, board of health, and other committees of that island. A member of the English bar.

BERKELEY, GEORGE.—Colonial secretary and comptroller of customs, Honduras; appointed 1845. Administered the government of Dominica from April 1860 to Dec. 1861. Lieutenant-governor of St. Vincent, April, 1864.

BERKELEY, CHARLES A.—Was appointed notary public at St. Kitt's in 1831; provost-marshal at St. Kitt's, 1833; government interpreter of French and Spanish languages, 1836; appointed to the customs under the Imperial Treasury, July, 1836; comptroller of customs and navigation laws at St. Vincent, 1848; assistant judge in the supreme court of judicature at St. Vincent, 1851, and member of the council at St. Vincent, 1854, appointed treasurer, &c., at Tobago, Dec. 1855; member of the legislative council, Tobago, 1861.

raw_text: BERRIDGE, JAMES S.—President of the legislative council, St. Christopher; legislative council, 1846; puisne judge, court of Queen's Bench and Common Pleas, 1847; executive council, 1858; administrative committee, 1858; chairman of Board of Health, 1860; postmaster, 1860; was deputy postmaster-general, under Imperial Act.

BERRIDGE, T. PROBYN.—Was a member of assembly, St. Christopher, from 1843 till 1851, when appointed clerk of the legislative council. Lieut.-col. commanding the Militia, 1861; was A.D.C. to Prince Alfred when His Royal Highness was in St. Christopher; appointed to the magistracy in 1862.
BERRY, R. B.—Comptroller of customs, Kingston, Jamaica; appointed 1854. Has been in the colonial service since 1831; was landing and tide surveyor from 1838 to 1854.

BERWICK, T.—Admitted an advocate of the supreme court of Ceylon, 1851; was appointed to the Queen's advocate's department, 1851; deputy Queen's advocate, Galle, 1851; acting-deputy Queen's advocate, Kandy, 1857; acted from Jan. to June, 1860, as deputy Queen's advocate for the island, when he resumed his own duties, acting-deputy Queen's advocate, Kandy, 1862, and again for the Island in 1863, resumed his own duties Nov. 1863; acting deputy Queen's Advocate again in 1864.

BESTEL, NICHOLAS GUSTAVE.—Was third judge of the court of appeal at Mauritius, Sept. 1832; vice-president of the same, Mar., 1833; additional substitute to the procureur and advocate-general, May, 1843; senior district magistrate of Port Louis, April, 1854; appointed second puisne judge of the supreme court, March, 1855; first puisne judge, Oct., 1858. Is also acting first judge of the vice-admiralty court in that island.

BEVERIDGE, WILLIAM.—Educated at Glasgow University; entered the colonial service in Jan., 1860, as landing waiter in the Treasury Department, Antigua; appointed treasury accountant 12th Feb. 1862; and provisional treasurer and Comptroller of Her Majesty's Customs, 12th Oct. 1864, and held that office until April, 1865; is ex officio a commissioner of "Loan from Her Majesty's Government;" appointed lieutenant and adjutant of militia infantry, March, 1859.

DUVERGER.—Appointed district clerk at Flacq, Mauritius, April, 1852; clerk to the magistrate at Port Louis, May, 1854; acting district and stipendiary magistrate at Black River, Oct., 1856; at Grand Port, March, 1857; was appointed district magistrate at Flacq, March, 1858; acting protector of immigrants, May, 1859, and confirmed in that appointment, July, 1861.
BINNEY, EDWARD.—Chief clerk in the revenue department, Nova Scotia.

BIRCH, ARTHUR N.—Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, February, 1855; promoted to 3rd class, October, 1859; assistant private secretary to Sir E. Bulwer Lytton, 1858; appointed private secretary to Mr. Chichester Fortescue, September, 1859, until Feb., 1864, when he was permitted, while still remaining on the establishment of the colonial office, to accept the appointment of colonial secretary of British Columbia; is senior member of the executive council, and ex-officio clerk of the same; is also president of the legislative council; administrator of the government from Sept. 1865, to Nov. 1866; assistant senior colonial office, July 5, 1866.

raw_text: BIRCH, J. W. W.—Employed in the commissioner of roads' department, Ceylon, 1846; was appointed a writer, 1852; commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Mallagam, 1853; joint commissioner of requests, and police magistrate, Jaffna, 1853; ditto, Galle, 1853; commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Mallagam, 1854; acting commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Chavagacherry, 1854; assistant government agent, Trincomalie, 1854; district judge, commissioner of requests, and police magistrate of Batticaloa, 1856; acting assistant government agent, 1858; resumed duties as district judge in 1862; acting assistant government agent, Ratuapoora, 1864; promoted to district judgship of Kornegalle, August, 1865.

BIRD, J.—Entered the Royal Engineers in 1856; resigned commissioner, 1862; held temporary appointment under surveyor-general, Hong Kong, in 1860 and 1861; appointed acting assistant surveyor-general in 1864; and was employed in the service of the government of the Cape of Good Hope from 1834 to 1840; was government surveyor at Natal from 1847 to 1850; acted as surveyor-general of that colony from 1851 to 1853; was appointed chief clerk in the office of the colonial secretary, 1853; and resident magistrate of Pietermaritzburg, 1859.

BISSET, ALEXANDER.—In the East India mercantile marine and transport service since 1840; in New Zealand during the war of 1844 and 1845; commander from 1847 to 1858; appointed special magistrate with Kangra, British Kaffraria, Sept., 1861.

BLACK, GEORGE.—Police magistrate at St. John's, Antigua, appointed 1857; inspector of prisons, 1848; is a member of the legislative assembly; served as a member of the administrative committee and board of audit in 1862. Was formerly acting as a stipendiary magistrate.

BOAKE, REV. BARCROFT.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin, where he graduated, July, 1836. Was appointed principal of the Colombo Academy, April, 1842, in conjunction with which office, to which is now added that of Queen's College, Colombo, he held, between 1847 and 1863, at different periods, the offices of officiating chaplain to the troops, and chaplain of Trinity Church, Colombo.

BOOTHBY, BENJAMIN.—Was called to the bar at Gray's Inn, 1841; went the Northern Circuit till 1853; was appointed one of the revising barristers for the West Riding of Yorkshire, 1845 to 1852; recorder of Pontefract, and judge of the court of record there; second judge of her Majesty's supreme court of the province of South Australia by warrant under the Queen's sign manual, February, 1853 (at which time Charles Cooper, Esq. was sole judge); presided at such court as senior judge within the province from Dec. 1856, to July, 1858; deputy-judge of the court of vice-admiralty, 1856 to 1861. Is author of a work on criminal law, 1st edition, 1842, 2nd, 1854; and of a pamphlet, 1844, on the reform of the local and circuit courts of England, and of the superior courts at Westminster, some of the suggestions of which are in operation by imperial legislation since 1853.

BOWEN, G.C.M.G. (Creat. 1860) SIR GEORGE FERGUSON.—Educated at the Charterhouse, and at Trinity College, Oxford, of which he was elected scholar in 1840; graduated 1st class in classics, 1844; in the same year was elected fellow of Brasenose College, and admitted a member of Lincoln's Inn; graduated M.A. in 1847, in which year he was appointed president of the university of Corfu (which office he resigned in 1851); chief secretary to the government of the Ionian Islands in 1854; C.M.G. in 1855, K.C.M.G. in 1856, and promoted to G.C.M.G. 1860; appointed first governor-in-chief and vice-admiral of Queensland, Australia, 1859. Is author of "Ithaca in 1850," "Mount Athos, Thessaly, and Epirus."
BOWEN, H. T.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, June, 1834; appointed puisne judge of Trinidad, 1849. Also assessor to the court of intendant.

BOWEN, RICHARD HENRY.—Appointed revenue officer, Honduras, March, 1837; in Jan., 1842, was keeper of the powder-magazine and militia arms and clothing; clerk of market, March, 1848; keeper of the gaol, May, 1857.
BOWLES, THOS H.—Registrar of the supreme court of the Cape of Good Hope, appointed 1828; acted as a puisne judge in the supreme court from Nov., 1850, to July, 1851.

BOWRING, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1854) SIR JOHN.—Educated at Moretonhampstead, Devon, and received the degree of LL.D. from the university of Groningen; author of numerous works on foreign languages and literature, politics, political economy, and finance; was secretary to the commission for investigating public accounts during Earl Grey's ministry; has been commercial commissioner from Great Britain to France, the Zollverein, the Levant, &c.; was the first editor of the "Westminster Review," and held the office for many years; appointed British consul at Canton, Jan. 1849; subsequently, acting plenipotentiary in China; next, governor, commander-in-chief, and vice-admiral of Hong Kong and its dependencies, as well as superintendent of trade in China, Feb. 1854; knighted on receiving these last offices; was M.P. for Kilmarnock from 1835 to 1837, when he was an unsuccessful candidate; sat for Bolton from 1841 to 1849; unsuccessfully contested Blackburn in 1832 and 1835, and Kirkaldy in Jan. 1841; appointed deputy-lieutenant of Devon, 1860.

raw_text: BRADSHAW, G. T.—Was a clerk in the treasury at Mauritius, Feb., 1854; clerk in colonial secretary's office, Sept., 1854; and appointed a landing-waiter, Feb., 1859.

BRADSHAW, ROBERT.—Colonial surgeon of Sierra Leone, appointed Feb. 1856. Entered the army as staff assistant surgeon in March, 1851, and served in that rank at the Gambia and Sierra Leone to 1856, when he resigned his military commission; served with detachments of 2nd and 3rd West India regiments and Gambia militia, in May, 1852, against the Mahometan forces of Combo, at Jeswung, in the Gambia River; also with a joint naval and military expedition against the kings and chiefs of the Moriah country, Melicourie river, West Africa, in November, 1854; and was also present, and acted as principal medical officer to the detachments of the 1st and 3rd West India regiments, and naval force, at the destruction of the large Mandingo town of Malaghea, near Sierra Leone, on the 22nd and 23rd May, 1855, where, out of a force of 150 men, upwards of 70 men and 4 officers were killed or wounded. For this service he was honourably mentioned in the despatches of both military and naval commanders; is a member of council, and coroner for the colony. Entered the civil service of the colony as superintendent of the colonial hospital, 1853.
BRADY, SIR FRANCIS, KNT.—Called to the Irish bar, 1836; appointed chief justice of Newfoundland and judge of the vice-admiralty, 1847. Knighted by patent, 1860; retired, 1865.
BRAND, SIR C. J. BRAND, K.B. (Creat. 1860.) —Speaker of the house of assembly at the Cape.
BRAVO, A.—Chief commissioner of audit, Jamaica; appointed 1851. Appointed official assignee, 1846.

BRAYBROOKE, P. W.—Was appointed assistant to the government agent, western province of Ceylon, 1842; assistant government agent and district judge of Hambantotte, 1843; assistant government agent and district judge, Nuwerakalawiya, 1849; acting assistant government agent at Kurnegalle, 1850; assistant government agent, district judge, commissioner of requests, and police magistrate of Badulla, 1851; acting principal assistant colonial secretary, and clerk to the councils, 1854; acting auditor-general, 1855; appointed principal assistant colonial secretary, and clerk to the councils, 1855; acting government agent and fiscal for the Central province, 1858, in which appointment he was confirmed, May, 1860. Is a member of the legislative council.

BREEN, HENRY HEGART, F.S.A.—Provost-marshal of St. Vincent, appointed April 1862; first entered the colonial service in March, 1832, as deputy registrar of the Royal Court of St. Lucia; was appointed registrar-in-chief and registrar of deeds and mortgages in April, 1833; was a member of the executive and legislative councils of that island from Nov. 1835 to Sept. 1838; acting colonial secretary and treasurer, and member of the executive and legislative councils, from June, 1840 to May, 1842; was commissioned in July, 1840, by the Lords Commissioners of H.M. Treasury, to inquire into defalcations in the accounts of four former treasurers of St. Lucia. His reports on the subject received their lordships' confirmation. Vice-president of the African Institute of Paris in Nov. 1842; was elected first mayor of Castries, on the introduction of municipal institutions into St. Lucia, in Feb. 1851; was colonial secretary and chosen chairman of the trustees of the St. Lucia Public Library and Museum, in Sept. 1855; administrator of the government from Sept. 1857 to Sept. 1861; is author of "St. Lucia, Historical, Statistical, and Descriptive;" "The Diamond Rock;" "Modern English Literature." Received the special thanks of the Emperor Napoleon for the "cordial and sympathetic part" taken by him on the occasion of inaugurating a statue to the Empress Josephine, at Martinique, in Aug. 1859.

BRINE, MAJOR FREDERIC, K.T.S., F.R.G.S.—Entered the Royal Engineers, 1847; promoted to be lieutenant, 1850, captain, 1856, and major, 1863; served in the West Indies, 1851-3; Crimea, 1855-6; China and Japan, 1861-5; was present at the siege and fall of Sebastopol, and attached to Vice-Admiral Sir Augustus Kuper, K.C.B., in the combined naval operations in Japan, being present at Kagosima, 1863; thanked in despatches; appointed commandant of the Hong Kong Volunteers, 1862; magistrate for the colony same year; lieutenant-colonel commandant of Hong Kong and Canton Volunteers, 1864, which rank he was for his services gazetted to retain, 1865; attached to Japanese mission, 15 Dec. 1865, to 16 Jan. 1866; created a Knight of Tower and Sword of Portugal, 1866.

raw_text: BRISBANE, 1st BISHOP, (Australia). Founded 1859.—RIGHT REV. EDWARD WYNDHAM TUFNELL, D.D.—Educated at Eton; scholar and fellow of Wadham College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A., 1836; M.A., 1842; D.D., 1859; ordained, 1837; appointed rector of Beeching Stoke, Wilts, 1846; prebendary of Salisbury, 1850; senior proctor for university of Oxford, 1857; select preacher, 1858; rector of St. Peter and St. Paul, Marlboro'.
BROADHEAD, D.—Stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana; appointed 1859. Was formerly in the customs' department as first clerk and warehouse-keeper.

BRODIE, A. O.—Was appointed assistant civil engineer and assistant commissioner of roads, Ceylon, and was employed in the north-western provinces 1845 to 1847; stipendiary justice of the peace for the eastern, northern, and north-western provinces, 1848; acting assistant government agent, Nuwerakalawiya, 1850; appointed commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Point Pedro, 1852; assistant government agent, Kandy, 1853, and additional district judge of Matura, 1857 and, 1858, assistant agent, &c., at Matelle; retired, 1864.

raw_text: BROOKE, K.C.B. (Civil. Creat. 1848.)—SIR JAMES BROOKE entered the military service of the E.I.C., on the Bengal establishment; was severely wounded at Rungpoor in the Burmese war, and returned to England; subsequently visited China, and on his return from that country undertook an expedition at his own cost to Borneo, where he assisted the Malay rajah, Muda Hassim, in suppressing an insurrection; subsequently became rajah of Sarawāk, and exerted himself in arresting piracy, facilitating European commerce, and introducing some degree of civilization among the Dyak tribes; was appointed governor and commander-in-chief of Labuan, when that island was taken possession of by the British government, Dec. 1847, and held that office till Feb. 1856; was also commissioner and consul-general to the sultan and independent chiefs of Borneo; received the degree of D.C.L. from Oxford in 1847.

BROWN, JAMES H.—Clerk of the house of assembly of St. Vincent; was appointed to the commission of the peace in 1845; has acted at various times, from 1844 to 1859, as provost-marshal. Was selected in 1858 as agent for St. Vincent, to proceed to Madeira, Teneriffe, and the Cape de Verds, to procure immigrants; appointed acting inspector-general of police, 1861; appointed immigration agent, 1864.

BROWN, JAMES NIBBS.—Entered public service in Antigua, 1825, as clerk to the registrar of slaves, and private secretary of Major-General Sir B. D'Urban, then Governor of the Leeward Islands. Was afterwards chief clerk in the registry of deeds office until May, 1830; landing waiter and searcher and acting collector of customs in Grenada, from 1838 to 1843; member of the House of Assembly there, from 1843 to 1859; also member of the committee for auditing public accounts, a member of the board of works, chairman of the roads board and board of health, Parish St. George; J.P., 1856; a member of executive council and of the board of education; Feb. 1859, appointed police magistrate, Western District, Grenada; appointed May, 1860, police Magistrate, Abaco, Bahamas; is also coroner and notary public.

BULLER, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1848).—SIR ARTHUR WILLIAM BULLER.—Educated at Edinburgh College and Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated M.A. 1834; was called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1834, and went the western circuit; was Queen's advocate in Ceylon from 1840 to July, 1848, when he was appointed a judge of the supreme court of Calcutta, and knighted; retired 1858; has been M.P. for Devonport since June, 1859.

BULWER, HENRY ERNEST, C.M.G.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1859; accompanied Lieut.-Gov. CAPE TOWN, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1847.— RIGHT REV. ROBERT GRAY, D.D.—Educated at Eton and at University College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1831, M.A. 1834; ordained 1833; appointed perpetual curate of Whitworth, Durham, 1834; vicar of Stockton-on-Tees, 1845; honorary canon of Durham Cathedral, 1846; and 1st Bishop of Cape Town, 1847. The diocese at its foundation included the whole of the Cape of Good Hope, Port Natal, and St. Helena, but in 1853 it was confined to the western districts with the island of St. Helena, and made metropolitan in jurisdiction, while two new dioceses were cut off and made suffragans, vide Graham's Town and Natal, to which have been added the dioceses of St. Helena and those for Central Africa and the Free State.

CARDWELL, EDWARD, RT. HON.—Educated at Balliol Coll., Oxford, of which he became a fellow; took a double first-class degree in 1835, and was made an honorary D.C.L. 1863. Called to the bar at the Inner Temple 1838. Was secretary to the Treasury from Feb. 1845 till July 1846. President of the Board of Trade from Dec. 1852 till Feb. 1855; chief secretary for Ireland from June 1859 till July 1861, when he was appointed Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster; a liberal conservative; sat for Clitheroe from 1842 (when he was seated on petition) till July 1847; for Liverpool from the last date till July 1852, when he was an unsuccessful candidate for that town and for Ayrshire; for Oxford city from Jan. 1853 till April 1857, when he was an unsuccessful candidate there; again returned for Oxford July following; secretary of state for the colonies, April 4, 1864, to July 6, 1866.

CHAPMAN, HENRY SAMUEL.—Barrister. Appointed judge of the supreme court of New Zealand, June, 1843; continued to hold the office until March, 1852, when he was appointed colonial secretary of Van Diemen's Land, now Tasmania. In November of the same year he vacated his office. In October, 1854, he commenced practice in Melbourne; in February, 1855, was elected a member of the legislative council; and in March, 1857 (the New Constitution having been introduced in the meantime), he became attorney-general, but the cabinet of which he was a member only held office two months. In March, 1858, being then member of the assembly in St. Kilda, he was called upon by Sir Henry Barkly to form a ministry, in which he succeeded, he himself resuming his former office of attorney-general, which he retained till October, 1859, when the ministry of which he formed part was defeated. In 1862-3, during the absence, on leave, of Sir Redmond Barry, one of the judges of the supreme court, Mr. Chapman filled his seat on the bench, sitting as equity judge. This office he vacated on the return of Sir Redmond Barry, in March, 1863. For several years during the interals of office, he filled the chair of law lecturer at the Melbourne University, which office he continues to hold. He was for many years a writer in the Westminster, London , and other quarterly reviews; contributed some articles to the seventh edition of the "Encyclopædia Britannica;" and still furnishes articles on subjects of colonial law to the Law Magazine .

CHOPPIN, JAMES CLEMENT.—Attorney-general of St. Vincent. Appointed 1863. Was called to the bar at the Middle Temple, 1843; first appointment in colonial service in 1845 as acting police and stipendiary magistrate in St. Vincent; appointed solicitor-general in 1853; was for some years speaker of assembly; appointed to legislative council in 1863. CHRISTCHURCH (New Zealand), 1st BISHOP of. Founded 1856.—RIGHT REV. HENRY JOHN CHITTY HARPER, D.D.—Born at Gosport, 1807; educated at Hyde Abbey, Winchester, and at Queen's College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1826, M.A. 1840. After having been private tutor to the sons of Sir Charles Coote, he officiated for many years as "conduct" or chaplain to Eton College, by which body he was presented in 1840 to the vicarage of Stratfield Mortimer, near Reading, whence he was appointed, in 1856, first Bishop of Christchurch. The diocese includes a portion of the middle island of New Zealand, the whole of the southern island, the Auckland islands, and other smaller islands.

CHRISTIE, WILLIAM H.—Late major 80th Regt.; was educated at Rugby, and at the Royal Military Academy, Woolwich, obtaining a certificate of the first class as a student at the Royal Military College, Sandhurst, in 1832; was appointed ensign, 1825; lieutenant, 1827; captain, 1833; major, 1839. Major Christie's colonial appointments date as follows: engineer of Liverpool stockades, 1837; assistant police magistrate, 1839; agent of church and school lands, 1842; serjeant-at-arms, legislative council, 1847; secretary to board of education, 1848; postmaster-general of New South Wales, 1852.

CHURCHILL, J. F.—Appointed an assistant civil engineer and commissioner of roads, Ceylon, 1856; acted as civil engineer &c. of the island, 1859 to 1861, when he resumed his own duties as assistant civil engineer, Colombo; proceeded to England, July, 1861, where he was employed, under the direction of the secretary of state, on duty connected with the proposed Ceylon railway; resumed his duties in Ceylon April, 1863.

CLARKE, ROBERT, L.R.C.S.E., May 20, 1834.—Appointed assistant colonial surgeon at Sierra Leone, 1837; held subsequently the offices of colonial apothecary and acting colonial surgeon for Freetown till 1854; suggested important alterations and improvements in the Freetown prison, which were approved; appointed surgeon on the Gold Coast, Oct. 1854; discharged the duties of civil commandant, magistrate, postmaster, &c., without pay; appointed sub-collector of customs, April, 1856; acting judicial assessor, July, 1857, to May, 1858; member of executive and legislative councils of the Gold Coast; stipendiary magistrate of Cape Coast; pensioned owing to illhealth in 1860. Author of "Manners and Customs of Liberated Africans," "Notes on the Prevailing Diseases in the Colony of Sierra Leone," read before the British Association, 1855; "Medical Report on the Gold Coast for 1858" ( vide parliamentary reports of Her Majesty's colonial possessions), "Remarks on Topography of the Gold Coast," &c.

CLARKE, SIR ROBERT BOWCHER, C.B. and B.C.L.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated LL.B. 1827; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1827; was solicitor-general of Barbados from 1837 to 1842, and speaker of the house of assembly there; appointed chief justice of Barbados in 1842. Was also chief justice of St. Lucia from 1848 to 1859; was knighted by patent, 1840, in recognition of his services in promoting the termination of the apprenticeship system and other measures connected with the emancipation of the slaves; created a C.B. in 1848.

Kurnegalle, 1853; district judge, &c. of Badulla, 1859; retired 1863.

CLARKE, WILLIAM PHILLIPS.—Entered the colonial secretary's office Barbadoes, May, 1853; appointed chief clerk, 1855; was private secretary to Governor Walker during his administration of the government of Trinidad, 1860-61; acted at the same time as clerk of the council of government in that island; returned to Barbados, March, 1861, and resumed his duties in the colonial secretary's office; private secretary to Mr. Hollyar, who administers the Government of St. Kitts, 1864-5-6.

M. 2nd dragoon guards (queen's bays), as cornet, by purchase in 1848; was promoted to be lieutenant by purchase, Aug. 1850; was appointed adjutant to the regiment in April, 1852; resigned the adjutancy, and exchanged to the 15th king's hussars in 1854; retired from the army by the sale of his commission in 1855; was a captain in the royal Sherwood foresters in 1856-58; appointed inspector-general of police in Sept. 1858; and colonel and adjutant-general of militia in Barbados, Feb. 1860.

raw_text: COCHRANE, K.C.B., 1847; KNT. BACHEL., 1825.—SIR THOMAS JOHN COCHRANE.—Attained the rank of admiral of the white in 1857; served in the expeditions against Belleisle, Ferrol, Cadiz, and Egypt, &c.; was governor of Newfoundland from 1825 to 1834; was M.P. for Ipswich from 1837 to 1841; unsuccessfully contested Westminster in 1835; received the honour of knighthood, having been proxy for his father at the installation of the Bath in 1812; was commander-in-chief on the East India station from 1842 to 1846.

COCKBURN, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1841.)—SIR FRANCIS COCKBURN.—Received the honour of knighthood by patent; became a general in the army in 1860; colonel of the 95th foot, 1853; has served in Canada, and was governor at Honduras; was, in 1837, governor and commander-in-chief of the Bahama Islands.

COCKBURN, SAMUEL.—Held several appointments in Grenada: viz., interpreter-general, 1844; inspector of prisons, 1845; inspector-general of schools, 1853; provost-marshal, 1854; stipendiary magistrate, 1855; immigration agent, 1856; president of Montserrat, 1860 and 1862; police-magistrate, British Honduras, June, 1862.

CODRINGTON, K.C.B. (Creat. 1855.)—SIR WILLIAM JOHN CODRINGTON.—Entered the army in 1821; became lieut.-col. coldstream guards in 1836; commanded an infantry brigade in the early portion of the Crimean campaign; and subsequently commanded the light division at the capture of Sebastopol; appointed commander-in-chief of the British forces in the East, with the local rank of general, 1855, and received the brevet of lieut.-general in 1856 "as a mark of royal approbation;" made a K.C.B. 1855, grand cross of the Sardinian order of Savoy, and commander of the legion of honour, 1856, for his services against the Russians; M.P. for Greenwich since Feb. 1857 to May, 1859; appointed governor and commander-in-chief at Gibraltar, May, 1859.

COLOMBO, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1845.—RIGHT REV. JAMES CHAPMAN, D.D.—Educated at King's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1823, M.A. 1826; was ordained in 1824; became one of the masters of Eton, and fellow of King's College; appointed rector of Dunton-Waylett, Essex, in 1834; consecrated 1st Bishop of Colombo in 1845; thereupon received the degree of D.D. from Cambridge, and was admitted ad eundem at Oxford. Retired on a pension, 1861, and elected fellow of Eton College, 1862.

COWAN, WALKINSHAW.—Appointed resident magistrate of York district, Western Australia, 1863. For many years was guardian of Aborigines, in the colony.

COWPER, CHARLES.—Colonial secretary and vice-president of the executive council of New South Wales—and premier; was premier in the second ministry formed after the introduction of responsible government, from Aug. 26, 1856, to Oct. 2, 1856; again from Sept. 7, 1857, to Oct. 20, 1859. Took office as colonial secretary, and represented the government in the legislative council, in the ministry formed by Mr. Robertson on March 9, 1860, and having been elected to represent the city of Sydney in the legislative assembly, became premier on Jan. 10, 1861.
COWPER, WILLIAM MACQUANE, M.A., of Magdalen Hall, Oxford.—Dean of Sydney, New South Wales. Is son of the late Archdeacon Cowper of Sydney.

COX, AUSTIN W.—Immigration agent, Honduras, 1865; member of the executive council; surveyor of shipping, July, 1855; shipping master, Feb. 1864; coroner, Feb. 1864; speaker of the House of Assembly, from 1861; and performed the duties of colonial secretary, from Aug. 1864 to Oct. 1865, in Honduras.

CREAGH, COLONEL C.—Inspector-general of police, and adjutant-general, and inspecting field-officer of militia and volunteers of St. Vincent. Late 80th regiment, captain and bt.-major; served in India under the late Field-Marshal Viscount Hardinge, in the campaigns of the Sutlej and Punjub, was present at the general actions of Moodkee, Ferozeshuhur, and Sabraon; was specially mentioned by Lord Hardinge at the battle of Ferozeshuhur; as a captain, commanded his regiment for ten days on service; served twice on the Staff and was appointed on special service as major during the Crimean war; is a lieut.-colonel in the Turkish Army; and has received a medal and three clasps for his Indian services, and the Turkish Crimean war medal.

(Creat. 1860).—SIR EDWARD SHEPHERD CREASY.—Educated at Queen's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1835, M.A. 1838; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1837; was for some years professor of ancient and modern history at University College, London; author of "Poems" and of some historical works; appointed chief justice of Ceylon, 1860, and on that occasion was knighted; was previously deputy assistant judge at the Middlesex sessions.

raw_text: CROOKS, ROBERT.—Inspector-general of police at Tobago. Appointed 1854.

CROSBY, JAMES.—Educated and graduated at Trinity College, Cambridge; called to the bar by the honourable society of the Middle Temple, and went the Hampshire sessions and the Western circuit; went to St. Vincent in the West Indies, on a special retainer; and in May, 1844, became police magistrate for the Kingstown police district in that island, with liberty to practise at the bar in all cases except those in which he had acted as a magistrate; continued as such police magistrate until March, 1857, except during the short periods of the termination of one Police Act and the enactment of another Police Act, during one, in 1853, he became speaker of the house of assembly, of which for many years he had been a member; and subsequently, in 1854, became acting treasurer of the island, until the Police Act of 1853, of which he was the author, came into operation. While in St. Vincent was the author of a Code of Laws, giving summary jurisdiction to justices of the peace, for the establishment of a police force, and for the consolidation of offences punishable by summary convictions, which code, having been successfully carried through the house of assembly, he afterwards, as speaker, signed on being passed. In March, 1857, became a stipendiary justice of the peace in and for the colony of British Guiana; and on the 1st July, 1858, was appointed immigration agent general during the absence in China of Mr. John Gardiner Austin, now lieutenant-governor of the Honduras. On the return of Mr. Austin from China, he became, from the 1st October, 1862, acting immigration agent general; and in February, 1864, on the promotion of Mr. Austin, was confirmed in the appointment of immigration agent general in and for the colony of British Guiana; and on the 8th November, 1863, was appointed acting second puisne judge of British Guiana and so continued until 22nd August, 1866, when he assumed his appointment of immigration agent general. During the early part of his residence in St. Vincent, he commanded, as lieutenant-colonel, the southern regiment of militia, and was aide-de-camp to the governor-in-chief of the Windward Islands and to the lieutenant-governor of St. Vincent; and was also the author of several other Acts of the Legislature of St. Vincent, giving a permanent revenue to that colony.

DALY, SIR DOMINICK, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1856).—Was appointed secretary to the province of Lower Canada in 1827 (having previously held the office of assistant secretary in that province for two years), on the union of the provinces of Upper and Lower Canada in 1840; was appointed secretary of the province of Canada, which office he held until 1848; in 1849 was appointed a commissioner of inquiry into the claims, &c. &c., of the New and Waltham Forests, which he held until the close of the commission in 1850-1; appointed lieut.-governor of Tobago, 1851, transferred to the government of Prince Edward's Island in 1854, which he held until 1859; and appointed governor and commander-in-chief of South Australia, Nov. 1861.
DALY, JOHN.—Administrator-general of Demerara and Essequibo; appointed 1851. Paid by fees. Was appointed harbour-master at Berbice, 1827.
DALY, JOHN, jun.—Stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana; was inspector of police and also a sworn clerk in the registrar's office.

DALZIEL, JOHN.—Entered the army in 1813; embarked with his regiment, 71st light infantry, for the West Indies, Jan. 1815; but the destination of the regiment being altered, landed in Flanders; served during the campaign of 1815; was present at the battle of Waterloo, and subsequent operations; was present at the surrender of Paris, served with the army of occupation, and returned to England in 1818; served with the 78th highlanders in Ceylon, 1826-37; was appointed superintendent of police at Colombo, 1839; police magistrate, coroner, and justice of the peace, Colombo, 1844; he also acted for three months in 1857, as commissioner of requests in Gampolla, and for the same period as district judge in Caltura, 1858-9; retired 1864.

raw_text: DAMPIER, FREDERICK ELLIOT.—Superintendent of rivers and creeks, Essequibo, British Guiana 1860; Stipendiary Magistrate, 1865.
DANIELL, G. W.—Police magistrate, and member of the legislative and executive councils of Nevis.

DANIELL, WM. MEADE.—Entered the colonial service in 1839, as clerk in the governor's office; Antigua, and served in that capacity under three successive governors-in-chief; was private secretary to President Daniels, when administering the government of Antigua; appointed a landing-waiter of the customs establishment in 1844, and on its abolition was nominated to a clerkship in the treasury; harbour-master of Parham in 1857; and re-appointed to the treasury, 1862.

D'ARCY, COLONEL GEO.—Appointed governor and commander-in-chief of the Gambia, June, 1859; was adjutant of the 94th regiment, in which corps he served 16 years, promoted by purchase as lieutenant-colonel in the 3rd West India regiment; after commanding the corps for three years, agreeable to royal warrant gazetted as brevet-colonel, July 7, 1857; sold out, May, 1858; appointed governor of the Gambia in June, 1859; served in the southern Mahratta war, 1845-6, East Indies; organized an expedition against the powerful robber king of Badiboo, and commanded the royal Gambia militia artillery throughout the successful campaign which followed during the months of Feb and March, 1861; was aide-de-camp from 1839 to 1848, to five successive governors of Bombay, and also to General Sir Willoughby Cotton, G.C.B., 1850-51, when he commanded the Bombay army; author of a work on Light Infantry Drill.

DARLING, SIR CHARLES HENRY, K.C.B. (Creat. 1862.)—Entered the army as ensign on the 7th Dec. 1826, by recommendation from the Royal Military College, Sandhurst, after public examination; became in 1827, assistant private secretary to the late General Sir Ralph Darling, then governor of New South Wales and major-general commanding the forces in that colony and Van Diemen's Land; and was also appointed military secretary to that officer in 1830. In 1833, while a student in the senior department of the Royal Military College, was appointed secretary to the late Lieut.-General Sir Lionel Smith, upon that officer's nomination to be governor and commander-in-chief of Barbados and the Windward Islands, and was employed in that capacity until 1836, Sir Lionel Smith having been in that year nominated to the government of Jamaica, Mr. Darling was appointed governor's secretary for that colony, and retained the appointment until the termination of Sir Lionel Smith's government in 1839; in that year obtained an unattached company, and retired from the army in 1841; in 1843, appointed by the Earl of Elgin, then governor of Jamaica, agent-general of immigration for the colony, and held also the office of adjutant-general of militia, was a member of the assembly and of several executive boards; was again appointed governor's secretary during the ad-interim administration of the government of Jamaica by Major-General Sackville H. Berkeley, and continued to hold that office during the earlier period of the government of the Right Hon. Sir Charles Grey in 1846-47; in 1847 was appointed lieutenant-governor of the island of St. Lucia; in 1851, appointed lieutenant-governor of the Cape of Good Hope, an office specially created for the conduct at Cape Town of the civil government while Sir George Cathcart the governor and commander-in-chief, might be engaged in his military and civil duties on the frontier. After the departure of Sir George Cathcart, administered the government of the Cape from May, 1854, to Dec. 1854, during which period the parliamentary constitution of the settlement was inaugurated and established; was nominated to the government-in-chief of Antigua and the Leeward Islands, previously to his sole administration of the government of the Cape; but never assumed the duties of that government, having shortly after his arrival in England, been required to proceed to Newfoundland as administrator of the government of that colony, of which he was subsequently appointed governor and commander-in-chief; in Feb. 1857, was appointed captain-general and governor-in-chief of Jamaica, an office which embraces the general superintendence of the affairs of British Honduras and the Turks' Islands, and at that time included also the government-in-chief of the Bay Islands; received the order of the bath (K.C.B.), 1862, for his "long and effective public services." Appointed governor of Victoria, 1863; recalled, March, 1866.

DARRELL, JOHN H.—Chief justice of Bermuda; appointed 1856. Formerly of Trinity College, Cambridge; a barrister of Lincoln's Inn. Is a member of the executive and legislative councils; has been in the colonial service since 1819; was attorney-general from 1834 to 1856. Published an edition of the Laws of Bermuda in 1861.
DARRELL, NATHANIEL JOSEPH.—District commissary of taxation, British Guiana.

DE BROSSARD, LOUIS AMEDEE.—Colonial Secretary, Montserrat. On the 4th March, 1835, he was nominated to the office of solicitor-general in St. Lucia; on the 5th Feb. 1836, was appointed an official member of the legislative council; he discharged, at different periods, the duties of attorney-general, viz., in the years 1836, 1840, and 1841, and was finally confirmed by Her Majesty in that office on the 24th January, 1842; in the same year he was appointed to the office of stipendiary magistrate, and on the 4th May, 1847, provisional puisne judge of the royal court; in the month of April, 1854, was appointed immigration agent; on the 30th March, 1863, he was nominated colonial secretary, treasury, comptroller of customs and navigation laws, clerk of the crown, registrar of deeds, and registrar-general of births, deaths, and marriages, in Montserrat; is a barrister-at-law.

DENISON, K.C.B. (Civil, Creat. 1856); KNT. BACHEL. 1846.—SIR WILLIAM THOMAS DENISON.—Became lieut.-col. royal engineers in 1855; was appointed lieut.-governor of Van Diemen's Land in June, 1846, and on that occasion knighted; governor-general of New South Wales, Sept. 1854 to 1860, when he was appointed governor of the Madras presidency.

DERBY, 14th EARL of (England, Creat. 1485) BARON STANLEY, 1832 (United Kingdom); BART. 1627 (England); Privy councillor, 1830.—EDWARD GEOFFREY SMITH-STANLEY.—Succeeded his father in 1851; was educated at Christ Church, Oxford, where he gained the Latin Verse prize in 1819 (subject Syracuse ); was under-secretary for the colonies during a part of the Goderich administration; chief secretary for Ireland from 1830 till March, 1833; secretary of state for the colonies from March, 1833 till July, 1834, and from Sept. 1841 to Dec. 1845; first lord of the treasury from Feb. to Dec. 1852, and from Feb. 1858 to June, 1859; lord warden of the cinque ports, temporarily, from Oct. 1852 to Jan. 1853. Was elected lord rector of Glasgow University in 1834; and chancellor of the University of Oxford, Oct. 1852; was M.P. for Stockbridge in 1820, and for Preston from 1826 to 1830; sat for Windsor in 1830 and 1831; for North Lancashire from 1832 to Sept. 1844, when he accepted the Chiltern Hundreds, and was in the next month summoned to the House of Peers in his father's barony of Stanley of Bickerstaffe; is a deputy-lieut. of Lancashire.

CASTLE-CUFFE, 1793; BARON OF DESART, 1733 (Ireland).—JOHN OTWAY O'CONNOR CUFFE.—Son of the second earl, succeeded his father in 1820; educated at Christ Church, Oxford; was elected M.P. for Ipswich in June, 1842, but was unseated on petition in the August following; elected a representative peer of Ireland, 1846; was under-secretary for the colonies from March to Dec. 1852.

DICKSON, J. F.—Educated at Westminster and Christ Church, Oxford, where he obtained a studentship, 1855; was first class in classics, moderations. Easter, 1857, and graduated B.A., 1859. Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, Feb. 1859; attached to the colonial secretary's office Sept. 1859; acting secretary to the school commission, 1860; continuing to do duty in colonial secretary's office, and acted early in 1862 as 2nd assistant colonial secretary; police magistrate at Dambool, Oct. 1862; acting assistant agent in Kaigalle, Oct. 1862; acting police magistrate at Kandy, April 1863; acting 2nd assistant colonial secretary, Dec. 1863; secretary to the commission appointed in June, 1865, to inquire into the military expenditure and establishments of Ceylon; police magistrate of Galle, continuing to act as assistant colonial secretary, Oct. 1865.

DICKSON, HON. WILLIAM GILLESPIE.—Procureur and advocate-general, Mauritius, appointed Dec. 1856. Is a member ex-officio of the executive and legislative councils, Queen's advocate in vice-admiralty court, &c.

raw_text: DILLET, STEPHEN.—Postmaster at the Bahamas; appointed 1846; and adjutant-general of the militia.

DILLET, T. WM. HENRY.—Educated at King's College, London. Entered as a student of the Inner Temple, 19th Aug., 1838; was for a short time in 1845 acting clerk of the police court at Nassau Bahamas; appointed a lieutenant in the Militia Field Artillery, Feb. 1844; served till its disbandment by the legislature; reappointed when the corps was reorganized in 1845. Called to the bar at the Bahamas, 18th April, 1848; secretary to the Board of Education, 5th June, 1850, to 31st Oct., 1851; all at the Bahamas was acting Queen's advocate at Turks Island from April to Nov. 1852, and admitted a notary public there, May, 1852. Was in the legislative assembly of Bahamas from 24th March, 1857, to Sept., 1865, when he was called to the legislative council, of which he is now a member. From 28th Oct., 1859, to 17th May, 1860, legal adviser to the military department; acting assistant justice of the general court, Oct. 1864 to Oct., 1865; chief judicial office within the colony, from Aug. till Oct., 1865.

DINGLI, K.C.M.G. — Appointed 1860; C.B., 1859—SIR ADRIAN DINGLI. — Created doctor of law, 1836; received at Malta bar, 1837; elected member of the council of Malta, 1849; appointed crown advocate, legal adviser of the government, and ex-officio member of council, July, 1854. Received the order of St. M. and St. G. for official services.

DIX, MACNAMARA. — Colonial treasurer, St. Lucia; appointed 1860. Is member of the executive and legislative councils, and comptroller of customs and navigation laws; was auditor-general of Dominica from 1849 to 1860, and administered the government of St. Lucia from Oct. 1861 to May 1862.

DUSMANI, SIR ANTONIO LERCOCHILO.—COUNT DUSMANI, K.C.M.G.—Entered the service Feb. 16, 1829, assistant in the senate office, Ionian Islands March 6, 1833, under-secretary of the senate; Nov. 3, 1834, secretary of the senate for the political department; Aug. 8, 1853, secretary of the senate for the general department; Dec. 26, 1834, general archivist; March 7, 1836, secretary of the general agricultural society, and directing secretary of its central committee, established by act of parliament; Nov. 17, 1841, secretary of the general commission on public instruction, until 1857, when the act of parliament establishing it was repealed; also member of the same from 1853; 1843, member of a commission to revise the judicial organization and procedure; 1855 and 1856, member of a commission to inquire into all the departments of the state; 1855, member of an extraordinary central sanitary commission during the prevalence of cholera in the Ionian Islands; 1859, member of a commission to examine the establishments and the departments of the state. May 30, 1844, appointed Companion of the most distinguished order of Saints Michael and George; Dec. 1849, promoted knight commander; Nov. 23, 1852, appointed, under Her Majesty's warrant, officer of arms of the said order.

DYETT, EDWARD BOWMAN.—Resident puisne justice and paid magistrate, Montserrat; appointed 1858. Is also coroner and escheator-general; was acting treasurer in 1852, and also from January 31st, 1853, to 10th May; acted as Queen's counsel in 1857, and several times before; first appointed coroner in 1851; is a member of the executive council; and is of the colonial bar.

DYETT, RICHARD H.—Provost-marshal of Montserrat; appointed private secretary to the late Sir John Macgregor, Bart.; president of the Virgin Islands, in March, 1851; was private secretary to Lieutenant-Colonel Chads and Mr. Kortright, in the Virgin Islands; appointed coroner for the Virgin Islands in 1853; was also justice of the peace, and on several occasions acting provost-marshal there; is a notary public for the Virgin Islands; acted on several occasions as provost-marshal of Montserrat, and also as clerk of the council; held the office of coroner of Montserrat from August, 1862, to September, 1865, when he was appointed as provost-marshal; was private secretary to Mr. Robinson, and Capt. Shaw, R.N., in the government of Montserrat; is a non-elective member of the legislative assembly; and a justice of the peace for Montserrat.

ELLIOT, T. FREDERICK.—Assistant under secretary of state, colonial department; appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 5 July, 1825. In July, 1835, named by warrant from the crown to be secretary to commission of inquiry into the state of Canada, composed of the Earl of Gosford, Sir Charles Grey, and Sir George Gipps. In April, 1837, appointed to be the head of an office of emigration, then for the first time created, under the title of Agent General of Emigration. In 1840, this office being expanded into a Board of Advice and Management over Colonial Land and Emigration, made chairman of the same. In 1847 transferred to the colonial department, and appointed assistant under secretary of state for the colonies.

EVATT, H. A.—Was formerly in the army; served in the 90th light infantry, and Ceylon rifle regiment; whilst still on full pay acted as an assistant in the commissioner of roads department in Ceylon, from the 4th Feb. 1842 to 16th May, 1848, when he was appointed to act as commissioner of roads, and continued in that office until the 2nd Nov. 1849; when he resumed his duties as an assistant in that department. He sold out of the army in 1851, and was appointed assistant civil engineer, and commissioner of roads in April of that year. He acted as head of the department in August, 1854, and resumed his own duties in July, 1856. He acted as surveyor-general, with a seat in the legislative council, from May, 1858, to March 1859; and again from Nov. 1860 to Sept. 1861; appointed acting civil engineer and commissioner of roads in Oct. 1865.

ESPENT, W. BANCROFT.—First-class clerk, in charge of the dispatch and correspondence branch of the colonial secretary's office, Jamaica; received his first appointment as clerk in the immigration office, from Sir Charles H. Durling, K.C.B., on the 19th May, 1861; transferred by Governor Eyre to the executive committee office, on the 13th July, 1862, as 2nd clerk; appointed chief clerk, provisionally, by Sir Henry Storks, G.C.B., on the 10th April, 1866. On the change in the Constitution, was appointed to his present office, by Sir J. Peter Grant, K.C.B.; acted from 6th February, to the end of 1864, as secretary of police; is a lieutenant in the 2nd regiment of Jamaica militia, 18th July, 1863; and 1st lieutenant of the 2nd St. Catherine's Rifles, 30th August, 1864.

EVELYN, G. J. — Served in commissariat from August, 1828, to Dec. 1836; appointed treasury clerk at St. Kitt's, Aug. 1850, sub-treasurer, Sept. 1857; acting treasurer, 1850, 1851, and again in 1862; appointed immigration agent, 1857.
EVELYN, W. T. — Police magistrate, St. Joseph's parish, Barbados.

EYRE, EDWARD JOHN. — Was for some years engaged in explorations in Australia; in 1840-41 commanded an exploring expedition under the auspices of the South Australian government, penetrating to nearly $29^{\circ}$ S. lat., then returning and crossing from the head of Spencer's Gulf to Western Australia, an account of which will be found in a work published by him entitled "Journals of Expeditions of Discovery into Central Australia, and overland from Adelaide to King George's Sound in 1840-41." This journey, in connection with Mr. Eyre's previous ones, completed his transit across the continent of New Holland from east to west, from Sydney to Swan River. For these tracts the Royal Geographical Society awarded him their gold medal in 1842. First appointment under the colonial office, Sept. 1841, as resident magistrate at Moorundee in South Australia, which office he held until Dec. 1846, when he was appointed lieut.-governor of New Ulster and New Munster in New Zealand; on the expiration of his term of service, returned to England, Sept. 1853; appointed lieut.-governor of St. Vincent, Oct. 1854, which he held until the end of March, 1861; administered the government-in-chief of the Leeward Islands from April, 1859 to April, 1860, during the absence of the governor; specially commissioned in Feb. 1862, to administer the general government of Jamaica and its dependencies, during the absence of the governor, Sir Charles Darling, K.C.B.; appointed governor, April 1864. In Oct. 1865, put down an insurrection of the negroes, which was ushered in by the savage butchering of a number of magistrates and other gentlemen at St. Thomas's-in-the-East. Received address of thanks from the legislature, the bishop and clergy, from dissenting ministers, planters, and others, and from the ladies and women of Jamaica. The legislative council, in their addresses, recorded "grateful thanks for the energy, firmness, and wisdom with which your excellency has carried the island through this momentous crisis." The address from the assembly was in equally flattering terms. In Nov. 1865, Mr. Eyre induced the legislative bodies to abrogate their existing functions. The form of government which was afterwards instituted, the appointment of Sir H. Storks, and the reasons which induced Her Majesty's government to recal Governor Eyre, are to be found under the heading of Jamaica. Mr. Eyre returned in Aug. 1866.

FITZPATRICK, JAMES COLEMAN.—Is a graduate of Dublin University. In 1844, was called to the Irish bar; in 1847, was appointed judicial assessor to the sovereigns and chiefs in the countries adjacent to Her Majesty's forts and settlements of the Gold Coast; in 1853, was appointed chief justice of Her Majesty's settlements on the Gold Coast; in 1857, was called to the English bar, special gratiâ, at Lincoln's Inn; in 1861, was appointed judge of the supreme court of British Kaffraria.

FITZROY, GEORGE HENRY.—Was private secretary to the governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands from Jan. 1842, till Sept. 1845; was private secretary to the governor-general of the Australian colonies from Sept. 1845 till Jan. 1855; was assistant secretary to the convict prison board in 1857; was attached to the Earl of Elgin's special mission to China from April 17, 1857, till May, 1859; was appointed by the Emperor of China commissioner of His Imperial Majesty's customs at Canton, July 1, 1859.

FLANDERKA, J. L.—Appointed a writer in the civil service of Ceylon, 1846; assistant government agent, Mullettoe, 1847; acting assistant government agent, Jaffna, 1852; held the same office at Nuwerakalawiya, 1854; also at Manaar in 1855; assistant government agent, &c. at Anooradhapoora, 1856. Acted as government agent, fiscal and collector of customs, northern province, from Jan. to Nov. 1861, when he resumed his own duties.
FLEMING, C. W.—Police magistrate, St. George's Point, Barbados.

FLEMING, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1856.)—HON. SIR VALENTINE FLEMING.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin, where he graduated B.A. 1834, and took honours; was called to the bar at Gray's Inn, 1838; appointed commissioner of the insolvent court for Hobart Town, 1841; solicitor-general of Tasmania, 1844; attorney-general, Jan. 1848; chief justice of the supreme court there, Aug. 1854; and knighted by patent, 1856.

raw_text: FORD, EDWARD S.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Clanwilliam division of Cape Colony; appointed Dec. 1858; was magistrates' clerk at Richmond, 1848 to 1854; clerk to the civil commissioner, Graaff Reinet, Sept. 1854 to 1859.
FORD, H. A.—Appointed deputy postmaster at Kandy, Ceylon, 1847; acting police magistrate, Calpentyn, 1862. Retired on pension, 1866.

raw_text: FORSTER, WILLIAM EDWARD, M.P.—A magistrate and deputy lieutenant for the West Riding of York; unsuccessfully contested Leeds, April, 1859; first elected for Bradford, Feb. 1861; appointed under secretary of state for the colonies, Nov., 1865.

FORTESCUE, CHICHESTER, S., The Rt. Hon., M.P.—Educated at Christchurch, Oxford, where he was first class in classics in 1844, and obtained the chancellor's prize for the English essay in 1846; was a lord of the Treasury from Jan. 1854 to April, 1855; under secretary of state for the colonies, May 1857, to Feb. 1858, and re-appointed June 1859. Has been M.P. for Co. Louth since 1847. Privy counsellor, 1864; chief secretary for Ireland, Nov. 1865.

FORTH, FREDK.—Was formerly a captain in the royal North British fusiliers; appointed visiting magistrate, Van Diemen's Land, 1833; served in that colony as A.D.C. to the governor; officer in command of mounted police; police magistrate, deputy chairman of quarter sessions, commissioner of court of requests; and appointed in 1841 director-general of roads, which office he held until 1848, when he returned to England; was appointed president of the Turks and Caicos Islands from 1848 to 1854; treasurer of Hong Kong, 1857; a member of the legislative council, and acting colonial secretary from Aug. to Nov. 1858.

FOX, CHARLES JAMES.—Clerk, court of appeal, Mauritius, May, 1846, assistant district clerk, Port Louis, Mauritius, 1852; district clerk at Seychelles, Oct. 1858; appointed chief clerk, police court, Mauritius, Aug. 1861.
FOX, REV. GEORGE, A.M.—Principal of Queen's college grammar school, British Guiana.

events.text_span: Second revenue officer, Tobago, appointed 1860.

raw_text: FRANCIS, H.—Second revenue officer, Tobago, appointed 1860.

events.position: commanded the steam transport 'Tonning,' the leading ship in the first division of transports in the expeditionary force landed in the Crimea
