events.position: received a vote of thanks and a silver salver from the North of Europe Steam Navigation Company for his exertions in saving life during the hurricane at Balaclava
events.text_span: received a vote of thanks and a silver salver from the North of Europe Steam Navigation Company for his exertions in saving life during the hurricane at Balaclava, Nov. 1855.

raw_text: FRANKLYN, WILLIAM HALES.—Appointed a magistrate and government agent at Nainaimo, Vancouver's Island, June, 1860; holds an extra certificate of competency as commander in the mercantile marine; commanded the steam transport 'Tonning,' the leading ship in the first division of transports in the expeditionary force landed in the Crimea, Sept. 1855; and during the war with Russia was honourably mentioned by Lord Clyde and other distinguished officers; received a vote of thanks and a silver salver from the North of Europe Steam Navigation Company for his exertions in saving life during the hurricane at Balaclava, Nov. 1855.

raw_text: FRANKS, CHARLES W.—Educated at Eton, and Christchurch, Oxford, where he took his degree in 1847; was one of the assistant-secretaries on the staff of the commissioners for the international exhibition of 1862; is a fellow of the Royal Geographical Society, and a corresponding member of the Royal Zoological Society. Has edited (in conjunction with Tom Taylor) a catalogue of the works of Sir Joshua Reynolds. Appointed treasurer of British Columbia, April, 1864.

events.text_span: appointed 1848.

raw_text: FRASER, J. D.—Stipendiary magistrate of British Guiana; appointed 1848.

FRASER, ROSE DEMPSTER.—Comptroller of customs and navigation laws, and registrar of shipping, Barbados; entered the imperial customs, as landing-waiter and searcher at Bermuda, 1838; appointed senior searcher, 1839; nominated 8th April 1845, by warrant from governor of Bermuda (the late Sir W. Reid), to survey and report on all public moneys in the colonial treasury; nominated acting landing surveyor, 1848; appointed same year landing waiter and searcher at Bridgetown, Barbadoes; appointed landing waiter and searcher at Kingston, Jamaica, 1850; which appointment was cancelled in the year following, at his request, and on nomination as acting landing surveyor, Barbados; appointed first-class landing waiter, colonial customs, Barbados, 1853; landing surveyor, 1855; and comptroller, &c., 1857.

FREDERICTON, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1845.—RIGHT REV. JOHN MEDLEY, D.D.—Was educated at Wadham College, Oxford, where he was 2nd class in classics, 1826, graduated M.A. 1830, and D.D. 1845; was appointed vicar of St. Thomas's, Exeter, in 1838; prebendary of Exeter cathedral in 1842; and consecrated 1st Bishop of this see in 1845. Is author of a tract on episcopacy, an address to Sunday-school teachers, and has published several sermons. The diocese includes the province of New Brunswick, and was erected into a bishop's see in 1845.

events.text_span: appointed July, 1856.

raw_text: FREEMAN, J. D.—Emigration agent for Mauritius, at Bombay; appointed July, 1856.

events.position: appointed collector of customs at Jamaica
events.text_span: appointed collector of customs at Jamaica, 1854

raw_text: FREEMAN, WILLIAM GARDNER.—Entered the colonial service in 1835; appointed collector of customs at Jamaica, 1854; is a member of the privy council, late member of the legislative council. Is principal comptroller of customs and navigation laws; registrar of shipping and justice of the peace for the parish of Kingston and Port Royal, in the island of Jamaica.
FRENCH, A. LYNCH.—Member of legislative council, St. Christopher, appointed 1851; acting superintendent of police, and police magistrate for rural districts, July, 1862, till July, 1863.

raw_text: FRENCH, JOHN.—Clerk to the government secretary, British Guiana; appointed 1830; retired 1863.
FULLER, FREDERIC W.—Entered the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 28th April, 1862, after competitive examination.

raw_text: FURLONGE, WM. D.—Clerk in the marshal's office, Antigua, appointed in 1864; acting landing-waiter, treasury department, 1852; clerk in the colonial secretary's office, 1862.

FYERS, R.E., CAPT. A. B.—Entered Royal engineers, Dec. 1847; promoted to captain, Aug. 1856; served in Mauritius from 1849 until 1858, during the greater part of which time he was "government meteorological observer;" was stationed at the Curragh camp from 1858 until 1861, and at Malta from 1861 until 1865; appointed surveyor-general of Ceylon 26 April, 1866. Is on the seconded list.

GARLAND, S. S.—Appointed assistant receiver-general, Turks Islands, March, 1851. Is also assistant police magistrate and coroner; was first appointed to the customs in 1840.

GIBBS, GEORGE MEADE, M.A.—Archdeacon of St. Kitt's, Nevis, &c.; educated at Trinity College, Dublin; obtained at entrance recommendation for honours and Hebrew prize; graduated 1847; ordained by the bishop of Lichfield, 1848, for Trinity parish, Derby; appointed to curacy of St. Mary's, Southwark, 1851; Wonston, Hants, 1855; archdeacon of St. Kitt's, and rector of St. George's, Basseterre, by the Duke of Newcastle, on the nomination of the archbishop of Canterbury, Dec. 1861. Member of Her Majesty's executive and legislative councils, St. Kitt's, 1862.

acting police magistrate, Harrispastoo, 1866.

raw_text: GIBSON, J. W.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1865; acting police magistrate, Harrispastoo, 1866.

GIBSON, T. L.—Held the office of deputy to the Queen's advocate at Kandy, Ceylon, 1842; appointed police magistrate, Bentotte, 1844; and commissioner of requests at that place, 1845; district judge, Badulla, 1846; acted as district judge, Kurnegalle, 1848, in which appointment he was confirmed Feb. 1850; assistant government agent, &c., Ratnapoora, 1854; acting assistant agent, Matura, Aug. 1854; acting postmastergeneral, 1855; acting district judge of Galle, 1857; acting government agent, &c., eastern province, 1859; district judge, Kurnegalle, 1861; acted as district judge, Jaffna, March, 1861, in which appointment he was confirmed in 1862; district judge, Kandy, 1865.

GIBSON, W. C.—Appointed to the Ceylon civil service, Sept. 1832; attached to the colonial secretary's office, 1833; also in that year assistant collector of customs, Negombo, and assistant government agent, Colombo; was appointed in 1838 commissioner of loan board, auditor of the supreme court, and chairman of the savings' bank; acting district judge of Colombo, Nov. 1839; assistant colonial secretary, &c., 1840; acting government agent, western province, 1841; resumed duties as assistant colonial secretary, 1843; acting auditor-general, 1846; resumed duties as assistant colonial secretary, 1847; acting government agent, Western province, same year; resumed duties as assistant-colonial secretary, 1848; acting auditor-general, 1849; acting colonial secretary, 1850; auditor-general, 1851; acting colonial secretary, 1855; resumed duties as auditor-general; acting colonial secretary, 1859, in which office he was confirmed, Aug. 1860.

GIBSON, W. F.—Appointed in 1842 junior clerk in the office of Public Works, Ireland; promoted senior clerk in 1848, and chief clerk in 1852. His office having been abolished in 1857, he was placed on the redundant list, 1862; served in 1862 under the census commission for England, and subsequently was transferred to the treasury, from which in 1863 he was appointed registrar and accountant to the Royal Mint at Sydney, New South Wales.

raw_text: GIDDY, T. H.—Acting clerk to resident magistrate, Queen's Town, Cape Colony, July, 1854; temporary clerk in civil commissioner's office, Queen's Town, April, 1855; clerk to resident magistrate, King William's Town, British Kaffraria, 1855; acting clerk of the peace, Nov. 1857; master of the supreme court, and registrar of deeds, 1862.
GILBERT, ALEX. J. M.—Assistant receiver-general and commissioner of customs at Bermuda; appointed 1854. Was revenue officer at Ireland island, 1848 to 1852, and at Hamilton from 1852 to 1854.

raw_text: GILBERT, J. T.—Was solicitor-general of British Guiana; appointed 1856; retired 1863.

raw_text: GILL, WM. HENRY.—Clerk, first class, audit office, Tasmania, July 1, 1853.

events.position: appointed surgeon superintendent of quarantine at Flat Island, Mauritius, Oct. 1
events.text_span: appointed surgeon superintendent of quarantine at Flat Island, Mauritius, Oct. 1, 1862.

raw_text: GILLESPIE, JOHN W.—Late assistant surgeon H. M. 5th Fusileers; appointed surgeon superintendent of quarantine at Flat Island, Mauritius, Oct. 1, 1862.

GILLMAN, H. W.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin, where as undergraduate he obtained science honours (chiefly 1st class), seven times, with simultaneous classical honours (2nd class) five times; appointed an exhibitioner, 1852; graduated gold medallist and moderator in mathematics and physics, 1853; and obtained Bishop Law's mathematical prize, 1854.—Was appointed a writer to the Ceylon government, Sept. 1856; assistant government agent, Jaffna, 1858; assistant government agent, Colombo, 1862; acting landing surveyor; acting deputy collector of customs, Colombo, same year; acting commissioner of requests, Colombo, 1863; confirmed in that appointment May, 1865.
GILSON, A. A.—Second clerk, colonial secretary's office, Natal.

GLADSTONE, Privy Councillor (Gt. Brit.). Appointed 1841.—RIGHT HON. WILLIAM EWART GLADSTONE, M.P.—Was educated at Eton and at Christ Church, Oxford, where he attained a double first class in 1831; graduated M.A. 1834; and received the honorary degree of D.C.L. in 1848; was a lord of the treasury in Dec. 1834; under-secretary for the colonies from Jan. to April, 1835; vice-president of the board of trade, and master of the mint, from Sept. 1841 to May, 1843, when he became president of the board of trade, retaining the office of master of the mint; resigned both Feb. 1845; was secretary of state for the colonies from Dec. 1845 to July, 1846; chancellor of the exchequer from Jan. 1853 to Feb. 1855; lord high commissioner extraordinary to the Ionian Islands, Nov. 1858; reappointed chancellor of the exchequer, June, 1859; is author of "The State in its relations with the Church," "Church Principles considered in their Results," and other works; was M.P. for Newark from 1832 to Jan. 1846, and has sat for the university of Oxford since 1847; defeated 1865, and returned for South Lancashire.

GLEN, THOMAS.—Receiver-general, Newfoundland; appointed 1855. Is a member of the executive council and house of assembly; came in under responsible government in 1855, and went out of office by change of ministry in 1861.

GLENIE, REV. S. O.. M.A., F.L.S.—Classical prizeman, 1830, and elected foundation scholar, 1830, of St. Peter's College, Cambridge; appointed colonial chaplain of St. Paul's, Colombo, Oct. 30, 1834; colonial chaplain, Trincomalie, June 1, 1840; colonial chaplain, Kandy, May 1, 1843; returned, at his own request, to Trincomalee, Feb. 1, 1844; acting colonial chaplain, Kandy, 1848; acting senior colonial chaplain, Colombo, 1848; resumed duties at Trincomalee, Oct. 1, 1851.

events.text_span: Lieut.-Governor of Lagos, 1863

events.position: appointed to be colonial secretary at Lagos
events.text_span: appointed to be colonial secretary at Lagos, April, 1864.

raw_text: GLOVER, Commander, R.N.—Lieut.-Governor of Lagos, 1863; acting governor, owing to death of Mr. Freeman; appointed to be colonial secretary at Lagos, April, 1864.

GOOD, CHARLES, graduated at Exeter College, Oxford, B.A., 1856.—Entered colonial service as chief clerk in office of colonial secretary of British Columbia, April, 1859; acted as private secretary to governor Douglas during 1859, 1860, 1861; was appointed clerk to the legislative council 19th June, 1864, the duties of which office he performs in conjunction with those of chief clerk in the colonial secretary's department.

events.position: Called to the bar at the Inner Temple
events.text_span: Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, Nov. 1855

raw_text: GOODLAKE, EDW. WALLACE.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, Nov. 1855; was special pleader, Oxford circuit; appointed chief magistrate and member of council, Falkland Islands, 1861.

events.position: Appointed chief medical officer at Mauritius
events.text_span: Appointed chief medical officer at Mauritius, July, 1860

raw_text: GORDON, ARCHIBALD, M.D., C.B.—Appointed chief medical officer at Mauritius, July, 1860; is a deputy inspector-general of Her Majesty's hospitals.

GORDON, HON. ARTHUR, C.M.G.—Was assistant private secretary to the Earl of Aberdeen when secretary of state for foreign affairs, between September 1841 and July 1846; and also when first lord of the treasury. Was M.P. for Beverley from July 29, 1854, till March, 1857; accompanied Mr. Gladstone on his special mission to the Ionian islands, as lord-high-commissioner extraordinary, in November, 1858; was appointed a companion of the order of St. Michael and St. George in 1859, captain commandant of the 1st Aberdeenshire rifle volunteers, Feb. 25, 1860, and appointed lieut.-governor of New Brunswick, Oct. 1861.

events.text_span: appointed March, 1845.

raw_text: GORDON, J. G.—Provost-marshal, and marshal in chancery and ordinary, Virgin Islands; appointed March, 1845.
GORDON, ROBERT.—Auditor-general, Dominica; appointed 1860. Was previously a revenue officer in the treasury department of that island.
GORDON, ROBERT.—Coroner, Tobago; is also a member of the privy and legislative councils of that island.

GORDON, THOMAS ROBERT.—Sub-comptroller of customs and rum duties of British Guiana; performs also the duties of sub-book-keeper in the county of Burbice; is also sub-administrator for that county; was appointed sub-registrar of population and property in 1856; held the office of sub-commissary of taxation from 1858 to 1863; clerk to the auditor-general of the colony from 1863 to 1865, during which period he held the office of secretary to the board of prisons.

GORE, AUGUSTUS FREDERICK.—Acting private secretary to Sir A. Oliphant, C.B., chief justice of Ceylon, 1850; private secretary to Christopher Temple, second puisne judge of Ceylon, 1851; private secretary to P. E. Wodehouse, superintendent of British Honduras, August, 1851; acting colonial secretary of British Honduras, 1852, and returning officer (sheriff) of the Bay Islands, 1853; private secretary to Governor Wodehouse, C.B., of British Guiana, 1854; assistant government secretary of the colony in 1854 and 1857; acting stipendiary magistrate in 1856; acting circuit stipendiary magistrate in 1854 and 1857; appointed assistant government secretary of the colony in 1857; acted as government secretary in June, 1865; appointed an inspector of prisons, July, 1865. Militia: captain in 1856; present rank, lieut.-colonel; aide-de-camp to Governor Hincks.

GORMAN, W. J.—Captain, late of the Ceylon Rifles; appointed assistant commissary-general, Ceylon, 1858; acting deputy commissary-general, 1863, resumed duties 1864; acting deputy commissioner general, 1866.

GRAHAM, CYRIL CLERKE.—Was honorarily attached to Lord Dufferin, British commissioner in Syria, from September 10, 1860, till June, 1861; private secretary to the Earl of Carnarvon, secretary of state for the colonies, 6th July, 1866.

raw_text: GRAHAM, R.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Albany division, Cape Colony; appointed Sept. 1852.

GRAHAM, THOS.—Crown commissioner, colonial surveyor and civil engineer, Turks Islands; appointed 1853; appointed treasurer of Honduras, 1863; is ex officio member of the executive council; is commandant of the Belize volunteers; acting colonial secretary, June, 1866, was performing the duties of that office from October, 1863, to March, 1864. GRAHAM'S-TOWN (CAPE OF GOOD HOPE), 2nd BISHOP OF. Founded 1853. RIGHT REV. H. CUTTERELL, D.D.—Educated at St. John's Coll. Cambridge, of which he was elected a fellow; was Bell's scholar, senior wrangler, first Smith's prize-man, and first class in classics; ordained deacon 1835, priest 1836; appointed chaplain to the East India Company at Madras, 1836; principal of Brighton College, 1851; consecrated to this see 1856; author of the "Seven Ages of the Church." This diocese (originally part of the see of Cape Town) comprises the eastern districts of the Colony of the Cape of Good Hope, together with the districts south of the Orange, and east of the Ongar Rivers, and also the territory of British Kaffraria.

GRANT, JAMES MEYER.—Entered the colonial service as crown and colony surveyor, St. Vincent, 1848; appointed clerk in the treasury of that island, 1849, and treasurer, 1854; was a member of the legislative council, 1854, which he resigned at the instance of the government to serve in the assembly; was speaker of the assembly, 1859-60; was an executive councillor, 1856, and assistant justice of the supreme court of judicature, 1860; appointed colonial secretary and administrator of the government of St. Lucia, 1862.
GRANT, JOHN.—Keeper of the common gaol, Tobago.

GRANT, JOHN GLASGOW.—Master in chancery, commissioner of probates, and secretary and actuary of savings' bank, Barbados. Was for fifteen years a member of the local legislature and the several boards and standing committees connected therewith.

GREY, 3rd EARL, (Creat. 1806); VISCt. HOWICK, 1801; BARON GREY, 1806 (Utd. Kgdms.); BART., 1746 (Gt. Brit.); Privy Councillor, 1835.—HENRY GEORGE GREY.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge (M.A. 1823); was under-secretary of state for the colonies from 1830 to 1833; under-secretary for the home department from Jan. till July, 1834; secretary at war from April, 1835 to Sept. 1839; secretary of state for the colonies from July, 1846 to Feb. 1852; appointed lord-lieut. of Northumberland, 1847; was M.P. for Winchelsea from 1826 to 1830, for Higham Ferrars in 1831, for North Northumberland from 1831 to 1841, and for Sunderland from Sept. 1841 till July, 1845.

GREY, SIR GEORGE, K.C.B. (Civil.) 1848.—Entered the army as ensign in the 83rd regiment, 18; promoted to be lieut., 1833; and captain, 1839; left England in 1837 with instructions from government to explore the north-west part of Australia, the account of which will be found in a work published by him, entitled "Journals of Two Expeditions of Discovery in North-west and Western Australia, during 1837-8-9;" was for some time resident magistrate at Albany, West Australia; appointed governor of South Australia, Dec. 1840; governor of New Zealand, 1846; governor of the Cape of Good Hope, 1854; and specially reappointed governor of New Zealand, June, 1861; is author of "Polynesian Mythology."

GREY, 2nd BART. (Utd. Kgdms. Creat. 1814); Privy Councillor, 1839; G.C.B. (Civil) 1849.—RIGHT HON. SIR GEORGE GREY.—Educated at Oriel College, Oxford, where he was 1st class in classics, 1821; and graduated M.A. 1824; was called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1826; was under-secretary for the colonies from July to Nov. 1834, and from April, 1835 till 1839; was judge-advocate-general from 1839 to June, 1841; chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster from June to Sept. 1841; secretary of state for the home department from July, 1846, to Feb. 1852; secretary of state for the colonies from June, 1854, to Feb. 1855; again home secretary from March, 1855, to Feb. 1858; reappointed chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster, June, 1859; and again secretary of state for the home department, 1861; was M.P. for Devonport from 1832 to 1847; for Northumberland North from 1847 to 1852, and has sat for Morpeth since January, 1853.

GRIMBLE, LIEUT. GEORGE S.—6th Royal regiment. Appointed October, 1866, aide-de-camp to Sir John P. Grant, K.C.B., Governor of Jamaica.

GRINLINTON, J. J.—Was nine years on the ordnance survey of England; gazetted ensign in the 65th regiment; served in the Crimean campaign of 1865-6, as lieutenant in the 4th King's Own regiment, and assistant engineer during the siege of Sebastopol, and was engaged making a military survey of the Allied positions; was mentioned in the dispatches—received the 5th class of the Order of Medjide, the Crimean medal and clasp, and the Turkish medal. Appointed assistant surveyor in Ceylon, on 3rd Sept. 1857, and his appointment confirmed on 9th October, 1858, on retiring from the Army by the sale of his commission.

HARDING, W.—Appointed Clerk to first puisne judge at the Cape in 1829; registrar of circuit courts, 1830; secretary to the land board, 1833; in 1836, appointed to prepare a précis from the records in the supreme court, of all criminal trials since the year 1736, to give an insight into the Roman Dutch Law as it existed; in 1837-38 collected and published the laws in force in the Cape Colony; in 1838, appointed clerk of the peace, Swellendam; 1845, crown prosecutor at Natal; 1846, resident magistrate in Natal, and joint auditor of the public accounts; 1847, chief commandant, Burgher forces in Natal; 1848, established and commanded, as lieut.-colonel, the volunteer yeomanry cavalry corps; same year, sent on special mission to emigrant Boers; 1850, on special duty to the Amaponda chief, Faku; 1853-4, acting colonial secretary; 1856, recorder of Natal; 1858, chief justice of that colony and member of the executive council, and judge vice admiralty court.

HART, DANIEL.—Assistant clerk, commissariat department, Trinidad, 1825; ensign in the Royal Trinidad battalion militia forces, 4th Oct. 1831; adjoint-commandant of the districts of Santa Cruz, 1832; commandant of the districts of Las Cuevas and Marascas, 1833; lieutenant of St. Joseph's light infantry regiment of militia, 20th Nov. 1833; special magistrate for the abolition of slavery, 22nd July, 1834; provost-marshal general militia forces, 14th March, 1835; captain in the St. Joseph's light infantry regiment of militia, 31st May, 1837; sent by the governor of the island (Sir Geo. Hill) special commissioner to Venezuela, to claim indemnification for a drogher belonging to the island that had been captured by a Venezuelan cruiser, as also to enforce the release of two British subjects, 1837; commissioner of roads and bridges, 1842; governor of the Royal Gaol, 10th June, 1847; sent to Antigua to inspect and report on, prisons of that island, 10th April, 1857; sent to Barbados, to confer with the governor-general of that island under a dispatch from the secretary of state for the colonies (H. Labouchere, esq.), to Governor Hincks, in regard to the transfer of convicts of Barbados to Trinidad, 25th Oct. 1857; superintendent of prisons, 1st Feb. 1858; acted as inspector of police and inspector of weights and measures, from 22nd Oct. 1858 to 5th April, 1859; sanitary inspector, 1862; again acted as inspector of police, from the 8th Feb. 1862 to the 22nd Aug. 1863, when he resumed his duties as superintendent of prisons and sanitary inspector; sanitary inspector under local board of health, Nov., 1865; is author of the "History of Trinidad."

events.position: Appointed assistant clerk of the peace for the Western County of St. George
events.text_span: Appointed assistant clerk of the peace for the Western County of St. George, Trinidad, 10th March, 1866

raw_text: HART, D. MCDONNELL.—Appointed assistant clerk of the peace for the Western County of St. George, Trinidad, 10th March, 1866; was previously supernumerary in the office of keeper of maps and surveys, and in the immigration office.

HART, NATHANIEL.—Entered the public service at Trinidad, April 1, 1813, as assistant clerk in the ordnance department; was appointed acting clerk in 1818, and continued in that appointment until the reduction of the department in June, 1820, when he was appointed by commissary-general Turquand, as the clerk in the commissariat department; he was ordered from Trinidad to St. Lucia, where he served from July 1820, till June, 1822; in Jan. 1821, while at St. Lucia, he was appointed by General Mainwaring, the then governor, to be registrar of the vice-admiralty court, which office he was obliged to relinquish in June, 1822, when he was ordered back to Trinidad. He again served at Trinidad from June 1822 till Sept. 1825, when he was ordered to Barbados by Commissary-General Routh; he served in Barbados from Sept. 1825 to June 1826, when he was ordered to Tobago; he served at Tobago till Feb. 1827, when he was again ordered back to Trinidad, and served in that island from Feb. 1827 till Oct. 1828, when he was appointed by Colonel Farquharson the then acting governor, to be governor for the gaol and attorney for prisoners; he continued in this office till 1835, when he was appointed by Lord Glenelg, secretary of state for the colonies, to be provost-marshal of St. Christopher, in which office he continued till Jan. 1, 1850, when he was appointed by governor Mackintosh to be treasurer of St. Christopher (since confirmed by Her Majesty), which office he now holds; in 1855 he was appointed controller of customs, registrar of shipping, and shipping-master; in 1856, inspector of prisons; and in 1859, a member of Her Majesty's legislative council, by Sir Hercules Robinson, governor of St. Christopher, in recognition of his long services to the public.

raw_text: HARVEY, SETH.—Solicitor-general of Bermuda; appointed 1856; is a member of the house of assembly.

HARVEY, THOS. C.—M.A., Univ. Pens., 1837; articled pupil of W. Gravatt, C. E., London, 1837; civil engineer and surveyor on the Great Western Railway, 1840; on the improvement and navigation of the river Avon, 1841; also on the Sheffield and Doncaster railways, 1841; civil engineer on the Mons and Menage railway, Belgium, 1846; civil engineer at Honduras, 1853; assistant surveyor-general, Bahamas, 1854; civil engineer and surveyor, 1839; inspector of imperial lighthouses, 1857 and 1861; member of executive council, 1860.

HAVILAND, THOMAS H.—Clerk in cheque office of Navy Victualling Yard, Chatham, 1813; provost-marshal, Prince Edward Island, by warrant from Prince Regent, 1815; naval officer, 1818; member of executive and legislative councils, 1823; assistant judge of supreme court, 1824; colonial treasurer, 1830; marshal and serjeant at mace vice-admiralty court, 1835; president of legislative council, 1839; colonial secretary and clerk of executive and legislative councils, 1839; resigned these offices on introduction of responsible government, 1851; elected mayor of Charlottetown, 1857, and has been annually re-elected to that office.

HAW, C.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate at Caledon division of the Cape of Good Hope; appointed July, 1859; was clerk of the peace in the division of Albany, 1836 to 1842; clerk to the civil commissioner, Somerset, 1842 to 1850; clerk of the peace for the Victoria district, Sept. 1850 to 1856; civil commissioner for Cradock, Feb. 1856 to date of present appointment.

CESAR.—Entered the army as ensign in the 1st royals, Feb. 1836, and retired after 12 years service; appointed resident magistrate of the Weenen, in colony of Natal, Sept. 1852, and removed to resident magistracy of the Upper Umcomas division of that colony, Feb. 1855; was a major in a yeomanry cavalry regiment of volunteers called the Natal carbiniers.

HEAD, 8th BART. (Eng.; Creat. 1676); Privy Councillor, 1857.— RIGHT HON. SIR EDMUND WALKER HEAD.—Succeeded his father in 1838; educated at Oriel College, Oxford, where he was 1st class in classics, 1827; became a fellow of Merton, and graduated M.A. 1830; was university examiner in 1834; appointed one of the poor-law commissioners in Dec. 1841, having previously been an assistant commissioner; was lieut.-governor of New Brunswick from Oct. 1847 to Sept. 1854, when he was appointed governor-general of Canada; which government he held until Oct. 1861. Is author of "The Handbook of Spanish Painting." Was appointed in 1863, to be one of Her Majesty's civil service commissioners in England; is Governor of the Hudson's Bay Company.

Was magistrate's clerk for Victoria district, July 1848 to 1854; clerk to the civil commissioner at Albert, Aug. 1854; clerk to civil commissioner, Victoria, East, April 1856; and acting civil commissioner, 1857.

WALCOTT.—Entered the royal engineers, 1838; promoted to be lieut. 1841; captain, 1847; and major, Oct. 1858; Lieut.-Col. 1861; was appointed comptroller of convicts in Western Australia, from 1850 to 1862; appointed July, 1863, to succeed Sir Joshua Jebb as superintendent of convict prisons in England.

W. E. Gladstone), 1855; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1858; appointed in 1859 to be the first colonial secretary of Queensland; continues to hold that appointment under responsible government.

F.—Entered the colonial office as a junior clerk in Aug. 1838; served as private secretary to the following under-secretaries of state in succession—Mr. Hawes, Mr. Peel, Earl of Desart, Mr. Ball, Mr. Fortescue; was appointed private secretary to Lord Stanley in Feb. 1858, and to Sir Edward Bulwer Lytton in June of the same year; left the colonial office in November, 1858, upon being nominated by the lord chancellor one of the registrars of the court of bankruptcy; appointed master of the court, Nov. 1863.

raw_text: HILL, SAMUEL HARTLEY.—Entered the colonial secretary's office, Grenada, Feb., 1852; appointed chief clerk provisionally, Dec., 1854, and permanently, June, 1857; secretary to the Board of Education, March, 1864; held the appointments of police magistrate of the western district and coroner of St. Patrick, from March to August, 1865.

HILL, COL. STEPHEN JOHN, C.B.—Entered the army, Nov. 10, 1825; was promoted lieut. Feb. 13, 1828; a captain, April 15, 1842; commanded an expedition 80 miles up the Gambia in 1849; stormed and destroyed the fortified town of Kednung, May 7, and was present at the action and defeat of the enemy on the plains of Queenella; he also commanded a detachment of the 2nd and 3rd West India regiments in the combined attack of a British and French naval and land force under Commodore Fanshawe, in the attack and total defeat of pirates, at the island of Basis, Jeba River, Western Africa, Dec. 12, 1849; received the thanks of the lords of the admiralty and was promoted to the brevet rank of major, Feb. 15, 1850; obtained his majority, Sept. 9, 1851; was appointed governor and commander-in-chief in and over Her Majesty's forces and settlements, and their dependencies, on the Gold Coast, April 1, 1851; was promoted to a lieut.-colonelcy, Feb. 3, 1854; was appointed captain-general and governor-in-chief in and over the colony of Sierra Leone and its dependencies, Nov. 6, 1854; and consul-general in the Sherbro country, on the west coast of Africa, Nov. 11, 1854; which appointment he held till Sept. 1862, when he was appointed governor-in-chief of Antigua, and the Leeward Islands, Nov. 1862; became a colonel, April 6, 1857; exchanged into, and sold out of the 3rd light dragoons (now hussars) in 1860; his commission of colonel was, Oct. 26, 1858, antedated to Nov. 28, 1854, in accordance with the 14th clause of the royal warrant of Oct. 14, 1858; was acting judge in the mixed commission court at Sierra Leone from Aug. 8, 1858 till Jan. 12, 1859; was appointed a C.B. May 18, 1860; appointed sheriff of the colony in 1863.
HILLMAN, ALFRED.—Record keeper, survey department, Western Australia, appointed 1862, to which year from 1830 he served as an assistant surveyor.

raw_text: HINCKS, FRANCIS, C.B. (Creat. 1862.)—Was for several years a prominent member of the Canadian ministry, in which he held the office of inspector-general of accounts; appointed governor-in-chief of Barbados, and the Windward Islands, Oct. 1855; and governor and commander-in-chief, British Guiana, 1861.
HITCHCOCK, W.—Appointed assistant-refiner in the assay-office of British Columbia, Sept. 1857; and since the reduction of the staff in Dec. 1863, has acted as chief melter.

HODGES, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1858).—SIR WILLIAM HODGES was called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1833; practised for some years as a special pleader and went the western circuit; was recorder of Poole; appointed chief justice of the Cape of Good Hope, May 1858. Is also judge of the vice-admiralty court, and president of the legislative council of that colony; was knighted on his appointment as chief justice.

Has received at different times commissions as police magistrate of Belize, acting colonial secretary, and held the dormant appointment of administrator of the government of Honduras; private secretary to the Governor of British Columbia, 1864; and acting commissioner of lands and works.

Light, during his administration of the government of that colony, which he represented at the Paris Exhibition of 1855; was knighted and made a chevalier of the legion of honour 1855; represented British Guiana at the International Exhibition, 1862. Is mayor of Georgetown for the second time.

HORNE, WM. A.—Police magistrate, Grenada; appointed 1853. Is clerk of patents and receiver of church and land revenues, which appointments he has held since 1833; is a member of council.

HORSFORD, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1841); C.B. (Civil) 1852.—SIR ROBERT MARSH HORSFORD, eldest son of the Hon. Paul Horsford, late chief justice of Antigua (who died 1850).—Was educated at Winchester and at Exeter College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1820; called to the bar at the Middle Temple, 1822; was solicitor-general of Antigua from 1825 to 1846; attorney-general from 1846 to Nov. 1847, when he was appointed chief justice; retired from the bench, Aug. 1856; received the honour of knighthood for his services in the West Indies.

HOSEASON, CAPTAIN WILLIAM.—A post captain in the royal navy, on half-pay; appointed superintendent of ports at Malta, Dec. 1856. Is shipping-master under the Merchant Shipping Act of 1854, and ex-officio member of council.

HOULTON, SIR EDWARD VICTOR LEWIS, M.A., K.C.M.G. (Creat. 1859.)—Educated at Oriel and St. John's Colleges, Oxford, of which latter college he was elected fellow; graduated B.A. in 1845, M.A. in 1847; was private secretary to Sir Wm. Molesworth, when president of the board of works in 1853, and when secretary of state for the colonies in 1855; appointed chief secretary to the government of Malta, Sept. 1855. Is ex-officio member of the council of government.
HOUSTON, LEWIS.—Is administrator-general of Berbice; appointed 1845. Was sworn clerk and notary at Berbice, 1831.

events.text_span: Was employed in the colonial bank at Barbados, and appointed treasurer of that colony, 1860.

raw_text: HOWELL, JOHN SIMPSON.—Was employed in the colonial bank at Barbados, and appointed treasurer of that colony, 1860.

HOWSE, ALFRED RICHARD.—Appointed 26th Oct. 1863, clerk in charge of stores, &c., land and works department of British Columbia; a non-commissioned officer in the corps of royal engineers; served as surveyor and clerk in the land office, since May, 1859.

events.position: Attorney-general and premier of Newfoundland since the change of government in

raw_text: HOYLES, H. W.—Attorney-general and premier of Newfoundland since the change of government in 1861; appointed chief justice, 1865.
HUDSON, H.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Somerset division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed May, 1847. Was clerk to the lieut.-governor's secretary, 1838.

HUFFAM, FRED. SOWLEY.—Clerk of the supreme court of Hong Kong, appointed 1859; and registrar of companies, appointed 1865. Entered the colonial service, Hong Kong, as 3rd clerk in the colonial secretary's office, in 1855; appointed judges' clerk in 1856; acting deputy registrar, 1860, 1861, 1862; acting registrar, from Nov. 1861, to May, 1862.

HUGGINS, H. C.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, Jan. 3, 1858; appointed Queen's counsel at Nevis, and solicitor-general of that colony, 1860; and attorney-general of British Honduras, 1863; and was appointed in 1863 stipendiary magistrate at Demerara.

HUGGINS, HORATIO JAMES—Queen's advocate of Sierra Leone from 9 May, 1863, and chairman of commissioners "for the settlement of claims to land" from 1 Sept. 1863. Is member of the executive and legislative councils. Acted as chief justice and judge of the courts of vice-admiralty, insolvency, and divorce and matrimonial causes, from 20 May, 1865, to 10 Mar. 1866, and again, from 12 Sept. 1866; became judge ad interim of the mixed courts of justice under the slave trade treaties, on the death of Mr. Skelton, on 21 May, 1865, until the 19 March, 1866; was sent as chief commissioner, with Major Ireland, of the 3rd West India regiment, to the Moriah Country, in June, 1866, to effect the release of British subjects siezed and imprisoned during the war then raging in that territory between King Boharry and his nephew, Malaghi Baily, and to prevent further injury to life and property; and again as sole commissioner on a second commission to the same country in July, 1865, receiving on both occasions the thanks of the government for the readiness with which he undertook the duties and the "ability he displayed." He also acted as attorney-general in the island of St. Vincent in 1857 and 1858, when a note was made at the colonial office of the strong testimony borne to his able, painstaking, and laborious services. He was educated at a military college in France, and called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn in Hilary Term, 1838.

HUGON, THOMAS JOSEPH.—Was protector of immigration at the Mauritius from 1847 to 1860. Retired on pension, 1860; was formerly in the East India company's service.

raw_text: HUMPHREYS, N.—Clerk of the house of assembly, Antigua; appointed 1810.

HUMPHRYS, WM.—From 20th Aug. 1835, to March 1837, in H.M. customs at Dominica, when he was appointed secretary to the commissioners of compensation; July 1837 to June 1838, private secretary to Governor Light, of Dominica; July 1838 to Jan. 1841, private secretary to Governor Light, British Guiana, and also assistant government secretary; 1843, stipendiary magistrate, and immigration agent general; March 1853, stipendiary magistrate; sheriff of Essequibo, March 1863.

HUNT, CAPTAIN JOHN CALVIN.—Half-pay; joined Austrian army, 1844; served in 5th cuirassiers regiment; served at siege of Vienna, 1848, and campaign in Hungary; was present at all actions during the campaign; captain, 1852; was with the army of observation on the Vistula and Bukovina, in Poland, 1854; received extra certificates and thanks from the Emperor of Austria for good service and bravery; in 1855 proceeded to England, and joined the cavalry of British German Legion; in Nov. 1856 proceeded with German Legion to the Cape of Good Hope, where he was employed until disbanded in 1860; appointed acting deputy-assistant commissary-general at East London, British Kaffraria, 1860; Sept. 1861, appointed acting special magistrate to the Ganobie district, British Kaffraria.

HUNTINGTON, —Inspector of roads and bridges, Mauritius, 1866. HURON (CANADA), 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1857.—RIGHT REV. BENJAMIN CRONYN, D.D.—Educated at Cambridge, and Trinity College, Dublin; was rector of London, Canada West, from 1832 till his consecration in 1857. This diocese, formerly a portion of the see of Toronto, was erected into a bishopric when the diocese of Toronto was reconstituted in 1857. Population, 472,850 souls.

INGLOTT, FERDINANDO V.—Was appointed a teacher of a government primary school at Malta, Nov. 1841; served in the naval department from 1843 to 1855; assistant superintendent of ports, 1858; and appointed comptroller of charitable institutions, Jan. 1859. Is ex-officio member of council.

INGRAM, THOS. LEWIS.—Served as acting clerk of customs; from 28th Aug. 1854 until Sept. 1855; queen's advocate at the Gambia, May. 1863; is a member of the Honourable Society of the Middle Temple; appointed tide surveyor, superintendent of quarantine, and clerk of the courts, on the 10th Feb. 1857; 1st lieutenant R. G. M. A., 9th Dec. 1859; captain of ditto, 22nd July, 1860; served under Colonel D'Arcy during the Badiboo war, in command of the rocket guns on the left, with the skirmishers in advance, at the taking of Kenty-Cunda and Sabba, and assisted at the storming of the latter town, on the 21st Feb. 1861; author of a work on Light Field Gun Drill.

JAMAICA, 2nd BISHOP OF. Founded 1824.—RIGHT REV. AUBREY GEORGE SPENCER, D.D.—Educated at Magdalen Hall, Oxford; was Bishop of Newfoundland from 1839 to 1843, when he was translated to this see; appointed a privy councillor in Jamaica, 1855. The diocese includes Jamaica and the colony of British Honduras.

JANISCH, H. R.—Police magistrate and clerk in colonial secretary's office, St. Helena; date of appointments, 1851 and 1845; was acting Queen's advocate 1856, and acting colonial secretary, 1861; has been in colonial service since 1838.

JAYETILLEKE, F.—Was appointed acting commissioner of requests, and police magistrate at Calpentyn, Ceylon, Jan. 1861, and confirmed in the same year; acting commissioner of requests and police magistrate at Ballepittymodere, 1862; confirmed June, 1864; district judge, Chilan, 1865.

JENNER, GEORGE.—Was for 10 years, from 1848 to 1858, in H.M.'s 4th regt.; was attached to the land transport corps during the Crimean war; appointed inspector of police at the Mauritius, Feb. 1858; and police magistrate at Rodrigues, June, 1862.
JENNINGS, J. H.—Chief clerk, government office, St. Lucia, 1861; formerly stipendiary magistrate, St. Lucia; appointed 1849. Was first appointed clerk in government office, St. Lucia, 1837.

JOHNSTON, ALEXANDER R.—Was in the civil service of the Mauritius from the beginning of 1828 until the end of 1833; he then proceeded with the late Lord Napier to China, being appointed his lordship's private secretary; and, in 1835, he became third commissioner, which office he held until appointed deputy superintendent of the trade of British subjects in China in May, 1837; in the latter capacity he was actively employed during the war in China (for which he received the medal), and in superintending the trade in the South during the absence of the chief superintendent in the North; he also administered the government of Hong Kong for upwards of a year, from the first acquisition of that island to its being finally taken over by the colonial department; since 1843, he filled the office of secretary and registrar to the superintendency of trade in China; and on the abolition of that office, Sept. 25, 1852, he obtained a compensation allowance.
JOHNSTON, H. W.—Assistant clerk in the provincial secretary's office, Nova Scotia.

JOHNSTON, JAMES.—Stipendiary magistrate, St. Lucia; appointed 1849. Was judge of district court of requests, judge of court of appeal, and judge of court of petty sessions; appointed clerk in government office, St. Lucia, 1818; retired on a pension, 1862.
JOHNSTON, J. W.—Attorney-general of Nova Scotia, and member of the executive council and house of assembly.
JOHNSTONE, W.—Police magistrate, northern district, Dominica.

JORDON, E., C.B. (creat., 18th May, 1860).—Was for several years a member of the executive committee, Jamaica, but resigned in 1860; reappointed in April 1863; resigned in Oct. 1864; appointed receiver general in Oct. 1864, and resigned in Aug. 1865, when he was appointed island secretary and governor's secretary.

Stephen for seven years, and subsequently acted in the same capacity to Mr. Merivale for ten years; promoted assistant clerk, Nov. 1858; is also parliamentary clerk of the colonial office.

raw_text: JUMEAUX, ARTHUR.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1865; and acting police magistrate, Pan-tura.

KEATE, ROBERT WM.—Educated at Eton and Christchurch, Oxford; was appointed civil commissioner at the Seychelles Islands, 1849; lieut.-governor of Grenada, 1853; and governor, commander-in-chief, and vice-admiral of Trinidad, from Nov. 1856 to April 1864. Called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1844; governor of Natal, Nov., 1866.

KEENS, JAMES HENRY.—President of the privy council; assistant justice of the court of common pleas; member of the legislative council, 1845; and a member of the executive committee, Tobago; was master in chancery from 1837 to 1853; police magistrate from 1838 to 1856; administered the government of Tobago for ten months in 1856 and 1857.

raw_text: KELLY, WM. FRED. THE REV., M.A.—Was colonial chaplain of Matelle, Ceylon; appointed by the bishop of Colombo to the chaplaincy of Nuwera Ellia, 1864.
KEMBLE, W.—Clerk of the house of assembly at Jamaica; appointed 1854. Was appointed sergeant-at-arms to the assembly, 1844.
KENDRICK, CAPT. J. H.—Superintendent of lighthouses, Nova Scotia.

KENNEDY, ARTHUR E. ESQ., C.B., 1862.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin. Entered the army as ensign in the 11th regiment in 1827; lieutenant 1832; captain 68th light infantry, 1840; retired from the army in 1848 to accept civil employment; was selected (while serving in the army), in 1846 to fill the office of county inspector, under the board of works; and served uninterruptedly during the Irish famine, as inspector under Sir John Burgoyne's relief commissioner; and subsequently as inspector of poor laws, till the office was abolished in 1851; appointed Governor of the "Gambia," and transferred to Sierra Leone in 1852; consul-general of the adjoining Sherbro Country, in 1854; and removed in the same year to the government of Western Australia; administered the latter government without interruption till February, 1862; created companion of the Bath in 1862; and appointed governor of Vancouver's Island, November, 1863.

raw_text: KEON, MILES GERALD.—Appointed colonial secretary of Bermuda, March, 1859; and member of council in that colony, August, 1859; is corresponding member of the literary and scientific society of Abbeville in France; author of several educational and historical works; was for some years connected with the Daily Press and Quarterly Reviews in London, in their leading and literary departments.

KER, ALAN.—Called to the bar at the Middle Temple, Nov. 1842; chief justice of Nevis, 1854; appointed chief justice of Dominica, 1856; and assistant judge of the supreme court of Jamaica, 1861; acted as attorney-general of Antigua, from August, 1853, to March, 1854.

KING, FRANCIS O.—First assistant—sworn clerk, Registrar's Office, Demerara.

KILLIKELLY, WM. EDW.—Educated for the army at the Royal Military College, Sandhurst. Was private secretary and aide-de-camp to his father during his administration of the government of St. Kitt's in 1833; entered the service of the imperial customs at Demerara in 1835; promoted in same department to St. Kitt's in 1836; where he continued to serve until 1844, when he was promoted to be comptroller and landing surveyor at Grenada; specially appointed to be acting collector at Grenada in 1845, and comptroller of navigation laws in 1849; appointed comptroller of colonial customs in 1855; was private secretary to Lieut.-governor Kortwright from June 1859 to Jan. 1864; holds the same appointment under Lt.-governor Mundy; is a justice of the peace; a member of assembly; aide-de-camp to the lieut.-governor; appointed police magistrate of the northern district, Grenada, Aug. 1865.

raw_text: KINDER, THOS. WM.—Established the railway works at Bromsgrove and Oldbury in 1845; subsequently conducted locomotive department, and working of Shrewsbury and Birmingham railway; had the management of the locomotive department of the Midland Great Western Railway of Ireland, under contract, from 1851 to 1855; ensign Worcester militia, 1840; Lieutenant, 1846; Captain, 1853; on disembodiment of the Worcestershire regiment, was appointed captain in the third West York militia, 1859; elected associate of institution of civil engineers, 1860; appointed master of the Mint, Hong-Kong, Nov. 1863.
KING, Æ. A.—Appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, 1865, and in the same year acting police magistrate of Upper and Lower Doombera; acting assistant government agent, Kandy, 1866.

KING, J. HAMPDEN.—Graduated at Oxford; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1843; was a member of the house of assembly in Barbados from 1847 to 1853, and again in 1862; appointed a judge of the court of appeal at Barbados, 1858; acted as colonial secretary from April, 1860, to March, 1862; acted as auditor-general from March to May, 1862; appointed to administer the government of St. Kitt's during the absence of Sir B. C. C. Pine, 6th Aug. 1862.

KINGS, H. R. V.—Assistant surveyor-general. —Appointed 1855; was in the department from 1838. KINGSTON (JAMAICA), 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1856.—RIGHT REV. REGINALD COURTENAY, D.D.—Educated at Magdalen Hall, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1835, M.A. 1838; appointed archdeacon of Middlesex in Jamaica in 1853; and coadjutor to the Bishop of Jamaica, under the title of Bishop of Kingston, Feb. 1856; a privy councilor in Jamaica, Sept. 1856.
KIRK, JAMES.—Senior member of the privy council of Tobago.

raw_text: KIRKWALL, VISCOUNT, C. M. G.—Entered the army as an ensign in the 92nd Highlanders, Aug. 8, 1845; lieutenant, Nov. 9, 1846; captain, Nov. 1853; exchanged into the 71st Highland light infantry in 1854; served with that regiment in the trenches before Sebastopol; was at the taking of Kertch and Yanakale; received a medal and clasp for Sebastopol, and Turkish medal was granted March 2, 1858; Her Majesty's royal licence and permission to accept and wear the insignia of the 5th class of the imperial order of the Medjidie, which his Imperial Majesty the Sultan was pleased to confer upon him, as a mark of his Majesty's approbation of his distinguished services before the enemy in the late war. Served as aide-de-camp to Sir Henry Ward, G.C.M.G., then lord high commissioner in the Ionian Islands, from 1851 to 1854; appointed to the Scots fusilier guards in 1856; sold out of the army in 1857; is a deputy-lieutenant of Ayrshire, and a captain in the royal Bucks yeomanry cavalry.

LABORDE, EDWARD.—Colonial secretary, registrar of deeds, clerk of the courts, crown, and council of St. Vincent; appointed 1861. LABUAN, 1st BISHOP OF. Consecrated 1855.—RIGHT REV. FRANCIS THOMAS MACDOUGALL, D.C.L. —Educated for the medical profession at King's College, London, where he received the gold medal for general medical proficiency in 1837; was appointed demonstrator of anatomy there, and elected an associate; was admitted a member of the college of surgeons, 1839, and elected a fellow in 1854; was for some time at Magdalen Hall, Oxford, whence he received the degree of M.A. 1845, and hon. D.C.L. 1854; after having for some time had the management of some mining concerns in South Wales, he entered the church in 1845, and was successively curate of Fromingham, Pigott, and Lakenham, Norwich, and Christchurch, Bloomsbury; chief missionary to Borneo, 1847; commissary of the Bishop of London in Borneo, 1853-4; and 1st Bishop of Labuan, 1855. The diocese consists of the island of Labuan and its dependencies.

LA CAZE, LOUIS.—Attorney-general of St. Lucia; appointed 1850. Is a member of the executive and legislative councils; first appointed solicitor-general in 1842; has acted as puisne judge in the absence on leave of the judge, and also as chief justice on the resignation of Sir R. B. Clarke.

LEFEVRE, K.C.B. (Civil). (Creat. 1857.) SIR JOHN GEORGE SHAW LEFEVRE.—Educated at Eton and Trinity Coll. Camb., where he was senior wrangler, 1818; fellow of Trinity, 1819; graduated M.A. 1821; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1824; elected a bencher, 1855; appointed under-secretary of state for the colonies, 1833; poor-law commissioner, 1834; joint-assistant secretary to the board of trade, 1841; deputy-clerk of the parliaments, 1848; clerk of the parliaments 1856. Is an ecclesiastical commissioner, and vice-chancellor of the university of London. Was a civil service commissioner, but resigned 1862.
LEFROY, A. O'GRADY.—Appointed treasurer of Western Australia, 1856. Was private secretary to Governor Fitzgerald, 1849 to 1855.
LEFROY, H. M.—Superintendent of convicts in Western Australia; appointed 1859. Was deputy superintendent from 1853 to 1859.

LEGH, RICHARD CORNWALL.—Entered the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, May, 1841; appointed assistant secretary at Malta, June, 1851; and auditor-general, June, 1858; is ex-officio a member of council. Acted as chief secretary to the government from July 11 to Oct. 17, 1855, and June 24 to Oct. 6, 1857. Was sent on a special and confidential mission to Sicily with reference to government contracts during the Crimean war.

LE-MARCHANT, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1838); G.C.M.G. 1860; K.C.B. 1865.—SIR JOHN GASPARD LE-MARCHANT.—Entered the army in 1821; became a colonel in 1851, and held local rank as a major-general till fully promoted to that rank in March, 1858; retired from the command of the 85th foot in 1846 on proceeding to Newfoundland; was governor and commander-in-chief of Newfoundland from Feb. 1847 to June, 1852; lieut.-governor of Nova Scotia from June, 1852, to Dec. 1857, when he was appointed governor of Malta, and received local rank of lieut.-general there, 1859; is a brigadier-general in the service of Her Catholic Majesty; a knight of the 1st class and a knight commander of the orders of San Fernando and of Charles III. of Spain; received the honour of knighthood from the Queen, with permission to wear his foreign orders, which were conferred for services in Spain.

LIESCHING, C. F. H. L.—Commissioner of requests, &c., of Ballepittymodere, in the colony of Ceylon, April, 1859; acting assisting agent, Galle, 1862; assistant agent at Jaffra, June, 1864; appointed assistant agent at Galle, May 1865, and acting police magistrate, Gampolla.

LIESCHING, L. F.—Appointed sub-collector and landing waiter at Trincomalie, Ceylon, June, 1855; commissioner of requests, &c., Point Pedro, 1855; acting deputy-fiscal, western province, 1856; acting district judge, &c., Badulla, 1857; resumed duties at Point Pedro, 1859; acting commissioner of requests, and police magistrate, at Jaffna, 1861; assistant government agent, Jaffna, 1862; acting district judge, Matura, 1863; district judge, Tangalle, June, 1864; acted as assistant government agent, Matura, in addition to judicial duties from Nov. 1864 to May 1865, when he resumed his duties at Tangalle.

LINDSAY, J. MAINWARING.—Was acting colonial secretary at the Gold Coast from Oct. 1859 to July, 1860, when he was appointed assistant colonial secretary, which he held until 1863.

LORD, RICHARD H.—Was appointed manager of the 2nd eastern district, Sierra Leone. May, 1858. Is a magistrate, coroner, and commissioner of the court of requests in the rural districts of the colony and of the recently annexed territory of British Quiah. G.—Has been in the colonial service since 1829; major of Berbice militia, 22nd July 1835; justice of the peace, 28th June 1836; acting sheriff of Berbice, 26th Sept. 1839; was emigration agent at Berbice from 1841 to 1846; financial accountant from 1846 to 1850; and receiver-general of British Guiana, 1850. Retired on pension, 1862.

raw_text: LYTTELTON, 4th BARON (Great Britain, Creat. 1794); BARON WESTCOTE, 1776 (Ireland); BARR., 1618 (England).—GEORGE WILLIAM LYTTELTON.—Was educated at Trinity College, Cambridge; bracketed senior classic, and chancellor's medallist in 1838; was under-secretary of state for the colonies from Feb. to July. 1846; is lord-lieut. and custos rotulorum of Worcestershire, and high steward of Bewdley; unsuccessfully contested the election for high steward of the University of Cambridge in 1840; was appointed principal of Queen's College, Birmingham, in 1845.

LYTTON, 1st BART. (United Kingdom, Creat. 1838); Privy Councillor. 1858.—RIGHT HON. SIR EDWARD GEORGE EARLE LYTTON BULWER-LYTTON.—Educated at Trinity Hall, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1826, M.A. 1835; was M.P. for St. Ives in 1831, and for Lincoln from 1832 to 1841; has sat for Herts since July, 1852; appointed a deputy-lieut. of Herts, 1851; received the hon. degree of D.C.L. from Oxford in 1853; elected rector of the University of Glasgow, Nov. 1856 and Nov. 1858; his literary works are well known and very numerous; was secretary of state for the colonies from May, 1858, to June, 1859.

LYVEDEN, 1st BARON. (Creat. 1859.) Privy Councillor.—ROBERT VERNON.—Educated at Christ Church, Oxford, where he was 2nd class in classics, 1822; was a lord of the treasury from Nov. 1830 to Nov. 1834; was secretary to the board of control from 1835 to 1839; and under-secretary for the colonies from 1839 to 1841; secretary at war for a few weeks in Feb. 1852; president of the board of control from March, 1855, to Feb. 1858; sat for Tralee from 1829 to 1831, and for Northampton from 1831 to 1859.

MACARTNEY, W.—Served in the Irish constabulary; was appointed superintendent of police, Colombo, Ceylon, 1848; chief superintendent of police, Ceylon, 1858. Retired 1865.

M'CORMACK, JOHN.—Appointed police magistrate at Sierra Leone, 1855. Is registrar of insolvent debtors' court, and coroner for a district of the colony; was sheriff and provost-marshal, 1853; superintendent of lighthouses, 1852.
M'COY, WM. H.—Colonial secretary and clerk of the council, Dominica; appointed 1849. Is also registrar, clerk of the Crown, and clerk of enrolments.

MACDONALD, R. J. SOMERLED.—Appointed, after a competitive examination, a clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, June, 1859. Promoted to the 1st junior class, May 2, 1864.

MAC-DONNELL, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1855); C.B. (Civil) 1852.—SIR RICHARD GRAVES MAC-DONNELL.—Entered Trinity College, 1830; gained several honours both in classics and science when an undergraduate; obtained a scholarship in 1833; graduated B.A. 1835, M.A. 1838, hon. LL.D. 1844; called to the bar in Ireland, 1838, and at Lincoln's Inn in England, 1840; was chief justice of the Gambia from 1843 to 1847; governor of that colony from 1847 to 1851; conducted several exploring expeditions up the Gambia, and in the interior of Africa between that river and the Senegal; appointed governor of St. Lucia, 1852, and transferred to St. Vincent same year; appointed captain-general and governor-in-chief of South Australia, 1855, which government he held until March, 1862; appointed governor of Nova Scotia, April 1864; appointed governor of Hong Kong, 14th Oct. 1865.

events.text_span: Colonial engineer and surveyor, Tobago, 1853

raw_text: MAC EACHINE, R.W.—Colonial engineer and surveyor, Tobago, 1853; is a member of the executive council.
MACFARLANE, J.—Appointed resident magistrate, county of Weenen, Natal, 1855. Is captain commanding the Weenen Yeoman Cavalry.

MACKGLASHAN, JOHN CHAS.—Clerk of the privy council of Jamaica; is also chief clerk in the office of the financial secretary; was appointed clerk in the executive commission office, in June, 1856, by Sir Charles Darling, K.C.B.; was, in April, 1866, on the death of the secretary of the executive committee and clerk privy council, appointed by Sir H. K. Storks, G.C.B., G.C.M.G., provisionally to these offices, pending the change in the constitution of the island, under which it became a crown colony; held these appointments until the inauguration of the new form of government; was appointed to his present offices by Sir J. P. Grant, K.C.B., on 16th September, 1866; is ensign in the Jamaica militia and 1st lieutenant in the volunteer force of the island.

MACKENZIE, JAMES GEORGE.—Is a captain of the Royal Navy on half-pay. Served as midshipman in H.M.S. sloop, 'Philomel,' at the battle of Navarino, and was frequently engaged in her boats against pirates in the Grecian Archipelago, for which services he was promoted to the rank of lieutenant in 1830, on passing his examination. Was flag-lieutenant to Admiral Sir Thomas Briggs, G.C.M.G. at Malta from 1834 to 1838; first lieutenant of H.M.S. 'Indus,' under captain Sir James Stirling, Knt., in the Mediterranean from 1840 to 1844; was commander of H.M.S. 'Caledonia,' in the squadron of evolution under Admiral Sir Charles Napier, 1847; in command of the Cornwall and Somerset coast-guard districts, 1848 to 1853; subsequently served in H.M.S. 'Princess Royal,' attached to the Baltic Fleet, in the war with Russia; appointed Governor of the Falkland Islands, Sept. 1862; Lieut.-Gov. St. Kitts, Nov. 1866.

raw_text: MACLEAN, JOHN, C.B. (Civil.)—Was appointed agent at Fort Peddie, Cape of Good Hope, Nov. 1845; commissioner for the T'Sambie tribe of Kaffirs, Jan. 1847; chief commissioner for British Kaffraria, Sept. 1852, and appointed lieut.-governor of those territories upon their erection into a separate colony, Dec. 1860; appointed lieut.-governor of Natal, April 6, 1864.

M'LEOD, GEO. M.—Chief clerk in government office, St. Lucia; appointed 1857. Has been assistant clerk in same office since 1844; is secretary to the board of education; is stipendiary magistrate, and immigration agent, 1861; is presiding justice of the court of requests, inspector of prisons, coroner, &c., &c.
MACLEOD, JOHN.—Stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana; appointed 1834. Retired.

McNAB, J. B. D.—Clerk to the chairman of the board of works, Nova Scotia.
MACNAB, JAMES.—Receiver-general of Nova Scotia, and member of executive council.
M'NULTY, WM., LL.D.—Police magistrate of George Town, British Guiana; appointed 1840. Retired.
MACPHAIL, W. B. U.—Collector of import revenue, Nevis; and member of the legislative council.

MACREADY, W. C.—Educated at Westminster and Christ Church, Oxford; appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, Nov. 1854; extra assistant government agent and deputy fiscal, Kandy, 1855; acting assistant government agent Pultam, 1856; assistant agent at Kurnegalle, July 1858; acting assistant government agent, Colombo 1859; acting assistant government agent, Matura 1860; assistant government agent at Colombo Nov. 1860; district judge, &c., Chilaw, May, 1862 acting assistant agent and acting district judge, Putlam, 1863; resumed his duties at Chilaw, Nov. 1863; acting assistant government agent, Matura, 1st May, appointment confirmed, Dec., 1865; and appointed acting assistant government agent, &c., Putlam.

M'SWINEY, JOHN.—Sheriff of Berbice and stipendiary magistrate of the New Amsterdam judicial district, in British Guiana. Appointed at the close of 1835, by Lord Glenelg, government agent for establishing normal schools in the West Indies for the training of native teachers, and proceeded to the Bahamas; accepted this appointment on the same footing as a stipendiary magistrate, under the order in council as to emoluments; services transferred in 1838 to the trustees of the Mico charity on the same terms and was their agent, as well as the general superintendent of their schools in the Bahamas until 1839—in British Guiana until 1841—in Jamaica until 1842—and in the Windward Islands until 1843. The Parliamentary grant for education in the West Indies having been discontinued, he was required to discharge all the teachers and close all the country schools in Jamaica, most of which, however, he succeeded in transferring to other societies interested in the promotion of negro education, for the performance of which duty he received the approval of the trustees of the charity. In 1843 he performed a similar duty in Trinidad. From 1843 to 1848 was proprietor and editor of the 'Trinidad Standard' newspaper; was senior member of the town council of the port of Spain in 1848; appointed that year by Lord Grey inspector of schools in British Guiana, and in 1849 a stipendiary magistrate of British Guiana; was a commissioner of education in 1850; and in 1863 was appointed sheriff of Berbice.

MANNING, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1858.)—SIR WILLIAM MONTAGU MANNING, L.L.D.—Educated at Tavistock, at Southampton, and at University College, London; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1832; was chairman of quarter sessions in New South Wales from Oct. 1837 to Oct. 1844; solicitor-general there from Oct. 1844 to Jan. 1848; acting judge of the supreme court there from Jan. 1848 to Nov. 1849; again solicitor-general there from Nov. 1849 to May, 1856; attorney-general from May, 1856, to May, 1858; is a member of the executive council of New South Wales, and one of the Queen's counsel there; author of "Notes of Proceedings in Electoral Revision Courts," "Nevile and Manning's Reports in Court of King's Bench," 1832 to 1837.
MANNING, F. A.—Appointed superintendent of the melting department in the Hong Kong mint.

MANTELL, J. J.—Appointed chief justice at the Gambia settlement, Oct. 1847; is a barrister-at-law; was Queen's advocate, 1841 to 1847; on several occasions performed the duties of colonial secretary, and in 1844 administered the government of the settlement.
MAPLETON, H.—Was judge of the summary and police court, St. Helena; appointed 1847. Was formerly in the royal navy.

MARA, RICHARD WESTON, L.L.D.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; gained honours in science and classics; graduated B.A. in 1836, M.A. in 1839; LLD. in 1863; called to the Irish bar in 1840; appointed counsel to the attorney-general of Ireland in 1845; appointed attorney-general and judge advocate of Antigua in 1859; advocate-general and Queen's proctor, 1862; member of the executive council, and member of the house of assembly; is also Queen's counsel of Montserrat; acted as chief justice of Antigua in the absence of Sir W. Snagg in 1863-4.

MATHEW, GEORGE BENVENUTO BYAM.—Was appointed an ensign in the 52nd light infantry, July 7, 1825; was promoted to be lieutenant, unattached, Sept. 19, 1826; was appointed to the rifle brigade, Sept. 20, 1826; exchanged into the 70th foot, Nov. 26, 1828; was promoted to be captain, April 19, 1831; was appointed a lieutenant and captain in the Coldstream guards, July 26, 1833; captain in the 85th foot, June 17, 1836; captain, unattached, Sept. 23, 1836; and retired from the army April 9, 1841, as lieutenant and captain of the grenadier guards; he served in America and the Mediterranean; was returned M.P. for Athlone in 1835; and represented Shaftesbury from 1837 till 1841; was appointed governor and commander-in-chief in and over the Bahama Islands, March 26, 1844; consul at Charleston, in the United States of America, March 8, 1850; was consul at Philadelphia, from July 5, 1853, till May 28, 1856; when his exequatur was revoked by the president of the United States; was appointed consul-general for the Russian ports in the Black Sea, Oct. 31, 1856; and consul-general for the Russian ports in the Black Sea and sea of Azoff, to reside at Odessa, Sept. 14, 1857; and secretary to the legation in Mexico, July 24, 1858; was chargé d'affaires from Sept. 20, 1859, till May 25, 1861, when he was appointed chargé d'affaires and consul-general to the republics of Guatemala, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, Honduras, and Salvador; was promoted to be minister plenipotentiary to the republics in Central America, Aug. 21, 1861.

MAUNSELL, D. C.—Private secretary to Governor Seymour, British Columbia, appointed June 1864; private secretary to the Governor of Vancouver's Island, Jan. 1864; entered the Home Civil Service, Nov. 1853; in the office of the inspector-general of imports and exports; served under Her Majesty's Commissioner for the Exhibition of 1862 as assistant for finance and statistics.

raw_text: MAURITIUS, BISHOP OF.—RIGHT REV. VINCENT WILLIAM RYAN, D.D.—Educated at Magdalen Hall, Oxford; where he was 2nd class in classics, 1840; and graduated M.A. 1848; held preferment for some years in the Channel Islands; subsequently head of the Liverpool institution; became principal of Highbury College, the metropolitan training institution; and was consecrated 1st Bishop of this see, 1854.

MAXSE, LIEUT.-COLONEL.—Appointed lieut.-governor of Heligoland in 1863; entered the army in 1849, and served in the Eastern campaign of 1854 as aide-de-camp to Lord Cardigan, including the battles of Alma and Balaclava (wounded), siege of Sebastopol; medal and clasps, 5th class of the Medjidié and Turkish medals.
MAXWELL, W. J.—Was appointed extra clerk in the customs' department, Sierra Leone, Oct. 1856; 2nd landing-waiter, 1859; acted as 2nd clerk and locker, May, 1860, to May, 1861.

MEADE, JAMES.—Colonial secretary and treasurer, Montserrat; a non-elected member of the legislative assembly, and a member of the executive council in that island; was appointed a justice of peace and provost-marshal 1853, which office he held until 1865, when he was promoted to the offices of colonial secretary and treasurer; acted as puisne justice in 1852; was a member of the executive legislative council in 1861; is registrar of deeds; registrar-general of births, &c.; comptroller of customs; and a member of the board of public works.

MEAGHER, JAMES.—Superintendent of public works, Trinidad; appointed Aug. 1863. MELBOURNE (AUSTRALIA), 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1847.—RIGHT REV. MERCER, W. T.—Graduated at Exeter College, Oxford, B.A., 1843; M.A. 1851; entered at the Inner Temple, 1842, but has not been called to the bar. Has been colonial secretary of Hong Kong since 1854; and a member of both councils. Entered the colonial service as private secretary to Sir J. Davis, governor of Hong Kong, 1844; appointed acting colonial treasurer, 1845; confirmed 1847; administered the government of Hong Kong from July to Sept. 1861, and again from July 1862 to Sept. 1864; again on the departure of Governor Sir H. Robinson for Ceylon, from March, 1865, to March, 1866.

raw_text: MERIVALE, HERMAN, C.B. (Civil).—Was educated at Harrow, and at Oriel and Trinity Colleges, Oxford; Ireland scholar, 1825; graduated 1st class in classics, 1827; elected fellow of Balliol, 1828; obtained the chancellor's prize for English essay, 1830; was Eldon scholar, 1831, and professor of political economy, 1837; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, Nov. 1832; practised on the western circuit; was recorder of Penzance, Falmouth, and Helston from 1841 to 1847; was appointed under-secretary of state for the colonies in 1848, and under-secretary of state for India, 1859; created a C.B., 1859; is author of "Lectures on Colonization and Colonies, delivered at Oxford."

raw_text: MONCK, 4th VISCT. (Ire. Creat. 1800); BARON MONCK, 1797.—CHARLES STANLEY MONCK.—Succeeded his father in 1849; educated at Trinity College, Dublin; called to the bar in Ireland, 1841; was a lord of the treasury from 1855 to 1858; is a deputy-lieut. of Wicklow; was an unsuccessful candidate at the Wicklow election, May, 1848; was M.P. for Portsmouth from 1852 to 1857; was appointed a commissioner of charitable donations and bequests (Ire.) 1851; and appointed captain-general and governor-in-chief of Canada, Oct. 1861.

raw_text: MONTAGU, J. E.—Registrar of deeds, Cape of Good Hope, 1864; entered the service in the colonial office, 1844; was appointed chief clerk, 1852; under colonial secretary, 1858.

MONTANARO, GIUSEPPE.—Served in the commissariat department at Malta from 1806 to 1829; was purveyor of charitable institutions and collector of land revenue, and appointed cashier of the treasury, Malta, 1859. Is ex-officio member of the council of government.

MONTEAGLE, 1st BARON (United Kingdom, Creat. 1839); Privy Councillor, 1834.—THOMAS SPRING RICE, F.R.S.—Was educated at the University of Cambridge; studied for the bar for some time, but relinquished the profession on his first marriage; was under-secretary of state for the home department in 1827; secretary to the treasury from Nov. 1830 till June, 1834; secretary for the colonies from July to Nov. 1834; chancellor of the exchequer from April, 1835, to Sept. 1839, when he succeeded Sir John Newport as comptroller-general of the exchequer. Represented Limerick in parliament from 1820 to 1832, and the borough of Cambridge from 1832 to 1839.

MONTREAL, 2nd BISHOP OF. Founded 1836. —RIGHT REV. FRANCIS FULFORD, D.D.—Educated at Tiverton grammar school, and at Exeter College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. in 1824, and was elected a fellow, June, 1825; received the degree of D.D. in 1850; was rector of Trowbridge, Wilts, from 1832 to 1842; rector of Croydon, Cambridgeshire, from 1842 to 1845; was minister of Curzon Chapel, in the parish of St. George's, Hanover Square, London, from 1845 till his consecration in 1850; was also chaplain to the Duchess of Gloucester. This diocese is situated in Lower Canada, and was formerly a portion of the diocese of Quebec. Annual value of the see £800, from the colonial bishoprics' fund, &c.

MOODY, RICHARD CLEMENT.—Entered the royal engineers, Nov. 1830; promoted to be captain, March, 1844; lieut.-colonel, Jan. 1855; and colonel, April, 1858; appointed lieut.-governor of the Falkland Islands, May, 1841; governor, June, 1843; which appointment he held until 1847; appointed chief commissioner of lands and works in British Columbia, Sept. 1858, which appointment he held until Dec. 1863; now commands at Chatham.

MORRISON, WM. LAWTIE.—Entered the royal engineers, 1843; promoted to be 1st lieut. April, 1846; captain, Feb. 1854; was appointed superintendent of public works at Barbados, 1857; and surveyor-general of Mauritius, July, 1861. Is on seconded list, and does not receive any regimental pay.

MOSELEY, HENRY EDWARD.—Was Second clerk in the treasurer's department, Bahamas, from Aug. 8, 1863, to Aug. 31st, 1865; appointed librarian to the Nassau public library and clerk to the board of public works at the same place, on July, 1865.

raw_text: MUNDY, ROBERT MILLER.—Cadet Royal military academy, Woolwich, Feb. 1828; lieutenant Royal artillery, June, 1833; lieutenant Royal horse artillery, March 1841 to April 1844; 2nd captain, 1844; brevet major, Oct. 1846; invalided on half-pay, Oct. 1847; appointed magistrate for county of Hants, June, 1851; served as lieutenant-colonel, Osmanli horse artillery in Turkey, March 1855 to Aug. 1856; is lieut.-colonel in the Turkish service, and has received 3rd class medjidie; appointed lieutenant-governor of Granada, Sept. 1863; administering government of Barbados, 10th April, 1865.

MUNRO, G. G.—Appointed acting landing-waiter and searcher of customs for Carriacon, 22nd March, 1834; landing-waiter and searcher, Port of St. George, Grenada, Aug., 1834; surveying officer, 6th June, 1839; landing and tide surveyor, 24th July, 1840; Collector of customs, Tortola, 15th Sept., 1840, retained office until its abolition in 1852; appointed police magistrate, eastern district, Grenada, 5th Feb., 1861; immigration agent, 23rd May, 1863.

raw_text: MURRAY, E.—Marshal of the courts, Trinidad; appointed 1851; was registrar of slaves.

MURRAY, MAJ.-GEN. FREEMAN.—Served with 64th regiment from June, 1825, to Sept. 1827 at Gibraltar; from 1827 to 1834 at home; from 1834 to 1836 with 60th royal rifles at Malta; from 1836 to 1837 at Gibraltar; from 1837 to 1839 in the Ionian Islands, where he served as brigade-major; appointed aide-de-camp to the lord-lieutenant of Ireland in 1839; served on that staff until 1843, when he became assistant military secretary to the commander of the forces in Ireland; resigned that appointment to accompany the 60th rifles to India; served in India and Scinde from 1845 to 1847; became lieut.-colonel in 17th regiment in 1847; appointed to the command of the 72nd Highlanders in 1849; joined that regiment at Barbados, West Indies, and proceeded to Trinidad, where he commanded the troops until his regiment was ordered to North America in 1850; commanded the troops in New Brunswick from that time to 1853; administered the government of the colony of New Brunswick from May to Sept. 1852, during the absence in England of the lieut.-governor; was quartered in Halifax, Nova Scotia, from 1853 to 1854, when he was appointed governor of Bermuda, which government he held until the 14th of March, 1861.

MURRAY, JOHN IVOR.—M.D. and L.R.C.S., Edinburgh, 1845; F.R.C.S., Edinburgh, 1856; F.R.S.E., 1857; acting staff surgeon with the British army in the Crimea in 1855 and 1856, for which he received the Crimean and Turkish medals; appointed colonial surgeon, Hong Kong, 1858. Is also surgeon to the Lock hospital, in that colony.

MUSSON, SAMUEL PAYNTER.—Appointed a clerk in the governor's private secretary's office, Jamaica, 1st April, 1866. On change of constitution, appointed a second-class clerk in the office of the financial secretary, Jamaica, 16th September, 1866.

raw_text: MYERS, WILLIAM ROBERTSON.—Secretary of executive committee, and clerk of privy council, Jamaica; appointed 1854.

NAIRN, WILLIAM E., B.A.—Entered the government service in Tasmania, 1838, in the colonial secretary's office; police magistrate, 1840; comptroller-general and sheriff, 1856; president of the legislative council in Tasmania, 1859. NARRAWAY. — Employed in the colonial office as an extra clerk, since 1831. NASSAU.—Patent, Nov., 1861; 1st bishop, Dr. C. Caulfield. 2nd bishop, Dr. A. R. P. Venables, graduate of Exeter College, Oxford, 1848; late curate of St. Paul's Church, Oxford; appointed 1863. The diocese comprises the Bahama Islands, Turks, and Caicos Islands, the population being about 38,700. It contains 13 parishes and 2 districts.

NATAL, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1853.—RIGHT REV. JOHN WILLIAM COLENSO, D.D.—Educated at the Devonport proprietary school and at St. John's College, Cambridge, where he obtained a fellowship; graduated B.A. 1836 (when he was 2nd wrangler and Smith's prizeman); M.A., 1839; D.D., 1853; was fellow of St. John's College from 1837 to 1846; mathematical master at Harrow school from 1838 to 1842; rector of Fornsett St. Mary, Norfolk, from 1846 to 1853; consecrated 1st Bishop of this see, 1853. Is author of "Elements of Algebra," and other educational works. This diocese, originally part of the see of Cape Town, comprises the district of Natal.

raw_text: NEEDHAM, JOSEPH.—Chief justice of Vancouver Island, 1865; is a member of the English bar.
