raw_text: NELL, GEO. FRED., B.C.L., Cambridge.—Barrister-at-law; acting deputy Queen's advocate, at Galle, in 1861; and appointed deputy Queen's advocate for north-western province, 1863.

NEIL, LOUIS.—Advocate of the supreme court of Ceylon; appointed acting deputy Queen's advocate for the southern circuit at Galle, in January, 1863; then appointed to the northern circuit in April, 1863; acting again at Galle from November, 1865. NELSON (NEW ZEALAND), 1st BISHOP OF.—RIGHT REV. EDMUND HOBHOUSE.—Educated at Eton and at Balliol College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1838; M.A. (Merton) 1842; B.D. 1850; D.D. 1858; elected fellow of Merton, 1841; appointed vicar of St. Peter in the East, Oxford, 1843, and consecrated 1st Bishop of this see, 1858.

events.text_span: consecrated 1866.

raw_text: NELSON, 2nd BISHOP OF.—RIGHT REV. ANDREW BURN SUTEN.—Of Trinity College, Cambridge; lately incumbent of All Saints, Mile End New Town; consecrated 1866.

NESBITT, CHARLES R.—Was police magistrate at Nassau, 1831; appointed colonial secretary, registrar of records, and clerk of the council, Bahamas, 1838. Held dormant commissions as lieut. governor, 1841 and 1844; administered the government of St. Vincent from Jan. 1860 to June, 1861 and appointed administrator of the government of Bahamas, June, 1861, and again in 1864.

NESBITT, EDWIN J.—Was in the colonial secretary's office, Bahamas, during 1860 and 1861; acted as secretary to the board of education of that colony, Feb. 1862; appointed acting clerk of the house of assembly, Vancouver's Island, Nov. 1862; chief clerk in office of colonial secretary, Jan. 1863. NEWCASTLE (AUSTRALIA), 2nd BISHOP of. Founded 1847.—RIGHT REV. WILLIAM TYRRELL, D.D.—Educated at Charterhouse school, and at St. John's College, Cambridge, where he obtained a scholarship and was 4th senior optime; graduated B.A. 1831, and M.A. 1834; ordained 1832; appointed 1st Bishop of Newcastle in 1847, on the division of the bishopric of Australia.

NEWFOUNDLAND, 2nd Bishop of. Founded 1839.—RIGHT REV. EDWARD FEILD, D.D.—Educated at Rugby, where he obtained the prize for the best Latin poem in 1820; and at Queen's College, Oxford, where he obtained a Michel fellowship; was first class in mathematics, 1823, and second class in classics, and graduated M.A., 1826; appointed public examiner in 1827; was presented by his college to the living of English-Bicknor, Gloucestershire, in 1833, and consecrated Bishop of Newfoundland in 1834. The diocese includes Newfoundland and the Bermudas, and contains a population of 124,300 souls.

raw_text: NEWTON, EDWARD.—Assistant colonial secretary of Mauritius; appointed May, 1859; acting auditor-general, September, 1863; auditor-general, April, 1866.

NEWTON, WILLIAM.—Was employed in the surveyor-general's department at Mauritius, Feb. 1832; promoted to be inspector of works, Feb. 1846. NEW WESTMINSTER (BRITISH COLUMBIA), 1st BISHOP of, 1865.—REV. JOHN POSTLETHWAITE, graduated 1851, Trinity College, Cambridge; lately incumbent of Coatham, near Redcar.

NEW ZEALAND, 1st Bishop of. Founded 1841.—RIGHT REV. GEORGE AUGUSTUS SELWYN, D.D.—Educated at St. John's College, Cambridge; was a junior optime in mathematics, and 1st class in classics in 1831; consecrated 1st Bishop of New Zealand in 1841; was curate of Windsor previous to his consecration. The diocese was reconstituted in 1858 and made metropolitan over the sees of Wellington, Waiapu, Nelson, and Christchurch.

NICHOLSON, 1st BART. (Creat. 1859), KNT. BACHEL. 1852.—SIR CHAS. NICHOLSON, D.C.L.—Emigrated to Australia in 1834, and practised there as a physician; was elected a member of the first legislative council of New South Wales in 1843, and was three times chosen for the office of speaker (1845 to 1856); is provost of the University of Sydney.

raw_text: NICHOLAS, REV. S.—Portuguese colonial chaplain, Colombo, Ceylon, 1864.

NICOL, GEORGE WM.—Was acting fourth writer in colonial secretary's office, Sierra Leone, 1829; held various offices in the colonial and in the mixed commissions courts until Feb. 1837; in Dec. of that year was admitted to practice as a proctor in those courts; was an ensign in the Sierra Leone militia, 1829, a lieutenant, 1832; served in the military and naval expedition against the rebels at Cobolo on the Ribbie River; was appointed manager and magistrate of the second eastern district of the colony, 1838; fourth writer in the colonial secretary's office, and registrar of the court of chancery, 1841; registrar of the vice-admiralty court, 1846; and attained the rank of lieutenant-colonel of militia in that year; in 1850, was appointed auditor, and commissioner for the settlement of claims of land in 1859, and chairman of that board in 1862; colonial secretary and accountant and ex-officio member of the executive and legislative council, Aug. 1859.

raw_text: NIND, PHILIP H.—Educated at Eton and Oxford, graduated B.A., 1856, M.A., 1857; entered colonial service as extra clerk in the office of the colonial secretary of Vancouver's Island, October, 1859; appointed assistant gold commissioner and stipendiary magistrate for Cariboo, British Columbia, July, 1860; superintendent of gold escort, June, 1863; member of legislative council, Dec. 1863; magistrate at Douglas, March, 1864.

NOAD, W. R.—Was acting surveyor-general, Newfoundland, 1847; appointed assistant surveyor. Ceylon, Oct. 1852; acting surveyor-general, 1865. NORMANBY (RIGHT HON. GEORGE AUGUSTUS CONSTANTINE PHIPPS), MARQUIS OF,—succeeded to the title in 1863.—Privy councillor, appointed 1851; appointed ensign and lieutenant, Scots fusileer guards, 1838; deputy-lieutenant of Yorkshire, 1844; major, North York militia, 1841; resigned 1853; was treasurer of the Queen's household from Jan. 1853 to Feb. 1858, having been comptroller from July, 1851 to Feb. 1852; was added to the privy council on becoming controller; was M.P. for Scarborough from 1847 to 1851, when he was defeated after accepting office; regained his seat in July, 1852; appointed lieut.-governor of Nova Scotia, Jan. 1858; and resigned that appointment in Sept., 1863.

raw_text: NORRIS, HENRY C.—Appointed clerk in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, April, 1841; promoted assistant-clerk, Oct. 1859; was secretary to Lord Carnarvon from Feb. 1858 to May, 1859; held the same appointment with Mr. C. Fortescue from June till Oct. 1859.

NORTON, BERNARD GUSTAVUS.—B.A. of Queen's University, Ireland; of the Inner Temple, London; called to the bar, 1855; appointed solicitor-general, British Guiana, 6 March, 1863, as also judge advocate of the militia; now acting 2nd puisne judge, Berbice; admitted to practice at the bar of the supreme court of British Guiana, Dec. 1860; appointed 2nd puisne judge, 1865.

NOVA SCOTIA, 4th BISHOP of. Founded 1787.—RIGHT REV. HIBBERT BINNEY, D.D.—Educated at King's College, London; was afterwards successively scholar and fellow of Worcester College, Oxford, where he graduated 1st class mathematics, and 2nd class classics, 1842, M.A. 1844; appointed tutor of that college in 1846, and bursar in 1848; ordained a deacon, 1842, a priest, 1843, and consecrated 4th Bishop of Nova Scotia, 1851; is an honorary fellow of King's College, London; patron of the archdeaconries of Nova Scotia and New Brunswick, and visitor of King's College, Nova Scotia. This was the first colonial see founded by Great Britain; the diocese includes Nova Scotia, Cape Breton, and Prince Edward's Island.

events.text_span: appointed July 1, 1857.

raw_text: NOYES, WILLIAM T.—Police magistrate, Port Sorell, Tasmania; appointed July 1, 1857.

NUNES, W. A.—Was appointed in Nov 1835, amanuensis to Sir James Stephen, when under-secretary of state for the colonies; and in Nov. 1839 was made superintendent of the copy department of the colonial office, with which was combined the appointment of registrar in Jan. 1853.

events.position: Entered the service as colonial surgeon
events.text_span: Entered the service as colonial surgeon, 1826

raw_text: OFFICER, ROBERT, M.A.—Entered the service as colonial surgeon, 1826; speaker of the house of assembly of Tasmania.

OGILVY, DAVID STEWART.—Was ensign in Madras native infantry, 1851; appointed lieutenant land transport corps, 1854; captain, 1854; appointed to the Madras sappers and miners, 1857; assistant field engineer with Sir Colin Campbell's army, Lucknow, which appointment he quitted in March, 1858, proceeding to Mauritius on sick leave in consequence of a wound received in the assault on the king's palace at Lucknow. Has received the Crimean medal and Sebastopol clasp, Turkish medal and Indian medal and clasps; appointed stipendiary magistrate at Mauritius, Sept. 1858; was for some time acting protector of immigrants.

O'BRIEN, JAMES W.—Appointed clerk and sorter in the imperial post-office, Trinidad, in 1842; held that appointment till 1851; in charge of entire department from 1851 to 1858; and was also employed during that period as clerk in the colonial post-office; acted as Her Majesty's packet agent in 1858; in Jan. 1860, owing to the amalgamation of the imperial and colonial post-offices, was appointed chief clerk of the general post-office; promoted to chief clerk in auditor-general's department 17th April, 1862; was acting postmaster-general from July to Sept. 1862; acting auditor-general from 4th October to 31st December, 1865.

O'DONNELL, J.—Employed on the ordnance survey of England and Scotland, from October, 1847, to October, 1857; appointed to the surveyor-general's department in Ceylon, January, 1855; appointed assistant-surveyor, Ceylon, 1864.

raw_text: O'GRADY, H. E.—Acting assistant government agent at Jaffna, Ceylon, 1840; assistant government agent at Kandy, 1844; district judge of Batticaloa, 1845; acting district judge, Caltura, 1850; acting assistant colonial secretary and clerk of the councils, 1851; district judge, &c., Trincomalee, 1852; government agent, north-western province, 1855; acting government agent and fiscal for the southern provinces, 1856; resumed duties in north-west province, 1858; acting government agent, east province, 1864; appointment confirmed 1865.

raw_text: O'HALLORAN, WILLIAM LITTLEJOHN.—Entered army as ensign 14th foot, in Dec. 1823; obtained lieutenancy in action, 1826; company by purchase in 38th regiment, April, 1837; served at siege and storming of Bhurtapore in 1825-6; on staff, Sangor division, 1828-9; employed on recruiting service in Ireland in 1832-3; retired from service, April, 1840; appointed a member of the audit board in South Australia, 1843; private secretary to Governor Grey, and clerk of the councils, 1843; auditor-general, 1851.

raw_text: OKEY, CHARLES H.—Member of council, Antigua, barrister of the Inner Temple, knight of the legion of honour, author of works on international relations, educated at merchant tailors' school, also at Heidelberg University, acted as private secretary to Lord Stuart de Rothesay, ambassador to the court of France, and as counsel to Her Majesty's embassy under four successive ambassadors in Paris; acted as police magistrate, Antigua, from July 1862 to May 1863; appointed puisne justice of Antigua in August, 1863.

OLIPHANT, LAURENCE.—Has been called to the English bar; was appointed private secretary to the chief justice of Ceylon, 1848; accompanied Lord Elgin's special mission to Washington in May, 1854, as private secretary; was shortly afterwards appointed chief secretary and superintendent-general of Indian affairs in Canada; and accompanied Lord Elgin's special mission to China in April, 1857, as private secretary, and returned to England in May, 1859; was appointed a captain in the West Middlesex rifle volunteer corps, Feb. 28, 1860.

raw_text: OLIVIER, FELIX.—Appointed clerk in the registration and mortgage office at Mauritius, Nov. 1836, and taxing clerk, July, 1857.

raw_text: ONDAATJE, REV. S. D. J.—Appointed colonial chaplain of Jaffna, Ceylon, Dec. 1840; resigned that appointment, September, 1842; appointed colonial chaplain of St. Thomas' Church, Colombo, in that colony, Dec. 1861.

raw_text: ONDAATJE, W. C.—Lic. Med. and Surg., Bengal College; appointed medical sub-assistant to the government of Ceylon; 3rd class, 1846; 2nd class, 1851; 1st class, 1856; and assistant colonial surgeon, 1858.

ONTARIO, 1st BISHOP of, 1861.—Dr. J. T. Lewis.—The diocese of Ontario comprises that part of West Canada which is bounded on the north by the Ottawa river, on the south by the Lake of Ontario; eastward from and including Prince Edward island; on the south-east by the St. Lawrence river and the district of Vandreuil. It is a subdivision of the old diocese of Toronto. Its population is 370,000.

events.text_span: Dr. E. TWELLS.—Consecrated 1863.

raw_text: ORANGE RIVER, FREE STATE, 1st Bishop of.—Dr. E. TWELLS.—Consecrated 1863.

ORD, COLONEL HARRY ST. GEORGE, C.B., 1865.—Entered the royal engineers, 1837; promoted to be lieut., 1839; captain, 1846; brevet-major, 1854; lieut.-colonel, 1859; served principally in the West Indies and coast of Africa; served as brigade-major in the combined French and English expedition to the Baltic in 1854; and was at the siege and capture of Bomarsund, for which he received brevet rank; was employed under the colonial office in 1855-6 as commissioner to the Gold Coast; and in 1856-57 and 1860 at the courts of Paris and the Hague, to assist the ambassador and minister in carrying out negotiations with respect to the French and Netherlands possessions on the coast of Africa; appointed lieut.-governor of Dominica, Aug. 1857; and governor and commander-in-chief of Bermuda, Feb. 1861; sent on a special mission to the settlements on the West Coast of Africa, Oct. 1864; governor of Straits Settlements, 1867.

ORPEN, HENRY M. HERBERT.—Served as captain commanding Colesbey native levies, from Jan. 1851 to Aug. 1853, during the Kaffir war of those years; also as district adjutant of North Victoria, from April, 1852, to March, 1853; held several other military appointments during the same period; received a Kaffir war medal for services; appointed landing-waiter and searcher in the customs' department at Port Elizabeth, Cape Colony, Dec. 1853; measuring surveyor under merchant shipping act, Jan. 1856; senior landing-waiter and searcher, Dec. 1856; landing-surveyor, August, 1857.
OSBORN, M.—Clerk to resident magistrate at Ladigmith, Natal; appointed Sept. 1865, resident magistrate of Newcastle in that colony.
O'SULLIVAN, DANIEL.—Stipendiary magistrate, Dominica; appointed 1835. Is senior assistant justice of the court of common pleas, and a member of executive and legislative councils of that island.

raw_text: OTAGO, 1st BISHOP OF, 1865-6.—Right Rev. Henry Lascelles Jenner, D.D. of Trinity Hall, Cambridge, lately vicar of Preston, near Sandwich; consecrated 1861.

raw_text: PACKER, CHARLES.—Graduated B.A. Trinity College, Cambridge, 1839; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1841; appointed solicitor-general, Barbados, Jan. 1847; escheator-general, 1859; elected member of the general assembly for the parish of St. Andrew's, 1846; and has continued to represent the same constituency to the present time; elected speaker of house of assembly, 1861.

PADMORE, W.—Clerk in customs, Jamaica, 1839; clerk and warehouse keeper, St. Christopher, 1842; acted as collector of customs, 1845 and 1848; clerk and assistant comptroller of customs and navigation laws, Barbados, 1853; acting comptroller, St. Christopher, 1854; pensioned Jan. 1855, on abolition of customs in West Indies; Notary public, St. Christopher, 1858; superintendent of immigrants, 1858; private secretary, clerk to executive council, clerk to administrative committee, 17th June, 1865.

raw_text: PAKINGTON, 1st BART. (United Kingdom, Creat. 1846.) Privy Councillor, 1852.—RIGHT HON. SIR JOHN SOMERSET PAKINGTON.—Was educated at Eton and at Oriel College, Oxford; was secretary of state for the colonies from Feb. to Dec. 1852; was first lord of the admiralty, Feb. 1858 to June, 1859; became a deputy-lieut. of Worcestershire in 1832; major, Worcestershire yeomanry cavalry, 1856; was chairman of the quarter sessions of that county from 1834 to 1854, and has been M.P. for Droitwich since 1837.

PALMER, GEORGE.—Acting auditor of public accounts, Grenada, and colonial revenue officer; appointed 1854. Entered the Imperial customs in 1837; collector of customs, Grenada, 1842, and again in 1848; on the reduction of the customs staff in 1849, was made clerk and searcher, and held that appointment until its abolition in 1863; colonial revenue office, 1854; and acting auditor of accounts, 1857; appointed police magistrate, Jan. 1865; and coroner of St. Patrick, August, 1865.
PALMER, JOHN, M.A. of Trinity College, Dublin.—Colonial treasurer, Dominica; appointed 1852. Is comptroller of customs and navigation laws, and a member of the house of assembly.

PANTIN, H. J.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; called to the bar, Middle Temple, on 30 Jan. 1846. In July, 1848, appointed acting stipendiary magistrate for Naparima police district, Trinidad. In October following, transferred to southern police district, where he continued to act till May, 1849. In Oct. 1849, during riots, despatched with commission of special magistrate in charge of boat's crew of seamen from H.M.S. 'Scorpion,' under command of Lieutenant Morney, R.N., to inquire into outrages committed by a mob supposed to be connected with rioters in Port of Spain. Performed this duty, and received a letter of approval from the governor, Lord Harris. In Dec. 1849, appointed commissioner to visit and report on African immigrants located on estates. In April, 1830, appointed acting stipendiary magistrate for Naparima police district; acted as such till October of same year, when he was confirmed, and removed to the county of Caroni.

events.text_span: appointed to the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, Feb. 1858.

raw_text: PARK, H. C.—Was employed for four years on the ordnance survey; appointed to the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, Feb. 1858.

PARKER, C. S.—Educated at Eton and University College, Oxford; first-class in classics, M.A., fellow of University College; public examiner, 1859, 1860, and 1863; examiner in modern languages, 1862-3; curator of the university galleries; appointed private secretary to Mr. Cardwell, May, 1864, to July, 1866.

PARKER, THOMAS.—Auditor and comptroller of public accounts, St. Lucia; appointed 1853. Is a member of the executive and legislative councils of that island; was formerly in colonial service at Barbados, for which he receives a pension.

PARKER, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1858.) SIR HENRY WATSON PARKER.—Was private secretary to Sir George Gipps when governor of New South Wales; was for many years a member and chairman of committee of the legislative council; was first minister and principal secretary and a member of the executive council of New South Wales. Residence, Richmond, S.W.
PARTELOW, J.—Auditor-general, New Brunswick.

Matura, 1851; assistant to the government agent, Kandy, 1851; commissioner of requests, Putlam, 1853; acting district judge, Tangalle, 1855; assistant government agent, &c. &c. Hambantotte, 1856; acting principal collector of customs, 1866.

raw_text: PASSMORE, THOS. H.—Medical officer for the parishes of St. John and St. Mark; appointed May, 1862.
PASSOW, F. W.—Examiner of accounts to the government of Nova Scotia.

PATERSON, G. W.—Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, February, 1859. Commissioner of requests and police magistrate, Point Pedro, 1862; acting assistant, government agent, Trincomalee, 1863; acting police magistrate, Galle, 1864; assistant to the government agent at Colombo, January, 1865.
PATTERSON, P.—Civil engineer, Natal, 1860. Was previously in the service of the colony of Grenada.

PAUNCEFOTE, JULIAN.—Called to the bar, Inner Temple, May, 1852; appointed private secretary to the late Sir Wm. Molesworth, when secretary of state for the colonies, in 1855. Has practised at the Hong Kong bar for several years; acting attorney-general in May, 1865; appointed attorney general of Hong Kong, 22 July, 1866. Is ex-officio member of the executive legislative councils of that colony.

events.text_span: Assistant surveyor-general, Vancouver's Island, appointed 1861

raw_text: PEARSE, BENJAMIN W.—Assistant surveyor-general, Vancouver's Island, appointed 1861; previously employed in same capacity by Hudson Bay Company during their tenure of the Island.

PEEL, Privy Counsellor. Appointed 1857.—RIGHT HON. FREDERICK PEEL, 2nd son of the late Sir Robert Peel, 2nd bart.—Educated at Harrow and at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was 1st class in classics, 1845; was called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1849; was under-secretary for the colonies from Nov. 1851 to March, 1852, and from Dec. 1852 to Feb. 1855; under-secretary for war from 1855 to 1857; is a deputy-lieut. of Warwickshire; was M.P. for Leominster from Feb. 1849 to July, 1852, and for Bury, Lancashire, from July, 1852, to April, 1857; re-elected for Bury, May, 1859, and appointed secretary to the treasury, 1860.

events.text_span: appointed 1858.

raw_text: PEMBERTON, AUGUSTUS.—Commissioner of police and stipendiary magistrate at Victoria, Vancouver's Island; appointed 1858.

appointed surveyor-general of Vancouver's Islands, Aug. 1860.

raw_text: PEMBERTON, J.D.—Employed in the capacity of colonial surveyor of Vancouver's island by the Hudson Bay Company from 1851 to 1859; appointed surveyor-general of Vancouver's Islands, Aug. 1860.
PEMBERTON, SHOLTO THOS.—Chief justice of Dominica, appointed 1861. Was previously solicitor-general of the island of Nevis, 1850; is from the colonial bar.

events.text_span: Appointed April, 1864

raw_text: PENNELL, G. B.—Appointed April, 1864; colonial engineer, civil engineer, and architect, Jamaica.

PENNELL, E. B.—Appointed, after a competitive examination, a clerk in the office of secretary of state for the colonies, April, 1859; promoted to the 3rd class, April, 1863; appointed 25th Nov. 1865, private secretary to Mr. Forster, under secretary of state for the colonies, until 6 July, 1866; also to Mr. Adderley, from that date to 1 Oct., and to Sir F. Rogers from 1 Oct. 1866.

PENNELL, R. C.—Colonial secretary and auditor, St. Helena; appointed 1844. Vice-consul at Rio de Janeiro in May, 1830; acting commissary judge and consul during part of 1832-3; special justice in Jamaica in 1834; and auditor at St. Helena, on the 24th Dec. 1836.

PENNINGTON, EDWARD.—Appointed a clerk, in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 1838; was transferred to the war department, on the separation of the two offices in 1854, as an assistant senior, and promoted to the 1st class in 1856; was assistant to the chief clerk from 1843 to 1854.
PERCH, THOS.—Police magistrate of Bridgetown, Barbados, and a master in chancery.

events.text_span: appointed 1862.

raw_text: PERKIS, A. E.—Consular clerk, Hong Kong; appointed 1862.

PERLEY, MOSES HENRY.—Was appointed one of the commissioners for Indian affairs in the province of New Brunswick in 1838; emigration officer for New Brunswick, June 17, 1843, and May 25, 1855; commissioner for the North American fisheries, under the reciprocity treaty with the United States of June 5, 1854. PERTH (WESTERN AUSTRALIA), 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1856.—RIGHT REV. MATTHEW BLAGDEN HALE, D.D.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1835, M.A. 1838; was appointed archdeacon of Adelaide, 1847; canon there, 1849; and consecrated 1st Bishop of this see, 1857. The diocese includes the whole of Western Australia.

PHELPS, WILLIAM R.—Appointed chief justice of St. Helena, 1863. Is also judge of the Vice-Admiralty court, and member of the council of that island.
PHILLIPS, H. L.—Second puisne judge, Natal; appointed 1863. Acting first puisne judge 1864.
PHILLIPS, MAURICE G.—Assistant clerk gold commissioners' office, Cariboo, British Columbia, formerly Inland revenue office, Somerset House.
PHILPOTT, EDWARD.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Alexandria division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1857. Was clerk to the magistrate at Port Elizabeth, 1837 to 1857.

raw_text: PICQUENARD, DONALD, C. JOSEPH.—Appointed extra clerk, registration office, March 1, 1853; clerk, post office, April 1, 1853; clerk, treasury, Jan. 1, 1854; junior clerk, treasury, July 1, 1859; curator of vacant estates, Mauritius, July 1, 1862.
PIERCE, WILLIAM EDWARD.—Secretary and clerk of cheque at the public hospital, British Guiana; also secretary to the committee of pilotage; was a sworn accountant.
PIERS, C.—Superintendent-general of convicts, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1856. Was resident magistrate, Tulbagh, from 1848.

PIKE, ADOLPHUS.—Entered the civil service of Sierra Leone as acting first writer, colonial secretary's office, 1843, having previously served as a temporary clerk in the commissariat department at that station, from Jan. 1853; was appointed agent of emigration for the island of Trinidad, 1844; and agent-general of emigration for the West Indies, 1846; receiver-general of unrepresented estates, 1848; acting harbour-master in 1855; acting collector of customs, 1856; postmaster, 1857; assessor of house and land tax, 1858; acting colonial secretary, 1858; is 1st writer in the colonial secretary's office, colonial auditor of accounts, a magistrate of the colony, and marshal of the mixed commission courts.

events.text_span: Appointed harbour-master, Sierra Leone, Aug. 1857

raw_text: PIKE, THOMAS —Appointed harbour-master, Sierra Leone, Aug. 1857; is ex-officio superintendent of quarantine, superintendent of lighthouses, and superintendent of the water police.
PILGRIM, F. F.—Police magistrate, St. John's Parish, Barbadoes.

events.text_span: appointed 1842.

raw_text: PINDER, C. F.—Registrar of the court of bankruptcy, clerk of the court of common pleas, and prothonotary, Bahamas; appointed 1842.
PINDER, JOHN. — Was inspector-general of police at Bahamas, 1840; harbour-master at Nassau, 1852; and provost-marshal, 1856. Is a member of council.

PINE, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1856.)—SIR BENJAMIN CHILLEY CAMPBELL PINE. — Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1834, M.A. 1840; called to the bar, 1841; appointed Queen's advocate at Sierra Leone, 1841; acting governor there, 1848; accompanied capt. Monypenny, R.N., to the Sherbro river, and destroyed a strong stockaded fort, from which outrages had been committed on British trading vessels. In 1849, again proceeded to the Sherbro, in conjunction with Captain Dunlop, R.N., and a detachment of troops, and put an end to the civil war, which had ravaged the country for years; lieut.-governor of Natal, 1849; 1851 received the thanks of her majesty's government for the measures he took to procure peace in the colony during the Kaffir war; in 1855, led a force of mounted volunteers against the Amabaca tribe, numbering 2,000 men, and completely enforced their submission; governor and commander-in-chief of the Gold Coast settlements, 1856; lieut.-governor of St. Christopher's, May, 1859. Is author of some articles in the Encyclopædia Britannica on the "African Colonies;" acting gov. Antigua, 1866.

PINE, RICHARD.—Appointed governor of the Gold Coast, 1862; was Queen's advocate, police magistrate, clerk of the councils, and superintendent of police at the Gambia, from 1855 to date of present appointment; prior to and during which period he held many appointments, and was frequently employed on important missions to native kings and chiefs.
PINES, HENRY G.—Immigration agent, Nova Scotia, and member of the legislative council.

PINNEY, F. B.—Collector of customs and principal comptroller of customs and navigation laws, Cape of Good Hope; is a member of the executive council; also, chairman of the harbour board; entered the home service as clerk in the legacy duty office in 1830; appointed to secretary's department of stamps and taxes in 1834; sub-collector of customs, Simon's Town, Cape of Good Hope, in 1838, and, in conjunction with that appointment, resident magistrate in 1848; acting sub-collector and comptroller of customs at Port Elizabeth, in 1857; confirmed in that appointment on the promotion of his predecessor, in 1861, and appointed to his present office in 1865.
PLAGUEMANN, O. F.—Superintendent of police, St. Christopher, March, 1858, till June, 1859; superintendent of public works, June, 1859, till the present time.

PLUES, S. S.—In the colonial service in Jamaica from 1843 to 1854, when he was appointed police magistrate of Honduras, which post he held till June, 1861; clerk of the courts and keeper of public records, June 1861 to August 1864; appointed attorney-general of Honduras, August 1864.
PLUMMER, H. P.—Stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana.

POLE, H.—Appointed commissioner of requests and police magistrate of Mulletivoe, Ceylon, 1845; commissioner of requests, &c., at Galle, 1847; at Jaffna, in 1848; district judge, &c., of Matura, 1854; acting assistant government agent, &c.; Manaar, 1859; resumed duties at Matura, 1861, acting district judge at Jaffna, 1863; resumed duties at Matura, 1865.

POLE, R. C.—Employed from July, 1859, till August, 1861, in the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon; acting landing and tide surveyor at Galle, April, 1862; acting assistant government agent, Jaffna, June, 1863; confirmed 1865.
POLLARD, WILLIAM BRANCH—Appointed auditor-general, British Guiana, 1860; was financial accountant from 1853 to 1860, when that office was abolished.
POLLARD, WILLIAM BRANCH, Jun. — Colonial civil engineer, British Guiana.

A.—Appointed colonial secretary of the Virgin Islands, 9th July 1844; registrar of deeds, August, 1846; notary public, 1851; registrar in Admiralty, March, 1847; clerk to court of Petty Sessions, February, 186; private secretary to Presidents Price, Longden, and Sir A. Rumbold, Bart., from 1859 to 1865; member of executive council, 1849 to 1852, when he resigned, but was re-appointed, August, 1865; is major of the Virgin Islands artillery.

PORTER, W.—Attorney-general of the Cape of Good Hope; appointed Sept. 1839. Is a member of the executive council, and has a seat in both houses of parliament; retired on a pension, 1865.

POTGIETER, CONRAD FREDERICK. — Assistant commissary general to the forces; served at the Cape of Good Hope in the commissariat, from 1835 to 1853, taking part in the Kaffir wars of 1835-46 and 1851-2, served in Turkey during the war with Russia, from 1854 to 1856; served in the civil service of British Kaffraria, in the treasury, from Feb. 1858 to June; and again as acting treasurer, from 1st Jan. 1860 to present time.

POWER, E. R. B.—Was gazetted to the office of extra assistant to the colonial secretary, Ceylon, on the 1st June, 1833; to be extra assistant, and to act as private secretary to the right hon. the governor, Sir R. Wilmot Horton, Bart. on the 6th Dec. 1833; to be private secretary to the governor on the 26th Dec. 1833; on the 16th Feb. 1835 proceeded to England by order of the governor, in charge of despatches for the secretary of state; returned to the colony, and resumed his duties as private secretary to the governor, on the 28th Oct. 1836, and continued to hold that appointment until the departure of Governor Sir R. Wilmot Horton from Ceylon in Nov. 1837; was appointed assistant government agent at Kurnegalle on the 10th May, 1837; and assistant government agent and district judge at Four Korles, on the 1st Oct. 1837; assistant government agent, &c. at Kaigalle, 1845; acting district judge, Badulla, 1849; principal assistant colonial secretary, and clerk to the councils. 1851; government agent, &c. central province, 1854; and a member of the legislative council; retired from the Ceylon civil service on a pension, May 1860; at the request of the committee appointed by the governor to further the objects of the International Exhibition, 1862, Mr. Power acted as the representative of Ceylon in London on that occasion.

POWER, JULIUS CHAS. — Entered the service in Oct. 1845 as accountant, surveyor-general's department, Hong Kong; appointed clerk of deed registry, also in the same department, 1847; appraiser of the supreme court in its ecclesiastical jurisdiction in Jan. 1846; was joint assessor and collector of police and lighting rates for the years 1856 and 1857; acted as colonial treasurer from April, 1860, to June, 1861; member of and honorary secretary to the board of education in June, 1861; acted as assistant magistrate of police, coroner, and sheriff, from Oct. 1861, to July, 1862; and as second magistrate of police and sheriff till end of 1862; reappointed sheriff in Jan. 1863, and held the office till Jan. 1864.

POWER, T. C. — Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1845; assistant agent at Galle, 1845; district judge, &c., Ratnapoora, 1846; assistant government agent, Kaigalle, 1849; assistant government agent, Kurnegalle, 1853; acting assistant agent, acting district judge, &c., Ratnapoora, 1859, in which appointment he was confirmed, 1862; district judge, &c., Badulla, 1863.

PRICHARD, C. J. — Late lieutenant in her Majesty's 38th regt. of foot; appointed warden of the Gaol at New Westminster, in the colony of British Columbia, Nov. 19, 1860. Is a justice of the peace for the colony, and holds an acting commission as judge of the county court.

PRIMET, C. BERESFORD. — Appointed clerk of customs, bonded warehouse-keeper, &c. at the Gambia, 1855; served under Colonel G. D'Arcy as Major in the royal Gambia militia, during the Badiboo war, and was present at the taking of Kinty Cunda and Sabba, and at the storming of the latter town on the 21st Feb. 1861; appointed assistant coroner, 1861; acting collector of customs, acting registrar, acting clerk of the crown, &c., justice of the peace, and commissioner of the court of requests, in 1862; and most favourably mentioned by the governor in despatch to secretary of state, 16th June, 1862, for zeal and energy displayed in bringing the Barra civil war to a conclusion. Acting auditor at the Gambia, 1864.

PYNE, WM. ROWLAND. — Appointed government clerk of the Falkland Islands, by Lord Taunton, Feb. 1858; clerk to executive and legislative councils and in charge of government stores, May 1859; was acting colonial secretary from January, 1862, to September of that year, when he was confirmed in the office of colonial secretary, by the Duke of Newcastle, K.G.; from January, 1862, to August, 1866, in charge of the colonial treasury; appointed magistrate, December, 1862, and a member of the executive council, January, 1863; is, by colonial ordinance, shipping master, officer of customs, and registrar of seamen; also, postmaster-general of the Falkland Islands; has, at different times, held the offices of coroner and register of lands, births, deaths, and marriages; is likewise colonial emigration and immigration agent; also, by custom, attorney general and crown prosecutor in the colonial law courts.

QUEBEC, 4th BISHOP OF. — DR. J. W. WILLIAMS, 1863; the diocese was first constituted by patent, 28 June, 1793, and comprised Upper and Lower Canada and their dependencies. It now includes districts of Quebec, Three Rivers, St. Francis and Gaspé, and contains 43 parishes.

QUIN, WM. — Formerly a sergeant in the royal artillery, and accompanied Colonel Chesney, R.A., in the last Euphrates expedition; after which he received an appointment under the imperial customs in the West Indies, 1840, and was made sub-treasurer of St. Christopher; appointed accountant in the treasury at Antigua, 1857; appointed captain superintendent of police at Hong Kong, 1862.

ROBERTS, HENRY.—Called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1836; appointed by Lord John Russell, Chairman, &c. of quarter sessions, and chief judge of common pleas in Jamaica, 1840; also commissioner of education in that colony in 1845; resigned Jan. 1853; appointed private secretary to the Duke of Newcastle, Jan. 1853; under secretary for war, Dec. 1854; resigned March, 1855; appointed commissioner of inland revenue, Oct. 1855, which appointment he now holds.

raw_text: ROBERTSON, D.—Member of the Royal College of Surgeons, Edinburgh; appointed surgeon of the liberated African department, M'Carthy's Island, Gambia, May 24, 1834; colonial surgeon, April 1, 1838; and colonial secretary, May 6, 1848; has served one year as Queen's advocate and judge of the court of common pleas; several years as assistant judge; three different times as acting chief justice; and nearly 34 months as acting governor; volunteered his services as surgeon of H. M.'s brig 'Curlew,' when the officers and crew were suffering from yellow fever, the surgeon and all the officers, with the exception of the commander and one midshipman and a third of the crew, having died in the harbour of Bathurst of that fatal disease. ROBINSON, BRYAN.—Assistant judge, Newfoundland; appointed 1858. Has been attached to the judicial establishment of the colony since 1833.

ROBINSON, SIR HERCULES, G.R.—Knt. Bachel. 1859.—Served for some time in the 87th fusiliers; on his retirement from the army was actively engaged during the Irish famine, 1846-9, under the commissioners of public works and poor law board in Ireland; was appointed in 1852 Chief Commissioner to inquire into the fairs and markets of Ireland; was appointed President of Montserrat, 1854; lieutenant-governor of St. Christopher, 1854; with which he held the dormant commission of governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands; appointed governor, commander-in-chief, and vice-admiral of Hong Kong, June, 1859. Was a member of the commission appointed in the autumn of 1863, to inquire into the financial condition of the straits settlements; appointed governor of Ceylon, Jan. 1865.

raw_text: ROGERS, 8th BART. (Eng. Creat. 1698-9.) — SIR FREDERIC ROGERS. — Educated at Eton, and at Oriel College, Oxford, where he attained a double first class in 1832; a Craven university scholarship, Oriel fellowship, Vinerian scholarship and fellowship; graduated M.A. 1835; B.C.L. 1838; called to the bar of the Inner Temple, 18; was appointed registrar of joint stock companies, 1843; and one of the commissioners on the colonial land and emigration board, 1846; assistant commissioner for the sale of encumbered estates in the West Indies, 1857; and permanent under-secretary of state for the colonies, 1860.

raw_text: ROWAN, K.C.B. (Creat. 1856.) — SIR WILLIAM ROWAN. — Entered the army as ensign, 52nd foot, in 1803; served 25 years with that regiment in Sicily, Portugal, Spain, France, Belgium (at Waterloo), and North America; was civil and military secretary to Lord Seaton in Canada from 1832 to 1839; attained the rank of major-general in 1846; commanded the forces in Canada with the local rank of lieut.-general from 1849 to 1855; and for a portion of that period administered the civil government of the province; made lieut.-general and col. of the 19th foot, 1854.

RUMBOLD, 5th BART. (GREAT BRITAIN. Creat. 1779.) SIR CARLO ARTHUR HENRY. — Entered the army in 1837; served with the 51st K.O.L.I. from 1837 to 1840, in New South Wales and Tasmania; with the 21st fusiliers from 1840 to 1842, in Bengal, Mauritius, and the Cape of Good Hope; purchased a company in 1st W. I. regiment in 1844; served in the West Indies until 1845; exchanged to 70th regiment and sold out in 1846; was appointed stipendiary magistrate at Jamaica, July, 1848; held that office until 1855; served with the Osmanli cavalry during the late Russian war, and was brigade-major to major-general Charles Havelock; holds the rank of colonel in the imperial Ottoman army, and has received her Majesty's royal licence to accept and wear the imperial order of the Medjidié; was appointed president of Nevis, 4th March, 1857, till March, 1863; president of the Virgin Islands, Oct. 1865.

RUMSEY, R. MURRAY. — Appointed, 1835, by Lord Glenelg, as stipendiary magistrate for St. Christopher; sent Jan. 1837, at the instance of Lord Glenelg, to the Virgin Islands, to associate himself with special justice Gordon, respecting the removal of some prædial apprentices from Thatch Island; noticed with approbation in a despatch of Lord Glenelg's, date Oct. 31, 1838, to Sir W. Colebrook, governor-in-chief, Antigua; offered by Lord John Russell the provost-marshalship of Dominica, 1841; mentioned in a despatch dated Aug. 19, 1841, with Mr. R. B. Cleghorn, as "the two stipendiary magistrates in this government whose reports have displayed in his Lordship's (Lord John Russell's) estimation the greatest judgment and ability." Appointed by Lord Stanley to the colonial secretaryship of Dominica, in 1842; and appointed by the same secretary for the colonies, colonial secretary of St. Christopher, 1844; also May 21, 1844, appointed by the governor-in-chief, registrar of deeds with the approbation of the council and assembly (Act 69, 1727). RUPERT'S LAND. 1st BISHOP OF. Appointed 1849. — RIGHT REV. DAVID ANDERSON, D.D. — Was formerly scholar of Exeter College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1836, M.A. 1839, and D.D. 1849; was ordained in 1837 and consecrated in 1849; was previously vice-principal of St. Bees' clerical college, and perpetual curate of All Saints' church, Derby. This diocese, comprehending the whole of the Hudson's Bay Company's settlements (or Prince Rupert's Land), was erected into a see in 1849, and endowed by a legacy from the late W. Leith, Esq. Retired 1864. RUPERT'S LAND. 2nd BISHOP OF. Appointed 1865. — RIGHT REV. ROBERT MACHRAY, D.D. — Late dean and fellow of Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge, and vicar of Madingley; graduated in 1855, when he was 34th Wrangler. The diocese of Rupert's Land is 37,000 square miles in extent, and has a population of 200,000 souls.

RUSHWORTH, EDW. EVERARD. — D.C.L. and late fellow of St. John's College, Oxford; appointed a clerk in the inland revenue office, Somerset House, 1842; was private secretary to Sir Gaspard Le Marchant, in the governments of Newfoundland and Nova Scotia, from 1847 to 1855; president administering the government of Montserrat from 1855 to 1861; appointed auditor-general of Mauritius, 1861. Was acting director of the railway department. Is a member ex-officio of the council and commissioner of admiralty court in that island; appointed March, 1866, government secretary, British Guinea; administrator, government of Trinidad, April, 1866; financial secretary, Jamaica, Oct. 1866.

RUSSELL, EARL (Creat. first Earl, Aug. 1861.) JOHN RUSSELL.—Was M.P. for Tavistock from 1813 till Mar. 1817, and also from July, 1818, till Mar. 1819; for Huntingdonshire from 1820 till 1826; and for Bandon Bridge from Jan. 1827 till 1830; was made a privy councillor, Nov. 22, 1830; was paymaster of the forces from Dec. 16, 1830, till Dec. 23, 1834; was M.P. for Tavistock from Dec. 1830 till Dec. 1832, when he was returned for South Devon, which county he represented till May, 1835; was appointed one of the ecclesiastical commissioners for England, Aug. 15, 1836; was secretary of state for the home department from April, 18, 1835, till Aug. 30, 1839; was M.P. for Stroud from May 21, 1835, till June, 1841; was secretary of state for the colonies from Aug. 30, 1839, to Sept. 3, 1841; was M.P. for the city of London, from July, 1841, to 1861; was first lord of the treasury from July 6, 1846, till Feb. 26, 1852; was secretary of state for foreign affairs from Dec. 28, 1852, till Feb. 20, 1853, when he retained a seat in the cabinet, but held no appointment; was lord president of the council from June 12, 1854, till Feb. 7, 1855; was appointed to be the unpaid charity commissioner for England and Wales, July 8, 1854; was sent on a special mission to Vienna, Feb. 11, 1855; was appointed one of a committee of council to superintend the application of any sums of money voted by parliament for the purpose of promoting public education, Feb. 28, 1855; was secretary of state for the colonies from March to July, 1855; was appointed secretary of state for foreign affairs, June 18, 1859; and one of the committee of council to superintend the application of any sums of money voted by parliament for the purpose of promoting public education, July 6, 1859; was in attendance upon her majesty when she proceeded to Coburg in Sept. and Oct. 1860; prime minister, Nov. 1865.

SANDWITH, HUMPHRY, C.B.—Was inspector-general of hospitals in the Turkish service; served with the Turkish army on the Danube in 1853, and was appointed, June 23, 1854, to serve under the orders of Colonel Beatson, and was attached to the staff of Brigadier-general (now Sir William) Williams during the defence of Kars in 1855; was granted, Feb. 7, 1856, her majesty's royal licence and permission to accept and wear the 3rd class of the Imperial Order of the Medjidie, which his imperial majesty the sultan was pleased to confer on him as a mark of his imperial majesty's approbation for his distinguished services before the enemy during the defence of Kars; was appointed a C.B. May 10, 1856; was attached to Earl Granville's embassy to Russia in July, 1856; was granted, April 30, 1857, her majesty's royal licence and permission to accept and wear the insignia of a knight, 5th class, of the Imperial Order of the Legion of Honour, which his majesty the emperor of the French was pleased to confer upon him, as a mark of his imperial majesty's approbation of his distinguished services before the enemy during the late war; was appointed colonial secretary of the Mauritius, Feb. 28, 1857. Resigned.

raw_text: SARGEAUNT, W. C.—Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, Feb. 1848; was permitted whilst on the establishment of that office to hold the appointment of colonial secretary of Natal from 1853 to 1858; was a member of the executive and legislative councils of that colony, a justice of the peace for the same, and lieut.-colonel of the Natal carbiniers, a corps of cavalry yeomanry; returned to the colonial office in 1858, and was appointed acting lieut.-governor of St. Vincent in 1859; returned to the colonial office, and was promoted an assistant clerk, 1860; appointed crown agent for the colonies, 1862.

SAUNDERS, F. R.—Appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, 1857; acting assistant agent, Trincomalee, July, 1860, in which appointment he was confirmed, 1862; acting landing-surveyor, Colombo; resumed duties at Trincomalee, 1862; again acting land surveyor, Colombo, 1863; acting assistant government agent at Kaigalle, 1864; promoted to be assistant to government agent at Kandy, August, 1865; acting district judge, &c., Kornegalle, Aug. 1865; district judge, &c., Ratnapoora, continuing to act at Kornegalle, Nov. 1865.

SAUNDERS, ROBERT.—Senior member of the executive council, Montserrat; appointed landing waiter and searcher to the customs' department, St. Kitt's, 1828; removed to Montserrat as collector of the customs in 1840, which office he held until 1855 (when the imperial customs were abolished); he was also the collector of the colonial tariff during that period, he then retired with a pension. Is a non-elective member of the legislative assembly; a justice of the peace and coroner; he has, as senior member of the executive council, administered the government upon four occasions during the absence of the administrator, i. e. from Jan. 28 to Sept. 1860, from May 14 to 30, 1862; from 7 Jan. to 11 April, 1865, and from 26 March, 1866, to

Edinburgh, took academical honours every year, and repeatedly the first; author of "The Practice of the Court of Session," &c.; was an advocate depute, and counsel for the lords of the treasury, and the hon. commissioners of woods and forests in Scotland; chairman of the united industrial or ragged schools; director of the philosophical institution; and captain in the volunteer rifles, Edinburgh; appointed chief justice of the Mauritius in May, 1860; is first judge of the vice-admiralty court, Mauritius, and receives certain fees as such.

SHEPSTONE, T., Sen.—Appointed, Jan. 8, 1835, head-quarter interpreter of the Kafir language at the Cape, and served on the staff of Sir Benjamin D'Urban, governor and commander-in-chief, during the Kafir war of 1835; appointed clerk to the agent general for native tribes on the frontiers of the Cape Colony, 1836; sent to accompany the first military expedition from the Cape to Natal, 1838; created justice of the peace under the Act 6 & 7 Will. IV., to 25th degree south latitude; returned to the Cape Colony in 1839; appointed resident diplomatic agent to the Hlambe tribes, and superintendent of Fingoes at Fort Peddie, on the frontiers of Cape Colony, 1839; appointed diplomatic agent to and over the tribes inhabiting the colony of Natal, 1845; appointed captain-in-chief of the native force in Natal, 1848; appointed to control, revise, and direct the administration of justice among the natives in that colony, according to native law, 1849; proposed and personally carried out a scheme for the taxation of the native population in the colony, which yielded about 10,000 l. , the first year, and now amounts to upwards of 16,000 l. per annum; appointed judicial assessor, Natal, under ordnance No. 1, 1855; was captain of the native police corps, Natal, from its appointment in 1848 to 1851, when it was disbanded; and has been engaged on various special missions and services in the colony not here enumerated; appointed secretary for native affairs, 1856; and member of the executive and legislative councils in that colony, 1856.

SKINNER, T. — Was formerly a major in the army, joined his regiment in Ceylon in 1819; was selected by Sir Edward Barnes to open a section of the road between Colombo and Kandy, 1820; employed from this time till 1837 on the staff, in laying out lines of communication throughout the colony, and in carrying out a trigonometrical survey and sketch of the mountain zone, comprising the Upper Kandyan provinces, and subsequently the greater portions of the island. The result of these surveys is published in two maps, by the late General John Fraser, deputy quarter-master-general of Ceylon; appointed to the charge of the combined departments of surveyor-general and civil engineer, Ceylon, 1838; commissioner of roads, 1841; to which, in 1850, the civil engineer department was added; acting auditor-general, 1859 and again 1865.

STANLEY, Privy Councillor; appointed 1858. — RIGHT HON. EDWARD HENRY SMITH-STANLEY, commonly called LORD STANLEY. — Educated at Rugby, and at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was 1st class in classics, 1848, taking also mathematical honours; elected M.P. for Lynn Regis, Dec. 1848; appointed a deputy-lieut. of Lancashire, 1848; was under-secretary of state for foreign affairs from March to Dec. 1852; appointed secretary of state for the colonies, Feb. 1858, and "Her Majesty's commissioner for the affairs of India," May, 1858; secretary of state for India and president of the council of India, Aug. 1858, to June, 1859.

STEVENTON, WILLIAM F., M.D., F.R.C.S., England.—Public medical attendant, Montserrat; formerly on the medical staff of the army; was for some years surgeon to Mining Company's staffs in South America and in Jamaica; author of, 1. "Fixation of the Globe in operations for Cataract." 2. "An Account of the Flora and Fauna, in Lat. 8°10', N., Long. 80°85', W." 3. "Tropical Infusoria." Appointed, in 1860, public medical attendant of Montserrat. Is a member of the executive council, and as such administered the government from the 16th to the 23rd July, 1863, during the temporary absence of Mr. President Robinson; principal medical officer in the public hospital, Jamaica, 1866.

STONE, G. F.—Attorney-general of Western Australia, Feb. 1860. Is a member of the executive council; was crown solicitor and inspector of weights and measures, 1852 to 1860; and sheriff, 1834.

raw_text: STONE, JOHN.—Stipendiary magistrate for the Port of Spain, and western district of the county of St. George, Trinidad; appointed 1855.

STORKS, THE RIGHT HON. SIR HENRY KNIGHT, G.C.M.G. and G.C.B.—Entered the army as ensign in 1828; promoted lieutenant, 1832; captain, 1835; major, 1840; lieut.-colonel, 1848; colonel, 1854; served in the 61st, 14th, and 38th regiments; served in the Ionian Islands from 1840 to 1842; proceeded to the Cape of Good Hope on special service in 1846; was appointed assistant adjutant-general, and served in the Kaffir war till its termination in 1848; was mentioned in general orders and promoted to the rank of lieut.-colonel for services in the field; has received a medal for the Kaffir war; in 1849 proceeded to the Mauritius as military secretary; was private secretary to the acting governor in part of 1850-51; appointed to the command of Her Majesty's forces at Smyrna, in Jan. 1855; appointed brigadier-general in July, 1855, and removed to Scutari, where he held the command of Her Majesty's forces, and of all the military establishments, from the Bosphorus to Smyrna inclusive, with the rank of major-general; superintended the evacuation of Turkey by the British army at the termination of the Russian war; in 1856 was placed on the list of officers receiving rewards for distinguished services; appointed K.C.B. in Jan. 1857; appointed secretary for military correspondence at the war-office on the 1st of Feb. 1857; appointed in May, 1857, member of the royal commission to inquire into the sanitary condition of the British army; member of the royal commission to inquire into the system of promotion of officers of the higher ranks in the army in 1858; member of a committee to examine and report on the scheme of Sir Charles Trevelyan, for abolishing the purchase of commissions in the army in 1858; was secretary to a secret military committee which sat in 1858, and of which General H.R.H. the Duke of Cambridge was president, to examine into and report on the defences of England; appointed lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands on the 2nd February, 1859, in succession to the Right Honourable William Gladstone, M.P.; received the grand cross of the order of St. Michael and St. George, from Her Majesty the Queen, on the 2nd February, 1859; governor of Malta, Nov. 1864; governor of Jamaica, December, 1865; and president of the royal commission appointed to enquire into the origin and circumstances of the disturbances in that Island; returned to Malta end of 1866; privy counsellor, November 1866.
STRACHEY, WILLIAM.—Appointed by Earl Grey in 1848, to be précis writer to the Colonial office; was formerly in the Bengal Civil Service.

STRAHAN, LIEUTENANT GEO. C.—Entered the royal artillery, Oct. 1857; was appointed, Jan. 1859, aide-de-camp to the Right Hon. W. E. Gladstone, M.P., when lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands; and Feb. 17th, was appointed in the same capacity by Sir Henry Storks, G.C.M.G., K.C.B.
STRINGFELLOW, T.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Fort Beaufort, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1850. Was clerk to the civil commissioner at Albany, 1841.

SURTEES, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1859.)—SIR STEPHENSON VILLIERS SURTEES.—Educated at University College, Oxford, where he graduated S.C.L. 1826, B.C.L. 1831; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1831; was for some time advocate-general at St. Lucia; appointed vice-president of the court of appeal in Mauritius, 1835; and was subsequently chief justice of that island, and judge of the vice-admiralty court there; retired on pension, 1860.

raw_text: SUTTON, H. C. MANNERS.—Educated at Harrow and Magdalen College, Cambridge, where he graduated in 1861; appointed private secretary to the governor of Trinidad, Sept. 6, 1864; private secretary to governor of Victoria, 1866.

SUTTON, CHARLES.—Entered the army, 1846; rose to be a lieutenant without purchase in Dec. 1853; acting barrack-master from 1st Sept. 1860, to 23d June, 1862, when he retired; present at the assaults on Sebastopol, on the 18th June and 8th Sept. 1855. Has a Crimean medal and clasp, and Turkish medal; inspector of prisons, Mauritius, 1862; inspector of police, Bahamas, June 1864.

SUTTON, HON. JOHN H. T. MANNERS.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated M.A. 1835; was under-secretary of state for the home department from Sept. 1841 to July, 1846; is registrar of the faculty court; was returned M.P. for the borough of Cambridge, Sept. 1839, but unseated on petition; sat for that borough from 1841 to 1847; appointed lieut.-governor of New Brunswick, June, 1854, which government he held until Oct. 1861; appointed April, 1864, to be governor of Trinidad; governor of Victoria, April, 1866.

SWAN, J.—Appointed second assistant colonial secretary, Ceylon, Oct. 1855; acted as principal assistant colonial secretary and clerk to the councils from Feb. 16 to June 1, 1862; and again from December, 1863. SYDNEY (AUSTRALIA), 2nd BISHOP OF.—Founded 1847.—RIGHT REV. FREDERIC BARKER, D.D.—Educated at Grantham School and at Jesus College, Cambridge, where he graduated A.B. 1829, M.A. 1835, D.D. 1854; became incumbent of Upton, Cheshire, 1830; of St. Mary's, Edge Hill, Liverpool, 1835; and of Baslow, Derbyshire. 1854; consecrated 1854; is author of "A Commentary on Twenty-six Psalms," &c.; is metropolitan bishop in Australia, subject to the general superintendence of the Archbishop of Canterbury. The diocese originally included the whole of Australia, and bore that name; but in 1847 it was restricted to the central portion of the colony, and called by its present title, while additional bishops were nominated for Newcastle, Adelaide, Melbourne, and Perth; by patent, 19th October, 1854. The bishop is a metropolitan bishop, whose suffragans are the bishops of the above dioceses.

TASMANIA, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1842.—RIGHT REV. FRANCIS RUSSEL NIXON, D.D.—Educated at St. John's College, Oxford, of which he became a fellow; was 3rd class in classics, 1827; and graduated M.A. 1841; D.D. 1842; is author of a "Visitation Sermon, 1840," and of "Lectures on the Church Catechism." The diocese includes the island of Van Diemen's Land (or Tasmania) and its dependencies; resigned 1864.

TASMANIA, 2ND BISHOP OF. Founded 1842.—RIGHT REV. CHARLES HENRY BROMBY, D.D.—Educated at Uppingham and St. John's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A., 1837, his name appearing in both triposes, M.A. 1840, D.D. 1864; consecrated 1864; is author of numerous educational works, including "The Church Student's Manual," "History and Grammar of the English Language," 13 volumes of "Papers for the Schoolmaster," &c.; became incumbent of St. Paul's, Cheltenham, 1843; and principal of the Cheltenham Normal College, 1847, in the erection of which he took a prominent part.

TAUNTON, 1st BARON. (Creat. 1859.) Privy Councillor; appointed 1835 (Great Britain).—HENRY LABOUCHERE.—Educated at Christ Church, Oxford, where he was 1st class in classics, 1820; and graduated M.A. 1820; was lord of the admiralty from 1832 till 1834; vice-president of the board of trade, and master of the mint, from April, 1835 to March, 1839; under-secretary for the colonies from March to Aug. 1839; president of the board of trade and master of the mint, from Aug. 1839 to Sept. 1841; chief secretary for Ireland, from July, 1846 to July, 1847; again president of the board of trade from July, 1847 to Feb. 1852; secretary of state for the colonies from 1855 to Feb. 1858; elected an elder brother of the Trinity House, 1850; was M.P. for St. Michael's from 1826 till 1830, and sat for Taunton from 1830 to 1859; is a deputy-lieut. of Essex.

TAYLOR, E. B. A.—Was private secretary to Mr. Matthew, when governor of the Bahamas; also to Mr. Gregory and Sir A. Bannerman, whilst holding that office, and to Mr. Bayley; is police magistrate, New Providence, Bahamas.

TAYLOR, RICHARD.—Retired half-pay rifle brigade; entered Cape colonial service, Jan. 1849, as finance clerk to chief commissioner of British Kaffraria; appointed resident magistrate of King William's Town, Aug. 1862; also a member of the Kaffrarian board, justice of the peace, paymaster of levies, member of criminal session court, and president of the same, 1861.

TEMPLE, CHRISTOPHER.—Was educated at Shrewsbury, and Magdalen College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. in 1833, and M.A. in 1840; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, Jan. 1836; appointed deputy Queen's advocate, Ceylon, Oct. 1840; district judge, Colombo, June, 1845; acted as one of the judges of the supreme court with scarcely any interruption, from July, 1846 to Oct. 1, 1856, when he was confirmed in the latter office.

raw_text: TEMPLER, P. A.—Appointed writer to the government of Ceylon, Sept. 1863; deputy fiscal, Kandy, 1864; police magistrate at Pantura, Oct. 1865; acting assistant government agent, Trincomalie, Nov. 1865; acting assistant Government agent, Kandy, January, 1866; acting police magistrate, Gampola, April, 1866; assistant collector and landing surveyor, Jaffna, April, 1866; acting assistant government agent, Colombo, July, 1866; acting district judge, Ratnapoora Sept. 1866.

TENNENT, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1845.)—SIR JAMES EMERSON TENNENT, LL.D.—Was educated at Trinity College, Dublin; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1831, but never practised; was elected M.P. for Belfast in 1832 and 1835; defeated at the general election in 1837, but subsequently seated on petition; returned again at the election in 1841, but unseated on petition; regained his seat in 1842, and held it till 1845; sat for Lisburn in 1852; was secretary to the India board from Sept. 1841 to July, 1845; civil secretary to the colonial government of Ceylon from July, 1845, to Dec. 1850; secretary to the poor law board from Feb. to Nov. 1852; appointed secretary to the board of trade, Nov. 1852; has published "Travels in Greece," "A History of Modern Greece," "Letters from the Ægean," works on Ceylon, &c.; was knighted on proceeding to Ceylon in 1845, and created a knight commander of the Greek order of the Saviour in 1842; is a deputy-lieutenant of Sligo, and was also of Fermanagh, but resigned in 1856; is a magistrate for Antrim and Down.

THIBOU, ISAAC BYAM.—Elected member of the assembly (for the city, Antigua), in 1845, re-elected in 1852, 1859, and 1862; appointed a magistrate and visiting justice of the jail in 1858; a member of the executive council and administrative committee and board of audit in 1863; also a commissioner of the treasury; acted as provost marshal for some months in 1863 and 1864.

THIBOU, J. S.—Assistant clerk, royal engineers' department, Antigua, Aug. 1846, to July, 1854; supernumerary clerk, colonial secretary's office, Aug. 1860; clerk, Oct. 1, 1863; registrar of marriages, 1863; chief clerk, colonial secretary's office, 29th July, 1864.

events.position: Appointed second clerk, colonial secretary's office
events.text_span: Appointed second clerk, colonial secretary's office, Antigua, 29th July, 1864.

raw_text: THIBOU, J. T.—Appointed second clerk, colonial secretary's office, Antigua, 29th July, 1864.
THOMAS, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1859.)—SIR JOHN THOMAS.—Formerly speaker of the house of assembly at Barbados; knighted by patent.

THOMSETT, HENRY G.—Entered the royal navy, 1848, and served actively for twenty-one years; was engaged in a combined attack of British and French naval and land force under Commodore Fanshawe on a horde of pirates at Basis, Jeba River, West Africa, 12th December, 1849; during two periods of service on the African station, assisted in the capture of twenty-seven slave vessels in the bight of Benin and coast of Loando; commanded H.M.'s ship 'Princess Charlotte' at Hong Kong, from February, 1858, to Sept. 1861; acting harbour master, 17th March to 26th Nov. 1860, and again 1st March to the 31st Aug. 1861, when he was appointed harbour-master, marine magistrate, and emigration and customs officer, at Hong Kong, July, 1861, and resigned active service in royal navy.

THOMPSON, EDWARD DEAS, C.B.—Emigrated to New South Wales in 1828, having received from Mr. Huskisson the appointment of clerk of the executive and legislative councils, which he continued to hold until Dec. 31, 1836; on Jan. 1, 1837, he was promoted by Lord Glenelg to the office of colonial secretary and registrar of records of New South Wales, and remained in the exercise of that appointment for nearly twenty years, having resigned it on June 5, 1856, on political grounds, when responsible government was first introduced into the colony. He held a seat in the executive council from Jan. 1, 1837 to Oct. 31, 1859; and in the late legislative council from Jan. 1, 1837 till its dissolution in 1856; since that period he has been a member of the present legislative council or upper house of the legislature; he held a seat in the colonial cabinet, but without office, from Oct. 3, 1856 to Sept. 6, 1857; during which time he was vice-president of the executive council, and in that capacity represented the government in the legislative council; he has been a member of the senate of the Sydney University since its establishment in 1850; and was appointed vice-chancellor of that institution in 1862; he has been a member of the board of trustees of the Australian Museum since its incorporation in 1853; and crown trustee since 1856; in 1856 he was appointed a civil companion of the most honourable order of the Bath; appointed chancellor of the Sydney University, 1865.

THOMSON, HENRY BYERLY.—Educated at University College, London, and Jesus College, Cambridge; graduated in honour's, 1844; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, May, 1849; is author of "The Law of War," "The Choice of a Profession," and other works; appointed Queen's advocate of Ceylon, May, 1858; acted as puisne judge of the supreme court from April to Nov. 1861. Allowed private practice. Appointed junior puisne judge of Ceylon, Jan. 1863.

F.—Entered the army as ensign in the 82nd Regt. on 28th Aug. 1840; promoted to lieut. in May, 1843; and to captain in March, 1850; passed through the senior department of the royal military college with two extra certificates in Nov. 1850; retired from the army in June, 1853; and was appointed captain in Hart's militia on 21st July, 1853; proceeded to Victoria in 1856, as private secretary to His Excellency Sir Henry Barkly, K.C.B., the position he now holds.

TORONTO, 1st BISHOP OF. Founded 1839.— RIGHT REV. JOHN STRACHAN, D.D.—Was archdeacon of Upper Canada previous to consecration; is archdeacon of York, rector of Toronto, and principal of King's College, all in Upper Canada; the diocese included the whole of Canada West, but the dioceses of Huron and Ontario have since been taken from it.

TRAPANI, GIO B., LL.D.—Clerk to the council and assistant in secretary's office, Malta; appointed 1859. Has been in the service since 1838.

TRAVERS, FREDERIC T.—Entered the royal artillery, March, 1839; promoted to be captain, 1848; brevet-major, for distinguished services in India, March, 1858; lieut.-colonel, April, 1860; was assistant instructor in surveying and military field-works at the royal military academy, Woolwich, 1847; proceeded to New Zealand in 1849, and commanded the royal artillery in that colony until Nov. 1855; was private secretary to the governor of New Zealand from Oct. 1854 to Sept. 1855; proceeded to the Cape of Good Hope, Nov. 1855, and joined the staff of Governor Sir G. Grey; appointed secretary to the high commissioner, 1856; volunteered for service in India, Aug. 1857, and commanded the royal artillery at the relief of Lucknow and the general action at Cawnpore; received his appointment at the Cape, June, 1858, from which time he performed the duties of military secretary, and secretary to the high commissioner, until Sir G. Grey's appointment to New Zealand; appointed military secretary to Sir P. E. Wodehouse, governor of the Cape, 1861.
TREACHER, J. G.—Colonial surgeon, Labuan; appointed 1848. Member of the legislative council; acting treasurer, 1862.

TRENCH, D. P.—Entered the colonial service on March 14, 1848, as auditor of accounts in the prison department of Jamaica; appointed second commissioner of audit on March 21, 1855; inspector and director of the public hospital and lunatic asylum, Jan. 11, 1859, when that office was first created; and inspector of the revenue department on the creation of that office in March, 1865.

TROTTER, HENRY.—Was private secretary to Major Blackall, when governor of Dominica, from April, 1852, to Nov. 1857, during which period, and up to Feb. 1859, he also held an appointment in the treasury department of that colony; appointed to a clerkship in the commander-in-chief's office, Horse Guards, in Feb. 1859, which he resigned in May, 1861, on being appointed deputy comptroller of the convict establishment at Bermuda; acting comptroller, March, 1862, and as such, had the entire control of that establishment until its dissolution in June, 1863; appointed inspector of post offices in Ceylon, Oct. 1865, after having been qualified for the appointment in the General Post Office.

TYLER, K.H. (Creat. 1833); Knt. BACHEL. 1838.—SIR GEORGE TYLER.—Entered the navy in 1806; in 1811, when engaged in the boats of H.M.S. 'Spartan,' in cutting out French vessels from under the batteries in Quiberon Bay, was severely wounded, and lost his right arm, for which he receives a pension of £200 a year; became a vice-admiral in 1857; was lieut.-governor of St. Vincent's from 1833 to 1840; received the Guelphic order on proceeding to St. Vincent's; was knighted for his conduct as governor during the general emancipation of the negroes; was M.P. for Glamorganshire from Feb. 1851 to April, 1857.

raw_text: VANE, GEORGE.—Served in the royal navy, from June, 1831 to March, 1834, and in the Foreign office from April to August, 1834; appointed to the imperial customs' department at Liverpool, 12 August, 1834; ditto at London, 1839; comptroller of customs at Jaffna, Ceylon, August, 1846; comptroller of customs, Colombo, Sept. 1848; appointed deputy comptroller, on transfer from the imperial service to the civil service, Jan. 1853; principal comptroller of customs, May, 1861; superintendent of the pearl fisheries, 1857 to 1862; acting treasurer, July, 1862 to July, 1864; comptroller of customs, July, 1864; acting treasurer, May, 1865; confirmed, Sept. 1865; is member of the executive and legislative councils, and justice of the peace for the island.

VIVIAN, LIEUT.-COLONEL JOHN L.—Formerly in the army; was lieutenant in the 3rd royal Surrey regiment of militia, from Oct. 1853 to June, 1855; served, during the Russian war, with the Osmanli cavalry under the command of Major-General Smith; is a lieutenant-colonel in the Turkish army; was superintendent of the Carmarthenshire county constabulary; lieutenant in the royal London regiment of militia, from Dec. 1857 to June, 1859; was appointed in June, 1859, superintendent of police for the island of St. Christopher; is a justice of the peace for the colony; ex-officio police magistrate for the rural districts; and a lieutenant-colonel in the militia protective force; chief constable of Swansea, 1863.

WADE, THOMAS FRANCIS.—Was appointed an ensign in the 81st regiment, November 2, 1838; exchanged into the 42nd Highlanders, August 23, 1839; was promoted to be lieutenant, November 16, 1841; exchanged into the 78th Highlanders, November 17, 1841; and sold out June 22, 1847, having served in China, at Chin Kiang Fu, and Nanking in 1842; acted as interpreter in the Canton dialect to the supreme court of Hong Kong in 1843; was appointed assistant Chinese secretary in 1847; vice-consul at Shanghae, 1852; acted as inspector of customs for the Chinese government at Shanghae in 1854; was appointed Chinese secretary at Hong Kong, Nov. 1855; was attached in that capacity to the Earl of Elgin's mission to China in 1857, 1858, and 1859; was appointed Chinese secretary to the mission in China, Aug. 29, 1859; was attached as Chinese secretary to the Earl of Elgin's special embassy to China in 1860; was present at the operations which took place at Pehtang and at the Taku forts on the Peiho; he accompanied the cavalry brigade from the Peiho to Tientsin.
WAKEFORD, H.—Was private secretary to the governor of Western Australia, and clerk of the councils, from 1855 to 1861, when he was appointed police magistrate of Perth.
WALBANK, M. W.—Chief clerk and registrar of the supreme court, and clerk of the central circuit court of Newfoundland; appointed 1863. Is a member of the local bar.

WALCOTT, ROBERT B., M.D.—Educated at Reading; graduated at the University of Paris as B.L. in 1838, and at the University of London as M.B. in 1845, and as M.D. in 1862; appointed one of the public vaccinators of the Island of Barbados, Aug. 1850, and acted as such until the office was abolished in Dec. 1853; appointed one of the district medical attendants to the police force, July 1854; as acting coroner for the district of St. Joseph and St. Andrew, of said island, May, 1855; and as full coroner for the same district, July 1855, on the death of the former coroner; was one of the commissioners of Census, 1861; is medical lecturer, &c., at Codrington College, and medical attendant of poor at St. Joseph's parish; holds commissions as surgeon to the 11th regiment of Barbados Militia, 1854, and as assistant surgeon to the regiment of Barbados Yeomanry Cavalry, 1860.

raw_text: WALKER, C. P.—Assistant government agent for eastern province, Ceylon, 1837; assistant government agent for the southern province, Hambantotte, 1838; district judge, Colombo, 1843; assistant agent, Ratnapoora, 1846; assistant agent for northern province, and district-judge, Manaar, 1847; district judge, &c., of Galle, 1854.

WALKER, JAMES, C.B. (Creat. 1862.)—Was for several years a clerk on the establishment of the colonial office, Downing Street; appointed registrar and clerk of the courts in Honduras, 1837; treasurer of Trinidad, 1839; and acted as colonial secretary of that island from June, 1839, to Sept. 1840; accompanied Sir Henry Macleod as secretary on a special mission to British Guiana, 1840-41; was appointed colonial secretary of Barbados in 1842; administrator of the government of Grenada, 1856; of the government of St. Vincent, 1857; of the government of Barbados and the Windward Islands, 1859; and lieut.-governor of Trinidad, 1860; appointed C.B. 1860; and governor-in-chief of the Windward Islands, 1861.

WALKER, WILLIAM.—Government secretary, and secretary to the court of policy, British Guiana, 1847; holds the commission of lieut.-governor since 1847; has administered the government four times, namely, from May, 1848 to Feb. 1849, from May, 1853 to March, 1854, from July, 1857 to May, 1858, and from May, 1861 to Jan. 1862. Entered the service, Jan. 1836, as private secretary to lieut.-governor Colebrooke in Bahamas; was also auditor of the public bank, and secretary to the board of public instruction; was member of the house of assembly for the district of Exuma; rejoined Sir William Colebrooke at Antigua, Dec. 1837; was first inspector of prisons, and stipendiary magistrate, of Antigua and Barbuda, and subsequently provost-marshal; in 1860 represented the colony as delegate at the International Statistical Congress in London; in August, 1862, was sent to Washington on a special mission relative to the introduction of liberated slaves from the United States to the colony and received the thanks of the court for the satisfactory manner in which that duty was performed; retired on pension, 1866.
WALLACE, J.—Clerk in the receiver-general's office, Nova Scotia.

East India Company's service, 1831; assistant adjutant-general in India, 1839; from 1839 to 1853 filled the posts of deputy adjutant-general of the army, deputy judge-advocate general, assistant quarter-master, general of a division; appointed commissioner of police for the province of South Australia, 1853.

WATTLEY, JOS. K.—Clerk of assembly, Dec. 1852; puisne judge, Nov. 1856; acting chief justice, Oct. and Nov. 1861; again, Jan. 1862; again, Oct. 1862; acting solicitor-general, Nevis, Oct. 1861; was formerly registrar in admiralty, St. Kitt's; Queen's counsel, May, 1861; is secretary to board of health, and to water commission. Has held various honorary appointments; mayor of Basse-terre, Feb. 1862; acting chief justice and judge of complaint court from Aug. 1863. Is a barrister.

WATTS, FREDERICK.—In 1836 was placed in the commission of peace for Barbados, for the purpose of assisting the stipendiary magistrates under the Emancipation Act to appraise the unexpired services of the apprenticed labourers; acted as general justice of the peace until 1839, when he was appointed acting police magistrate of St. George, coroner of St. Philip from 1839 to 1841; police magistrate of St. Philip, 1841; in 1842 was elected member of assembly for St. Philip; retired in consequence of an act passed which prohibited his representing the constituency he registered; in 1861 sat as acting judge of the assistant court of appeal; is major in the Barbados yeomanry cavalry.

WEBB, J. H.—Was appointed inspector of public schools in the Bahamas in 1847; is acting secretary to the board of education, and a justice of the peace for the colony; appointed, 1865, registrar of records of Courts of Ordinary and Chancery in the Bahamas.

WEBB, NATHANIEL.—Entered the colonial service in 1838 as clerk of the inferior court, Bahamas; appointed assistant private secretary to Major-General Sir Francis Cockburn, governor of the Bahamas, in 1839, and continued to hold that office until 1843; promoted to be private secretary, 1844; appointed junior clerk to Legislative Assembly, 1840; served in New Providence Militia till that corps was disbanded; appointed superintendent of New Providence Asylum, 1846; member of the legislative assembly, 1863.

Appointed 1858.—CHARLES JOHN ABRAHAM.—Formerly archdeacon of Waitemata (1852); educated at King's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1837, M.A. 1840; was for some years assistant master at Eton.

WHYTE, JOHN CHARLES.—Graduate of Trinity College, Dublin, where he took honours in classics; called to the bar of Ireland, March, 1847; appointed second police magistrate of Hong Kong, August, 1862; acted as judge of the court of summary jurisdiction in that colony from April, 1863, to October, 1864, again from April, 1865, to the present time; appointed a provisional member of the legislative council in June, 1865.

WILLIAMS OF KARS, 1st BART. (Utd. Kgdm.) Creat. 1856; K.C.B. (Military) 1856; C.B. (Civil) 1852.—SIR WILLIAM FENWICK WILLIAMS, educated at the royal military academy at Woolwich; entered the artillery as 2nd lieut. 1825, and became capt. in 1840; employed in Turkey from 1840 to 1843 as British commissioner at the conferences preceding the treaty signed at Erzeroum in 1847; was British commissioner for the settlement of the Turko-Persian boundary, June, 1848; received the brevet of colonel in that year for his military and diplomatic services; in Aug. 1854, while the British army was at Varna, he was appointed British commissioner with the Turkish forces, and received the local rank of brigadier-general in Dec. 1855; was granted a pension of 10000L. a-year for life, a baronetcy, the order of the Bath, the Turkish order of the Medjidie of the 1st class, the freedom of the city of London and a sword, the honorary degree of D.C.L. from Oxford, &c., for his gallant defence of Kars in 1855; appointed commandant at Woolwich, June, 1856; M.P. for Calne, July, 1856; governor of Nova Scotia, 1865.

Wynyard, Oct. 1861, then acting governor of the Cape; exchanged to 96th regt. in 1864; garrison adjutant, King William's Town, Br. Kaffraria, June, 1864 to Aug. 1864; district adjutant of Natal, Sept. 1864; private sec. to Lieut.-governor Maclean, C.B., 31st Dec. 1864; and clerk to the executive council, 31st Dec. 1864.
