WODEHOUSE, SIR PHILIP E., K.C.B. (Creat. 1862.)—Entered the Ceylon civil service as a writer, May, 1828; promoted to be assistant colonial secretary and clerk of the executive and legislative councils, Oct. 1833; district judge of Kandy, 1840; government agent for the western province, 1843; was appointed superintendent of Honduras, 1851; in Feb. 1854, governor of British Guiana; was employed in 1858 on a special mission to the government of Venezuela; and in 1861 was appointed governor of the Cape of Good Hope; is also high commissioner in South Africa.

raw_text: WOLFENDEN, RICHARD.—Appointed Oct. 26, 1863; superintendent of government printing office, British Columbia, a non-commissioned officer of the corps of the Royal engineers; served five years as a clerk in the lands and works department; was superintendent of the printing department under Col. Moody.

WOLFF, SIR HENRY DRUMMOND, K.C.M.G.—Entered the foreign office in 1846; was attached, 1852, to Her Majesty's legation at Florence, where he served for a short time as acting chargé d'affaires; was attached, 1856, to the Earl of Westmoreland's special mission to Brussels to congratulate the king on the twenty-fifth anniversary of his accession, and with Lord Westmoreland accompanied the king on part of his progress through the kingdom; was appointed by royal decree a knight of the order of Leopold of Belgium, but was not allowed by the queen to accept the decoration, it not being conferred for military services; was for some time an officer in the 1st or western regiment of Norfolk militia; was appointed in Feb. 1858, assistant private secretary to the Earl of Malmesbury, secretary of state for foreign affairs; in Nov., private secretary to the Right Hon. Sir Edward Bulwer Lytton, secretary of state for the colonies; was named by the Queen in April, 1859, C.M.G., and appointed to the honorary office of king of arms of St. Michael and St. George; was appointed in June, 1859, public secretary to the lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands; in the same year a member of a commission presided over by the lord high commissioner, to inquire into the state of public departments; in 1860, was named member of a commission to inquire and report on the state of the Ionian finances; was delegated to represent the Ionian government at the international statistical congress held in London in 1860; was appointed, 1861, by the Ionian government, vice-president of a commission to organize, in 1862, an exhibition of the works of Ionian art and industry, and in the same year member of a commission to inquire into and prepare a project of law for regulating the system of public instruction in the Ionian Islands; was commissioner for the Ionian Islands at the International Exhibition of 1862; and was created K.C.M.G.

WOODFORD, G.C.B. (Creat. 1852); G.C.M.G. 1832.—SIR ALEXANDER GEORGE WOODFORD.—Served in Holland and Denmark; was aide-de-camp to Lord Forbes on the staff in Sicily in 1809, and subsequently with the Coldstream guards in Portugal and Spain; was at Waterloo in command of that regiment, at the capture of Paris, and at Cambrai during the three years' occupation of France; became a general in 1854, and colonel of the 40th foot in 1842; received a medal and two clasps for his services at Salamanca, Vittoria, and Nive; was lieut.-governor of Malta in 1825; was subsequently commander of the forces in the Ionian Islands; was governor of Gibraltar from 1835 to 1842; appointed lieut.-governor of Chelsea Hospital, Nov. 1856; received the orders of Maria Theresa and St. George, 4th class, for his services at Waterloo; was aide-de-camp to George IV.
WOODGATE, A.—Deputy postmaster, Nova Scotia.

WOODS, WILLIAM WHITFIELD.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; entered the public service in 1828 as a clerk in the education office, Ireland; in 1836 was appointed to the office of the ecclesiastical commissioners, Ireland; and in 1844 to the office of public works, Ireland, and superintended the relief department under that board during the famine of 1845-46; and, subsequently, the land improvement department in 1856; on the abolition of the office he was transferred to the treasury department, London; and in 1859 was appointed assistant librarian to the colonial office.
WOODS, I. TYNDAL L.—Employed in the colonial office from 1860 to 1864, when he was appointed to be colonial secretary at Labuan.

raw_text: YOUNG, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1847).—SIR HENRY EDWARD FOX YOUNG.—Was entered as a student of the Inner Temple, but has been uninterruptedly employed in the colonial civil service; was treasurer, secretary, puisne judge, and member of council at St. Lucia (1834); government secretary and member of the court of policy in British Guiana from 1835 to 1847; knighted on his return to England; appointed lieut.-governor of the eastern districts of the Cape of Good Hope, Feb. 1847; lieut.-governor of South Australia, June, 1847; governor-general and commander-in-chief of Tasmania, Sept. 1854.

YOUNG, 2nd BART. (United Kingdom, Creat. 1821); Privy Councillor (Great Britain and Ireland), 1852; G.C.M.G. 1855.—RIGHT HON. SIR JOHN YOUNG, son of the 1st baronet, born 1807; succeeded his father in 1848.—Was educated at Corpus Christi College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1829; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1834; was a lord of the treasury from Sept. 1841 to May, 1844; secretary to the treasury from May, 1844 to July, 1846; chief secretary to the lord-lieut. of Ireland from Dec. 1852 to March, 1855; was lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands from March, 1855 to Feb. 1859; created a G.C.M.G. on receiving that appointment; created K.C.B. on retiring from the office; appointed governor-general and commander-in-chief of New South Wales, 1860. Was M.P. for Cavan from 1831 to 1855.

YOUNG, J. D.—Appointed district road officer at Batticaloa, Sept. 15, 1851; from Jan. to June, 1852, employed as superintending officer on the Matelle road; returned to Batticaloa, June, 1852, and served there till May, 1855; assistant civil engineer at Galle, and confirmed in that appointment in 1858; especially employed in 1859 in constructing dam, &c., to the canal at Ovrebokhe.

raw_text: YOUNG, CHARLES, LL.D. of Newton University.—Called to the bar of Nova Scotia, 1837; appointed to legislative council, Prince Edward's Island, 1840, and continued therein till 1862; queen's counsel, 1847; member of the executive council and attorney-general, 1851; surrogate and judge of probate, 1852; president of legislative council, 1853; again attorney-general, 1858; administered the government after the departure of Sir Dominick Daly, and until the arrival of lieut-governor Dundas, 1859.

YOUNG, JAMES JUDSON.—Employed from Feb. 1856 to March 1857 as an extra clerk at Somerset House, in the department of the accountant-general of the navy; appointed Jan. 1860, clerk in the office of the colonial secretary, British Columbia; also acted as private secretary to Sir J. Douglas, from Sept. 1861, to date of his retirement in 1864.

raw_text: YOUNG, WILLIAM A. G.—Was for some years in the royal navy, and served as naval secretary to several flag-officers upon different foreign stations; on the last occasion was secretary to the commodore, the Hon. F. J. Pelham, of the Baltic fleet from Feb. 1855 to June, 1856; appointed secretary to the North American boundary commission, 1856; and colonial secretary and auditor of the colony of Vancouver's Island, March, 1859.
YOUNG, WILLIAM.—Chief justice of Nova Scotia.

YULE, J. N.—Was appointed resident at Swan River, Western Australia, 1838; acting protector of natives, 1843; acting clerk of the council, 1849; acting colonial secretary, 1850; and appointed police magistrate at Perth, 1852. Retired on a pension, 1862.

events.position: Appointed to be superintendent of the civil hospital

raw_text: YULE, JOHN ALEX.—Appointed to be superintendent of the civil hospital, Hong Kong, June, 1864.
ZAMMIT, ANTONIO, LL.D.—Appointed magistrate of judicial police, Malta, Jan. 1829. Is a supplementary judge of the criminal court.

ZIMELLI, HECTOR.—Served as clerk in chief secretary's office, Malta, 1834; was clerk in audit office, and superintendent of public works department, and appointed superintendent of police in that island, Jan. 1855. LONDON: PRINTED BY HARRISON AND SONS, ST. MARTIN'S LANE.
